[discussion] Discussion The present study constitutes an investigation of menstrual cycle-associated alterations in microvascular and structural characteristics within the macular and peripapillary regions. The investigation was conducted through a comprehensive analysis of OCT parameters such as GCIPL, CMT and CT, as well as OCTA-assessed SCP, DCP, and CC layers. The study analysis demonstrated a significant decrease in macular CT from the follicular to the mid-luteal phase of the menstrual cycle. Peripapillary CT measurements also exhibited a significant reduction in the temporal and inferior quadrants. Furthermore, detailed OCTA analysis revealed significant variations in VD. In the macula, a significant increase was observed exclusively within the inferior quadrant of the CC layer. VD measurements within the peripapillary region revealed significant differences between menstrual cycle phases, particularly in the superior quadrant of the SCP and the inferior quadrant of the DCP. It is noteworthy that significant differences were observed in the CC VD between the different menstrual cycle phases within the peripapillary region, specifically in the temporal and inferior quadrants. Whilst a recent study has examined menstrual cycle-related changes in the microvasculature of the macula and the peripapillary region, the present study is the first to investigate layer-segmented peripapillary vascular changes across menstrual phases using OCTA [ 9 ]. The vasodilatory influence of estrogen on ocular blood flow is exerted via both endothelium-dependent and independent mechanisms [ 3 ]. Elevated estrogen levels have been observed to stimulate the synthesis of nitric oxide while concurrently inhibiting the production of endothelin-1. These counterbalancing effects collectively promote vasodilation and reduce vascular resistance [ 10 ]. In contrast, progesterone functions as an estrogen antagonist, inducing vasoconstriction [ 11 ]. The majority of studies examining the effects of hormones on ocular microvasculature have concentrated on the period of pregnancy. Previous research into the subject of pregnancy has indicated that the observed increase in vascular density within the retina and the optic disc during pregnancy is likely attributable to physiological adaptations necessitated by the gravid state [ 12 ]. Hormonal fluctuations constitute a potential mechanism underlying these ocular vascular changes. Given the established influence of sex hormones on various physiological and pathological processes within ocular tissues, it has been postulated that these hormones may contribute to the pathogenesis of certain ocular diseases [ 13 , 14 ]. A small number of studies have been conducted into the impact of the menstrual cycle on ocular anatomy and vascularization, as evidenced in the literature [ 4 , 5 , 8 ]. Guo et al. demonstrated a significant decrease in VD within the nasal and inferior quadrants of the DCP during the ovulatory phase compared to the early follicular and luteal phases. The authors hypothesise that hormone fluctuations across the menstrual cycle may be responsible for the significant changes in DCP microvasculature observed during the ovulatory phase. This finding contrasts with the stability of DCP VD observed during the early follicular and luteal phases [ 8 ]. In contrast to the aforementioned study, our findings revealed no statistically significant difference in SCP and DCP in the macular area. Nevertheless, a statistically significant alteration in VD was observed in the inferior quadrant of the CC layer. This discrepancy may be attributed to the fact that the choroidal circulation exhibits a comparatively limited capacity for autoregulation in comparison to the retina. The choroid is more directly influenced by systemic conditions and exhibits reduced capacity to maintain constant blood flow during fluctuations in ocular perfusion pressure [ 15 ]. Moreover, retinal arterioles function as the primary resistance vessels within the retinal vasculature, exerting a considerable influence on retinal hemodynamics through the modulation of their luminal diameter. The mechanism of vascular autoregulation in retinal vessels represents a significant area of investigation. Retinal arterioles modulate capillary perfusion by dynamically adjusting their luminal diameter through changes in vascular smooth muscle tone [ 16 ]. Furthermore, the utilisation of different OCTA devices may have contributed to the observed variability in the findings. Device-specific differences in OCTA technology, including segmentation algorithms, can influence the measurement and visualisation of vascular changes and may contribute to the observed discrepancies in VD across studies. In alignment with the established characteristics of the ovulation phase, characterized by elevated estrogen and reduced progesterone levels, the peripapillary region exhibited a notable increase in VD measurements between menstrual cycle phases. This was particularly evident in the superior quadrant of the SCP, as well as in the inferior quadrant of the DCP, and also in the temporal and inferior quadrants of the CC. Post-hoc analysis revealed a pronounced increase in VD during the ovulatory phase relative to both the follicular and mid-luteal phases. Furthermore, our study evaluated alterations in the choroid through OCT. The study demonstrated a significant reduction in macular CT from the follicular to the mid-luteal phase of the menstrual cycle. Also, peripapillary CT measurements demonstrated a significant reduction in the temporal and inferior quadrants. The structural changes observed in the choroid across the menstrual cycle were found to be parallel with the microvascular alterations in the CC. The present findings are consistent with those of recent studies exploring hormonal influences on posterior ocular structures. A recent study reported reduced DCP VD in women using combined oral contraceptives (COCs), suggesting a hormone-mediated modulation of retinal microvasculature detectable by OCTA. In a similar vein, Icoz et al. observed a significant decrease in choroidal vascular index values in individuals who used COC, in comparison to those who did not use COC. This observation is indicative of alterations in the luminal component of the choroid. These findings are complementary to our observation of increased VD during the ovulatory phase, suggesting that the physiological estrogen surge may enhance microvascular perfusion, whereas hormonally suppressed states such as COC use may attenuate it, thereby highlighting the hormonal sensitivity of retinal and choroidal microcirculation [ 17 , 18 ]. The existing literature has demonstrated alterations in CT throughout the menstrual cycle. Ulaş et al. employed spectral-domain OCT to demonstrate a significant reduction in CT during the mid-luteal phase in comparison to earlier stages of the menstrual cycle in a cohort of healthy women. Similarly, Asıkgarip et al. reported a consistent reduction in CT across all regions during the mid-luteal phase. Proposed mechanisms for these changes in CT include vasoconstriction mediated by alpha-adrenergic and/or renin activity, as well as hormonal fluctuations that are characteristic of the mid-luteal phase [ 19 , 20 ]. Although we discussed multiple mechanisms through which estrogen exerts its impact on retinal microvascular structure, there remains a degree of inconsistency in how this effect is represented within the scientific literature. Leung et al. conducted a study to explore the relationship between hormone replacement therapy and retinal arteriolar diameter in an older female population. Contrary to the prevailing hypothesis, their findings indicated no significant vasodilatory effect of hormone replacement therapy on retinal arterioles. In contrast, Wong et al. reported a cross-sectional association between estrogen replacement therapy and narrower retinal vessel diameters in a population-based study, independent of blood pressure and other vascular factors. Both studies employed a standardized methodology, measuring retinal arteriolar and venular diameters within a one-disc diameter radius of the optic disc using computer-assisted image analysis of digitized color fundus photographs [ 21 , 22 ]. Two decades ago, Karadeniz et al. investigated the changes in hemodynamics observed in the ophthalmic, central retinal, and posterior ciliary arteries across the menstrual cycle. Doppler ultrasonography was performed on 23 healthy women across six distinct menstrual cycle phases. No significant variations in pulsatility index, resistance index, or peak systolic velocity were observed among the arterial segments. The authors proposed that these findings indicate that the complex interplay of hormonal influences on systemic hemodynamics, including heart rate, blood pressure, and blood volume, may offset any potential menstrual cycle-related alterations in ocular blood flow [ 7 ]. Recent advancements in ophthalmic technology have shifted the paradigm for assessing intraocular perfusion away from traditional Doppler and pulsatility examinations towards the utilization of OCT and OCTA. In alignment with this technological shift, our study comprehensively employed OCT and OCTA imaging for data acquisition. In a previous OCTA study, Savastano et al. observed a significantly higher VD measurements of CC in patients with hypothalamic amenorrhea compared to controls, both in the whole area and in the fovea area. The authors attributed this finding to potential endothelial dysfunction secondary to estrogen deficiency, characterized by reduced nitric oxide production, elevated oxidative stress, and proinflammatory cytokine levels, which ultimately lead to abnormal vascular remodeling and microvascular constriction. Based on these findings, they hypothesized that the observed increase in VD might reflect compensatory microvascular changes in response to these underlying pathophysiological processes [ 23 ]. Consistent with the findings of Savastano et al., a recent study by Çetinkaya et al. utilized swept-source OCTA to investigate retinal and choroidal microvascular changes in postmenopausal women. The study reported a reduction in VD of CC in postmenopausal participants compared to age-matched controls with ongoing menstruation. Notably, a negative correlation between VD and menopause duration suggested that prolonged hypoestrogenism adversely impacts the microvascular structure of this layer [ 24 ]. A primary limitation of this study was the absence of serum sex hormone level measurements. Without direct quantification of estradiol and progesterone levels, we were unable to correlate retinal and choroidal microvascular changes with precise hormonal fluctuations during the menstrual cycle. Although menstrual phases were carefully timed using well-established cycle days (day 3, 14, and 21) and participants were required to prospectively document the onset of menstruation to confirm regular ovulatory cycles, individual variation in hormonal profiles may still exist. This limitation introduces the potential for misclassification of cycle phases, which could affect the accuracy of interpreting phase-specific vascular changes. The absence of biochemical validation may reduce the granularity of phase assignment. As such, the observed changes in VD and CT should be interpreted with caution, and future studies incorporating serum hormone analysis would offer a more robust framework for confirming the hormonal basis of the vascular alterations observed with OCTA. Additionally, the relatively small sample size may limit the generalizability of the findings and reduce statistical power to detect more subtle microvascular changes across menstrual phases. In conclusion, the present study indicates that the hormonal fluctuations associated with the menstrual cycle exert a substantial impact on the architecture and vasculature of the retina and choroid. A crucial finding is the identification of structural alterations in the choroid across menstrual phases, which may indicate a potential association with concurrent microvascular changes in the CC. Notable variations were observed in both peripapillary CT and CC microvasculature in the inferior and temporal regions. It is therefore recommended that clinicians take the menstrual cycle phase into account when interpreting OCTA data in women of reproductive age, to ensure optimal diagnostic accuracy and treatment planning.

[conclusion] Conclusion Hormonal fluctuations throughout the menstrual cycle appear to influence both retinal and choroidal microvasculature, particularly in the choriocapillaris and peripapillary regions. These findings underscore the importance of considering menstrual phase when interpreting OCTA measurements in women of reproductive age, to improve diagnostic accuracy and consistency in clinical and research settings.

retrieved_papers.title: Swept-Source OCT Angiography-Derived Regional Normative Data of Peripapillary Vessel Density in Healthy Populations.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] The purpose of this study was to investigate the distribution of peripapillary vessel density (VD) across different regions of retinal and choroidal layers and analyze its influencing factors after axial length (AL) correction.
This study included 337 eyes without significant fundus abnormalities or systemic conditions affecting blood flow. Swept-source optical coherence tomography angiography (SS-OCTA) was utilized to measure peripapillary VD and associated ocular parameters.
VD in all regions of the choroidal layer decreased significantly with increasing AL among AL groups. In non-highly myopic eyes, VD followed the pattern temporal (T) > superior nasal (SN) > inferior nasal (IN) in the inner retina and superficial vascular complex (SVC), whereas in the deep vascular complex (DVC), it was inferior (I) > superior (S). In the choroid, VD ranked as nasal (N) > T. Highly myopic eyes showed higher temporal but lower nasal VD in the inner retina and SVC, whereas DVC exhibited the opposite trend. Choroidal VD decreased significantly across all regions, most prominently in the T hemisphere. Multivariable analysis identified age, signal strength, and retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) thickness as key determinants of inner retinal VD, whereas AL, signal strength, vertical cup-to-disc ratio (C/D), Bruch's membrane opening (BMO), and optic disc-fovea distance (DFD) significantly influenced choroidal VD.
Inner retinal VD followed T > SN > IN, primarily contributed by the SVC, whereas choroidal VD followed N > T. With increasing AL, choroidal VD declined across all regions, most prominently in the T hemisphere, whereas the inner retinal VD trends varied by region and layer.
The findings of this study may contribute to the early warning of the disease and provide an important theoretical basis for the study of myopia-related microcirculatory alteration mechanisms.

[Introduction] Introduction Blood flow impairment to the optic nerve head (ONH) is one of the major causes of blindness or impaired vision, especially in blinding diseases such as glaucoma and ischemic optic neuropathy (ION). 1 Early detection of ONH microcirculatory abnormalities is clinically crucial for disease monitoring and intervention. However, traditional assessment methods have significant limitations: fundus photograph-based observation of optic disc color is highly subjective and unquantifiable; color Doppler ultrasound can measure blood flow velocity but is difficult to analyze the microvascular network; and fluorescein angiography, although it can dynamically observe blood flow, is invasive and potentially allergenic, limiting its clinical application. With advancements in tomographic imaging techniques, particularly optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA), stratified, noninvasive imaging enables the quantitative assessment of microvascular density, facilitating a deeper understanding of the relationship between various diseases and optic disc vessel density (VD). It has been shown that a reduction in VD is strongly associated with glaucoma severity, with retinal microvascular changes potentially preceding detectable visual field defects. 2 , 3 OCTA pinpoints areas of perfusion loss in ION, and its distribution is highly coincident with areas of retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) loss and visual field loss. 4 Notably, OCTA has also revealed significant structural remodeling and microcirculatory abnormalities in the ONH of highly myopic eyes, even in the absence of pathological changes. 5 Tang et al. 6 demonstrated that peripapillary microcirculatory parameters were more predictive of myopic progression than the macula, suggesting the potential importance of VD monitoring in the ONH region in myopia. However, large-scale baseline studies on peripapillary VD in healthy populations remain scarce, particularly regarding subregional analysis, leading to a lack of reliable reference standards for disease localization. Existing studies have predominantly focused on the retinal layer, and it has been well established that reduced choroidal perfusion is associated with myopia progression. 7 , 8 However, most of these studies have concentrated primarily on the macular region. Tang et al. 9 used ultra-widefield OCTA to observe the whole choroidal blood flow in patients with myopia and divided it into nine regions for their study, demonstrating for the first time that choroidal vessels in the optic disc grid were significantly thinner in the early stages of myopia. However, choroidal perfusion studies have largely overlooked peripapillary choroidal blood flow characteristics around the ONH, and research on ONH VD across different regions remains limited. The traditional quadrant partitioning method (superior/inferior/nasal/temporal) does not accurately correspond to capillary anatomic distribution and may obscure early pathological changes. Several factors influence OCTA-based VD measurements, including gender, age, axial length (AL), signal intensity, systemic diseases, and so on. 10 Understanding these influencing factors is essential for accurately interpreting OCTA imaging results and ensuring comparability across individuals. However, most prior studies focused primarily on the retinal layer, with inadequate control for confounding variables, such as the lack of AL correction. The primary aim of this study was to investigate the regional distribution of VD across different layers in a population with normal fundus structures. Additionally, we collected a broad range of ocular and physiological parameters to evaluate their influence on VD within different layers.

[introduction] Introduction Blood flow impairment to the optic nerve head (ONH) is one of the major causes of blindness or impaired vision, especially in blinding diseases such as glaucoma and ischemic optic neuropathy (ION). 1 Early detection of ONH microcirculatory abnormalities is clinically crucial for disease monitoring and intervention. However, traditional assessment methods have significant limitations: fundus photograph-based observation of optic disc color is highly subjective and unquantifiable; color Doppler ultrasound can measure blood flow velocity but is difficult to analyze the microvascular network; and fluorescein angiography, although it can dynamically observe blood flow, is invasive and potentially allergenic, limiting its clinical application. With advancements in tomographic imaging techniques, particularly optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA), stratified, noninvasive imaging enables the quantitative assessment of microvascular density, facilitating a deeper understanding of the relationship between various diseases and optic disc vessel density (VD). It has been shown that a reduction in VD is strongly associated with glaucoma severity, with retinal microvascular changes potentially preceding detectable visual field defects. 2 , 3 OCTA pinpoints areas of perfusion loss in ION, and its distribution is highly coincident with areas of retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) loss and visual field loss. 4 Notably, OCTA has also revealed significant structural remodeling and microcirculatory abnormalities in the ONH of highly myopic eyes, even in the absence of pathological changes. 5 Tang et al. 6 demonstrated that peripapillary microcirculatory parameters were more predictive of myopic progression than the macula, suggesting the potential importance of VD monitoring in the ONH region in myopia. However, large-scale baseline studies on peripapillary VD in healthy populations remain scarce, particularly regarding subregional analysis, leading to a lack of reliable reference standards for disease localization. Existing studies have predominantly focused on the retinal layer, and it has been well established that reduced choroidal perfusion is associated with myopia progression. 7 , 8 However, most of these studies have concentrated primarily on the macular region. Tang et al. 9 used ultra-widefield OCTA to observe the whole choroidal blood flow in patients with myopia and divided it into nine regions for their study, demonstrating for the first time that choroidal vessels in the optic disc grid were significantly thinner in the early stages of myopia. However, choroidal perfusion studies have largely overlooked peripapillary choroidal blood flow characteristics around the ONH, and research on ONH VD across different regions remains limited. The traditional quadrant partitioning method (superior/inferior/nasal/temporal) does not accurately correspond to capillary anatomic distribution and may obscure early pathological changes. Several factors influence OCTA-based VD measurements, including gender, age, axial length (AL), signal intensity, systemic diseases, and so on. 10 Understanding these influencing factors is essential for accurately interpreting OCTA imaging results and ensuring comparability across individuals. However, most prior studies focused primarily on the retinal layer, with inadequate control for confounding variables, such as the lack of AL correction. The primary aim of this study was to investigate the regional distribution of VD across different layers in a population with normal fundus structures. Additionally, we collected a broad range of ocular and physiological parameters to evaluate their influence on VD within different layers.

[methods] Methods Ethics This cross-sectional study enrolled patients attending the Second Affiliated Hospital of Xi'an Jiaotong University. Details of this study were reviewed and approved by the Ethics Committee of the Second Affiliated Hospital of Xi'an Jiaotong University (pid: 248264) and were performed in adherence to the tenets of the Declaration of Helsinki. Participants A total of 337 eyes were included in our study. All subjects underwent swept-source optical coherence tomography angiography (SS-OCTA, VG200; SVision Imaging, Ltd., Luoyang, China) examinations and fundus photography (Zeiss, Clarus 500) first to evaluate whether their retinal structures were normal. Patients who met the following criteria were enrolled in the study: (1) subjects with no obvious abnormalities in fundus structures; (2) subjects capable of cooperating with all required examinations; and (3) subjects with clear refractive media and image signal quality greater than 6. Exclusion criteria included previous ocular trauma, poor general health, and diseases affecting the fundus structure and blood flow, such as hypertension, diabetes mellitus, coronary artery disease, heart failure, cerebrovascular accidents, and others. Image Acquirement All participants underwent SS-OCTA examinations with a scanning speed of 200,000 A-scans per second. Each participant underwent both ONH angiography and biological measurement modes. ONH angiography covers a 6 × 6 mm 2 area centered on the ONH, including both vascular and structural information. It provides vessel information for the inner retina layer, superficial vascular complex (SVC), deep vascular complex (DVC), and choroidal layer. The inner retinal layer comprises the SVC and DVC. The SVC is defined as the region extending from the internal limiting membrane (ILM) to the inner third of the inner plexiform layer (IPL), encompassing both the radial peripapillary capillary plexus (RPCP) and the superficial vascular plexus (SVP). The DVC spans from the lower third of the IPL to the outer plexiform layer (OPL), and includes the intermediate vascular plexus (IVP) and the deep vascular plexus (DVP). The choroidal layer is delineated from Bruch's membrane to the outer boundary of the choroid, covering both the choriocapillaris and large choroidal vessels. Additionally, RNFL thickness, average cup-disc ratio (C/D), vertical C/D, Bruch's membrane opening (BMO), optic disc-fovea distance (DFD), and optic disc-fovea angle (DFA) were measured. In biological measurement mode, we can collect AL, central corneal thickness (CCT), aqueous depth (AQD), anterior chamber depth (ACD), lens thickness (LT), and choroidal thickness (CT). Image Processing VDs for the different layers were defined as the percentage of the area occupied by the vessels and was calculated automatically using the OCTA built-in software within a 2 to 4 mm circle centered on the optic disk. BMO, DFD, and DFA were measured manually using B-scan and en face images ( Figs. 1 E, 1 F) It is worth noting that all the indices from the ONH angiography mode should be corrected for AL using Bennett's formula, including RNFL thickness, BMO, and DFD. 11 , 12 VD was corrected by adjusting the diameters of each circle based on the formula. Figures 1 A to 1 D presents detailed VD in 5 regions of the inner retinal and choroidal layers before and after correction of the AL. These regions include superior temporal (ST), inferior temporal (IT), inferior nasal (IN), superior nasal (SN), and disc (D). Figure 1. Vessel density of inner retinal layer ( A , B ) and choroidal layer ( C, D ) before ( A , C ) and after correction ( B , D ) in five regions, including the superior temporal (ST), inferior temporal (IT), inferior nasal (IN), superior nasal (SN), and disc. BMO ( E ), DFD, and DFA ( F ) manual measurements. Statistical Analysis The statistical analyses were performed using SPSS Statistics 27.0 (IBM Corp.). The normality of the data was assessed using the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test. Data were expressed as means ± standard deviations (SDs) for normally distributed variables and as median (Q1 and Q3) for non-normally distributed variables. Differences in continuous variables were compared using the 1-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) test for normally distributed data and Kruskal-Wallis test for non-normally distributed data. Univariate analyses were conducted prior to multivariate analyses to screen for relevant variables. The variables with statistically significant associations in univariate analyses were included in the multivariate models. To mitigate multicollinearity, variables with a variance inflation factor (VIF) greater than 5 were excluded. Differences were considered statistically significant when P < 0.05.

[results] Results Characteristics of Subjects Finally, 337 eyes of 208 participants (70 male subjects and 138 female subjects) were included in this study. Owing to suboptimal image quality or participants’ inability to complete bilateral examinations, the contralateral eyes of 79 subjects were excluded from the analysis. Table 1 shows the demographic and clinical characteristics of the participants. Table 1. Clinical Characteristics of the Study Population Characteristics N = 337 Eyes of 208 Patients Number of subjects’ eyes, n 337 Female eyes, n (%) 226 (67.06) Age, y, M (Q1, Q3) 27.0 (15.5, 46.0) AL, mm, M (Q1, Q3) 24.29 (23.35, 25.32) CCT, µm, mean ± SD 562.93 ± 32.59 AQD, mm, M (Q1, Q3) 2.90 (2.62, 3.13) ACD, mm, mean ± SD 3.43 ± 0.40 LT, mm, M (Q1, Q3) 3.72 (3.44, 4.23) CT, µm, M (Q1, Q3) 289.16 (238.75, 348.05) Average C/D, M (Q1, Q3) 0.51 (0.38, 0.61) RNFL thickness, mm, mean ± SD 108.39 ± 9.62 Vertical C/D, M (Q1, Q3) 0.46 (0.34, 0.54) BMO, mm, M (Q1, Q3) 1803.62 (1693.73, 1902.01) DFD, mm, M (Q1, Q3) 4724.48 (4513.17, 5023.04) DFA, degrees, mean ± SD 6.64 ± 3.39 Inner retinal layer VD (%), M (Q1, Q3) 70.35 (66.05, 74.32) SVC-VD (%), mean ± SD 64.73 ± 5.48 DVC-VD (%), M (Q1, Q3) 19.61 (15.90, 23.58) Choroidal layer VD (%), M (Q1, Q3) 99.98 (96.68, 99.75) ACD, anterior chamber depth; AL, axial length; AQD, aqueous depth; BMO, Bruch's membrane opening; CCT, central corneal thickness; CT, choroidal thickness; C/D, cup-disc ratio; DFA, optic disc-fovea angle; DFD, optic disc-fovea distance; DVC, deep vascular complex; LT, lens thickness; SVC, superficial vascular complex; VD, vessel density. Characteristics of Each Parameter Grouped by Age and Gender All eyes were classified into 4 age groups: age 1 (≤20 years), age 2 (21–40 years), age 3 (41–60 years), and age 4 (≥61 years). ACD and AQD decreased progressively with age ( P < 0.001), whereas LT increased significantly ( P < 0.001), and intergroup comparisons revealed significant differences across all age categories ( P max = 0.005, α B = 0.008). RNFL thickness also showed a significant age-related decline ( P = 0.024), with the >60-year group exhibiting significantly thinner RNFL compared to the 1 to 40-year groups (p max = 0.005, α B = 0.008). The change of VD stratified with age and gender are shown in Figure 2 . In all layers, VD in the female population was higher than that in the male population in most age groups. In the inner retinal and SVC layers, VD exhibited similar trends across age groups, with a trend of decreasing VD with age in the >20 years group. In the choroidal layer, female participants exhibited significantly higher VD than male participants in both the 21 to 40-year and the >60-year age groups ( P >21–40 = 0.044; P >60 = 0.030). Across age groups, VD differed significantly in all layers except the DVC ( P < 0.001). Notably, only the inner retinal layer and SVC demonstrated consistent age-related trends, whereas other layers showed variable patterns of change with age. Figure 2. The change of vessel density stratified with age and gender in different layers. ( A ) The change of vessel density stratified with age and gender in the inner retinal layer. ( B ) The change of vessel density stratified with age and gender in superficial vascular complex (SVC). ( C ) The change of vessel density stratified with age and gender in deep vascular complex (DVC). ( D ) The change of vessel density stratified with age and gender in the choroidal layer. Statistical differences between gender groups are indicated by * ( P < 0.05). Characteristics of Each Parameter Grouped by Axial Length All eyes were divided into 3 groups based on AL: AL1 (≤24.00 mm); AL2 (24.01–26 mm); and AL3 (>26 mm). As the AL increased, parameters including AQD, ACD, DFD, and BMO diameter significantly increased (all P < 0.001), along with RNFL thickness ( P = 0.031). In contrast, LT ( P < 0.001), CT ( P < 0.001), and vertical C/D ( P = 0.002) showed a significant overall decrease. Furthermore, average VD in both the DVC ( P = 0.008) and choroidal layer ( P < 0.001) decreased progressively with increasing AL. No significant difference was found in the inner retinal layer and SVC average VD in the three groups. The distribution of average VD in the five regions among three groups is shown in Figure 3 . In the SVC and DVC layers, VD in the four regions surrounding the ONH exhibited opposite trends with AL, whereas the inner retinal layer followed a pattern consistent with the SVC. Among all layers, only the choroidal layer exhibited a consistent and significant reduction in VD across all regions as AL increased among the three AL groups ( P max < 0.001, α B = 0.017). Moreover, the choroidal layer displayed greater stability of data in terms of error bars. Figure 3. The average vessel density in five regions (disk [D], superior temporal [ST], inferior temporal [IT], inferior nasal [IN], and superior nasal [SN]) among the three groups (AL1 = <24.00 mm; AL2 = 24.01–26 mm; and AL3 = >26 mm). ( A ) The average vessel density in five regions in the inner retinal layer. ( B ) The average vessel density in five regions in the inner retinal layer in the superficial vascular complex (SVC). ( C ) The average vessel density in five regions in the inner retinal layer in the deep vascular complex (DVC). ( D ) The average vessel density in five regions in the inner retinal layer in the choroidal layer. Statistical differences between groups are indicated by * ( P < 0.05). Distribution of Vessel Density Between the Axial Length Less Than or Equal to and More Than the 26 mm Groups Eyes with an AL of less than or equal to 26 mm were included in the non-highly myopia group, and the remaining eyes were in the highly myopia group. The non-highly myopic group consisted of 282 eyes, wheres the highly myopic group included 55 eyes. The distribution of VD in the non-highly myopia group is shown in Figure 4 . In the non-highly myopic group, VD followed a consistent ranking pattern in the inner retina and SVC: temporal (T) > SN > IN, whereas in the DVC, it was ranked as inferior (I) > superior (S), and in the choroidal layer, it followed nasal (N) > T. When comparing the differences in VD between the superior-inferior and nasal-temporal hemispheres, consistent results were observed in the inner retinal layer and SVC, with the order S > I ( P inner = 0.004, P SVC < 0.001) and T > N ( P < 0.001). In the DVC, the order was I > S ( P < 0.001) and N > T ( P = 0.026). In the choroidal layer, N > T ( P < 0.001), no statistically significant differences were found in the S and I hemispheres. Figure 4. Vessel density distribution (the superior temporal [ST] on the top left , the inferior temporal [IT] region on the bottom left , the inferior nasal [IN] region on the bottom right , and the superior nasal [SN] region on the top right ) in different layers among the non-highly myopia group. ( A ) Vessel density distribution in the inner retinal layer. ( B ) Vessel density distribution in the superficial vascular complex. ( C ) Vessel density distribution in the deep vascular complex. ( D ) Vessel density distribution in the choroidal layer. In the same pie chart, different colored regions represent significant differences between regions ( P < 0.05). High saturation colors represent higher vessel density. As shown in Figure 5 , in the inner retina and SVC, the VD was higher in the temporal hemisphere and lower in the nasal hemisphere in the highly myopic group. In contrast, VD changes in the DVC exhibited an opposite trend to those observed in the temporal region of the SVC. In the choroidal layer, VD was significantly lower in all regions of the highly myopia group, with the most pronounced reduction occurring in the temporal hemisphere, but the ordering of VD was consistent between the two groups, as was the ordering in the inner retinal layer. A significant difference between the two groups was observed at all layers, except for the SN region in the DVC. However, the direction and magnitude of these differences varied across different layers and regions. Across different vascular layers, VD followed the pattern: choroidal layer > inner retinal layer > SVC > DVC. Figure 5. Vessel density distribution (the superior temporal [ST], the inferior temporal [IT], the inferior nasal [IN], and the superior nasal [SN]) in different layers among the non-highly myopia and highly myopia groups. ( A ) Vessel density distribution in the inner retinal layer. ( B ) Vessel density distribution in the superficial vascular complex. ( C ) Vessel density distribution in the deep vascular complex. ( D ) Vessel density distribution in the choroidal layer. Statistical differences between gender groups are indicated by * ( P < 0.05). High saturation colors represent higher vessel density. The bars indicate the vascular density percentage (VD%) across different regions. Blue denotes the non-highly myopia group, whereas orange represents the highly myopia group. Analysis of Factors Influencing Vessel Density at Different Layers Tables 2 and 3 summarized factors contributing on the VD in the inner retinal and choroidal layers. In univariable models, ACD, LT, and signal strength plays important roles in both models. However, gender, CCT, AQD, time, and DFA were not significantly associated with VD in either the inner retinal or choroidal layers. LT and average C/D were excluded, respectively, from the multivariable model 1 and 2 due to their VIF being greater than 5, which could lead to inaccurate model results. In multivariable models, ACD was no longer significantly associated with VD in either layer. LT and CT also became not significantly associated with VD in the choroidal layer. Finally, age, signal strength, and RNFL thickness were included in multivariable model for the inner retinal layer, whereas AL, signal strength, vertical C/D, BMO, and DFD were included in the multivariable model for the choroidal layer. Table 2. The P values in bold represent statistical significance.

[discussion] Discussion In this study, we analyzed the distribution characteristics of ONH VD across different regions and layers, as well as its influencing factors, in highly myopic and non-highly myopic populations using an OCTA imaging system. Our findings demonstrated that the correlation between age and VD exhibited significant tissue-layer specificity: a trend of decreasing VD with age in adults was observed in the SVC and inner retina, consistent with previous findings in the radial peripapillary retinal capillary plexus (RPC). 13 In contrast, the DVC and the choroidal layer did not show a significant correlation with age. We believe the age-related changes in the SVC and inner retinal layers may be associated with degenerative vascular conditions such as atherosclerosis. With aging, arterial walls progressively thicken and narrow, resulting in slower blood flow and reduced VD. 14 As atherosclerosis mainly affects large and medium-sized arteries, this could explain why the SVC is more prominently affected than the DVC. It is noteworthy that gender did not have a significant effect on VD in the three retinal layers. However, statistical significance was observed in the choroidal layer of individuals aged 21 to 40 years and those aged ≥60 years, probably indicating a potential hormonal influence that requires further validation through biochemical and molecular analyses. The effect of AL on VD demonstrated regional and layer specificity. In the different AL subgroups, we observed that VD in all regions of the choroidal layer significantly decreased with increasing AL. However, VD in the inner retina showed no significant change, likely due to the opposing trends observed in the SVC and DVC. Chen et al. 15 investigated the VD in RPC, SVC, and DVC. They reported significant differences in VD in the I, S, and N regions for RPC and SVC, with a decreasing trend associated with increasing AL in regions with group differences. However, no significant differences were found in the DVD across all four peripapillary regions. The influence of AL on VD varied by region within the same retinal layer, with the SVC demonstrating the most sensitive response, making it a key focus for analysis. Inter-regional analysis is particularly important to avoid misleading conclusions, as averaging VD across regions may obscure significant variations in VD trends. The effect of different ALs on retinal VD remains controversial in the literature. Cheng et al. 16 found that the VD in the RPC decreased with increasing AL in all five regions (I, S, N, T, and D). One study indicated that the VD in the D region of the RPC did not differ significantly between AL groups. 17 However, another study showed that average VD of the peripapillary regions in the RPC decreased with AL, whereas the VD in the D region increased. 18 Notably, in our study, the central region of the D region showed paradoxically elevated VD at all layers. This may be due to the fact that the D region is primarily supplied by the Zinn-Haller arterial circle, which has abundant collateral circulation, whereas the other four regions are supplied by the central arteries of the retina. 19 The difference between studies may be attributed to the different partitioning methods and the fact that the studies mentioned did not correct for AL, which standardizes the range of VD observations across different eyes, leading to more objective and accurate results. Additionally, differences in the layers of the VDs observed, the ages of the study populations, and the subgroups of AL may also account for the discrepancies. Consistent with previous studies, 9 the peripapillary choroidal layer VD may serve as a predictor of myopia. A tendency for VD to decrease with AL elongation was observed across all regions. Additionally, the SVC VD may also act as a sensitive predictor of myopia. However, this requires regional analysis due to varying trends in different regions, which still needs to be further validated by additional studies. We further stratified subjects into highly myopic and non-highly myopic groups to conduct a quantitative analysis of VD distribution and regional ordering. In the non-highly myopic group, the anatomic ordering of the VD in the inner retina mirrored the previously reported sequence of retinal artery and vein diameters. 20 The VD ordering in the inner retinal layers and the SVC followed a consistent pattern across the groups, likely due to the large retinal vessels traveling within the SVC, suggesting that perfusion in these two layers is predominantly governed by major retinal vessels. Interestingly, the VD distribution pattern in the DVC appears to be associated with that of the SVC. The IN region, which had the lowest VD in the SVC, showed a relatively higher rank in the DVC, whereas the ST region, which had the highest SVC VD, showed a reduced rank in the DVC. We hypothesize that this may reflect a compensatory vascular mechanism. VD changes may be influenced by transmural pressure, which varies across different vascular layers. Downstream vessels might undergo morphological changes to compensate for upstream flow alterations. 21 However, this speculation requires further investigation. Between the highly myopic and non-highly myopic groups, significant VD alterations were observed in the temporal hemisphere of the inner retinal layers, with an increase in VD in the SVC and a decrease in the DVC, whereas the opposite trend was observed in the nasal hemisphere of the layer, which may result in the two groups of VD being ordered differently in the SVC and the DVC but being ordered consistently in the inner retina due to the counteracting effect. Mo et al. 22 reported significant reductions in retinal VD in the N and T regions of the ONH in highly myopic eyes. These discrepancies may stem from the lack of correction in their analysis and inconsistencies in the segmentation of regions. As the AL increases, the central vascular trunk in the inner retinal layer is dragged nasally. 23 Jonas et al. 24 found that a reduction in the angle between the superior and inferior temporal vascular arches of the ONH may explain the observed increase in the temporal VD in the inner retinal layer. This may be attributed to biomechanical stretching, which could enhance perfusion efficiency in the temporal arcuate fiber region by altering vascular geometry, thereby preserving visual function. 17 The ordering of the choroidal layers showed a significantly higher VD on the nasal side than on the temporal side, with the possible explanation being that the watershed area located between the temporal artery and the area supplied by the posterior nasociliary artery mostly involves the temporal side and rarely the nasal side. 25 Most studies examining the correlation between choroidal blood flow and myopia have focused on the macular region. Changes in choroidal blood flow, which affect CT, may serve as a predictor for myopia progression, as demonstrated in studies on guinea pigs’ optic nerves. 26 Our results also showed that choroidal VD decreases with increasing AL across all five regions, with the most significant reduction in the T hemisphere region especially in the IT region, but there was no change in the ordering of VD, probably due to the absence of significant regional damage to the fundus structures. In addition, choroidal layer VD showed the most stable performance with the smallest standard deviation among all layers. This suggests that choroidal VD may serve as a sensitive indicator for early disease prediction, particularly in the T hemisphere region. Excessive axial elongation of the eyeball leads to biomechanical stretching of the retina and choroid, 27 , 28 which may result in the straightening and narrowing of vessels in highly myopic eyes and a reduction in vessel branching, which in turn leads to lower VD. 29 The reasons for the more pronounced reduction in the VD of the T region of the choroidal layer in highly myopic eyes remain to be further explored. Our study focused on the VD in a population with normal fundus structures and analyzed its influencing factors. Signal strength, a key parameter of OCTA imaging quality, was found to have a significant impact on VD measurements across all layers. Whether signal strength mediates the relationship between AL and choroidal VD is a noteworthy concern. To explore this, we conducted a mediation analysis. The results showed a direct effect of AL on choroidal VD of −0.6673 and an indirect effect of −0.0358 mediated by signal strength, leading to a total effect of −0.7031. The mediating role of signal strength accounted for approximately 5% of the total effect, indicating a limited influence. Therefore, we did not include signal strength as a covariate in the final analysis. After correction according to the AL, we observed no significant association between AL and inner retinal VD, which aligns with the findings of Lv et al., who reported a similar trend in the SVP VD. 30 In our model 1, we identified a positive correlation between RNFL thickness and inner retinal VD, which is consistent with a previous study demonstrating a similar positive correlation between RNFL thickness and SVC VD. Together, these findings reinforce the structural-functional relationship between retinal circulation and neural integrity. 31 CT thinning due to increased AL has been recognized as a strong correlate of decreased choroidal VD in several studies, 32 – 34 and this was confirmed in our univariable model. However, CT was excluded from the multivariable model. Both CT and choroidal VD are influenced by factors such as age and AL, and their physiological link requires further investigation. 35 This aligns with the results of our multivariable model, suggesting that CT may not be a sufficient indicator of choroidal circulation. Smaller LT and deeper ACD may reflect a compensatory mechanism to counteract the optical effects of axial elongation during myopic progression. 36 – 38 However, due to the confounding effects of LT and ACD with age, AL, and other factors, these variables were similarly excluded from the multivariable models 1 and 2. The effect of vertical C/D on the choroidal blood flow has not been previously reported, but a possible explanation is that vertical C/D is negatively correlated with AL, 39 and the exact mechanism needs to be further investigated. DFD and DFA are important parameters of the posterior fundus that collectively characterize the position of the ONH relative to the macula. DFD increased with axial elongation, but DFA did not appear to be significantly correlated with AL. The axial elongation-related increase in DFD was attributed to the enlargement of the parapapillary β / γ zone, which was defined as the parapapillary zone with visible sclera and visible large choroidal vessels and was associated with capillary occlusion. 40 , 41 This may explain the inclusion of DFD in the model 2. In moderately myopic eyes, BMO movement typically occurred, which may lead to passive exposure of the choroid, thereby forming the γ zone. In highly myopic eyes, the enlargement of BMO may directly stretch the choroidal capillaries leading to a decrease in branch density. 42 Therefore, BMO was also included in our multivariate model. Our study further confirmed that CT, ACD, AQD, and DFD all increased with increasing AL across the different AL subgroups, and these findings highlighted the need for further investigation to elucidate the exact mechanisms. Previous studies have suggested that the retinal and choroidal blood flow is influenced by circadian rhythms. 43 , 44 In our study, when we categorized time into ante meridiem and post meridiem as dichotomous variables, we did not find a correlation between time and VD. For the first time, we comprehensively examined the choroidal VD surrounding the optic disc across different regions and analyzed its influencing factors. Additionally, we experimentally collected and sequenced VD data from patients with glaucoma and those with glaucoma combined with high myopia, revealing distinct regional VD ordering patterns among diseased and healthy individuals. This finding highlights the importance of regional hemodynamic studies in disease characterization. Moreover, our partitioning method more accurately reflects vascular alignment and anatomic structures, an approach that has been rarely explored in previous studies. Furthermore, our study corrected for AL-related imaging distortions in OCTA, collected a broad range of ocular parameters, and accounted for the effects of physiological factors such as age, gender, and examination time. Our findings may contribute to the early detection of disease-related microvascular alterations, provide a theoretical foundation for understanding the mechanisms of myopia-associated microcirculatory changes, elucidate the dynamic evolution of VD with axial elongation, and establish a baseline database of VD across different ONH regions and layers in healthy populations. However, several limitations should be acknowledged: First, we used AL instead of diopters as a variable to assess its effect on VD. Second, the highly myopic group had a relatively small sample size of only 55 eyes, representing 16.3% of the total sample, which may limit the generalizability of the findings. Future studies should incorporate larger highly myopic cohorts to enable a more comprehensive assessment of VD alterations. Last, due to the exclusion of patients with hypertension and diabetes, recruiting elderly subjects was challenging. Nonetheless, we accounted for age-related effects when assessing VD. In conclusion, we examined the distribution of retinal and choroidal VD across different regions after correcting for AL and analyzed the factors influencing VD at both layers. Our findings demonstrate that choroidal VD remains relatively stable, exhibiting a progressive decline in all regions with increasing AL, with the most pronounced reduction observed in the temporal hemisphere, but the ordering of the different regions did not change. However, not all retinal regions exhibited similar reductions, expanding our understanding of blood flow dynamics around the optic disc.

[conclusion] Conclusions Inner retinal VD followed T > SN > IN, primarily contributed by the SVC, whereas choroidal VD followed N > T. With increasing AL, choroidal VD declined across all regions, most prominently in the T hemisphere, whereas the inner retinal VD trends varied by region and layer.

retrieved_papers.title: Choroidal thickness changes during the menstrual cycle.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2013]
[Abstract] To evaluate the effects of the menstrual cycle on the choroidal thickness of healthy women of reproductive age using spectral-domain optical coherence tomography.
A total of 23 right eyes of 23 women with regular natural menstrual cycles of 28-30 d and ages 22-30 years were included in this prospective study. The choroidal thicknesses of the women were measured using high-resolution spectral-domain optical coherence tomography line scans with the activated enhanced depth imaging mode in the early follicular, ovulatory and mid-luteal phases of the menstrual cycle (3rd, 14th and 21st days) using a follow-up scanning protocol of the Spectralis optical coherence tomograph by setting the early follicular phase scan as the reference image. Choroidal thickness measurements were taken at the fovea and at two points that were 1500 µm nasal and temporal to the fovea. The mean arterial pressure, spherical equivalent, intraocular pressure, central corneal thickness and retinal and retinal nerve fiber layer thicknesses were also measured. All measurements were taken within the same menstrual cycle.
The mean ± standard deviation (standard error of the mean) of subfoveal choroidal thicknesses in the early follicular, ovulatory and mid-luteal phases were 383.87 ± 84.38 (17.59), 373.74 ± 82.40 (17.18) and 359.09 ± 79.65 (16.61) µm, respectively. The relative reductions in choroidal thickness between early follicular and ovulatory phases and early follicular and mid-luteal phases were 2.64% and 6.47%, respectively. The subfoveal choroidal thickness was decreased significantly in the mid-luteal phase when compared with both the early follicular (p < 0.001) and ovulatory phases (p = 0.003). However, the measures of subfoveal choroidal thickness in the early follicular phase were non-conclusively greater (p = 0.071) than in the ovulatory phase. Additionally, the mean arterial pressure, spherical equivalent, intraocular pressure, central corneal thickness and retinal and retinal nerve fiber layer thicknesses did not significantly differ during the menstrual cycle (p > 0.05 for all).
The choroidal thickness decreased significantly in the mid-luteal phase of the menstrual cycle in young, healthy women. These findings emphasize the importance of the menstrual phase in the interpretation of choroidal thickness measurements in women of reproductive age.

[Introduction] Introduction The menstrual cycle is defined as the regular, cyclical variations in the secretion of female hormones and the accompanying changes in the reproductive organs during a woman’s reproductive years [ 1 ]. The menstrual cycle can be divided into three phases based on the levels of hormones present in the blood. During the follicular phase, low levels of estrogen and progesterone are observed. In contrast, the ovulatory phase is characterized by high estrogen and low progesterone levels. Finally, the luteal phase is typified by high estrogen and progesterone levels [ 2 ]. The eye exhibits sensitivity to sex hormones and functions as a target organ for their effects. Previous research has documented differences in ocular blood flow, tear secretion, and biometric parameters, particularly between premenopausal and postmenopausal women [ 3 , 4 ]. Furthermore, application of optical coherence tomography (OCT) in reproductive-aged women has revealed cyclical variations in choroidal thickness (CT), and optic disc morphology during the menstrual cycle [ 5 , 6 ]. In addition to the aforementioned structural alterations, vascular changes were observed in the ophthalmic and central retinal arteries via Doppler ultrasonography during the menstrual cycle [ 7 ]. Optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) has emerged as a valuable modality for comprehensively evaluating intraocular blood flow. This non-invasive technique offers quantitative data on the microvascular network within both the macular and peripapillary regions. A recent study identified significant microvascular alterations within the macula during the menstrual cycle [ 8 ]. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to evaluate layer-segmented peripapillary microvascular changes across distinct menstrual phases using OCTA. The objective of this study was to assess both microvascular and structural changes in the macular and optic nerve using OCTA in a cohort of healthy reproductive subjects.

The menstrual cycle can be divided into three phases based on the levels of hormones present in the blood. During the follicular phase, low levels of estrogen and progesterone are observed. In contrast, the ovulatory phase is characterized by high estrogen and low progesterone levels. Finally, the luteal phase is typified by high estrogen and progesterone levels [ 2 ]. Furthermore, application of optical coherence tomography (OCT) in reproductive-aged women has revealed cyclical variations in choroidal thickness (CT), and optic disc morphology during the menstrual cycle [ 5 , 6 ]. In addition to the aforementioned structural alterations, vascular changes were observed in the ophthalmic and central retinal arteries via Doppler ultrasonography during the menstrual cycle [ 7 ]. Optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) has emerged as a valuable modality for comprehensively evaluating intraocular blood flow. This non-invasive technique offers quantitative data on the microvascular network within both the macular and peripapillary regions. A recent study identified significant microvascular alterations within the macula during the menstrual cycle [ 8 ]. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to evaluate layer-segmented peripapillary microvascular changes across distinct menstrual phases using OCTA. The objective of this study was to assess both microvascular and structural changes in the macular and optic nerve using OCTA in a cohort of healthy reproductive subjects.

[methods] Materials and methods This prospective study was conducted at Beyoglu Eye Training and Research Hospital, University of Health Sciences. The study protocol received ethical approval from the Hamidiye Ethics Committee at the University of Health Sciences and adhered to the tenets of the Declaration of Helsinki (Approval number 20/508). This study included healthy female subjects with a documented history of regular menstrual cycles over the preceding six months. Participants were recruited from among hospital staff volunteers rather than routine ophthalmology outpatients, in order to minimize potential confounding factors related to ocular or systemic pathologies. All participants provided written informed consent after receiving a detailed explanation of the study protocol. The inclusion criteria stipulated that only one eye per participant was eligible for inclusion in the study, provided that both eyes met the requisite criteria. The selection of the included eye was randomized to minimize selection bias. Eumenorrhea was confirmed based on self-reported menstrual cycle history over the preceding two years. The study included women aged 20 to 30 years with self-reported regular ovulatory menstrual cycles ranging from 24 to 35 days. To ensure accurate phase identification, participants were instructed to prospectively track and document the onset of their menstrual cycles. Individuals with irregular or highly variable patterns were excluded. Additional exclusion criteria included a history of pregnancy, childbirth, or breastfeeding within the past year; current or previous use of hormonal contraceptives within the preceding six months; systemic diseases such as hypertension, diabetes mellitus, or thyroid dysfunction; and the use of continuous systemic medications.Ocular exclusion criteria comprised refractive errors exceeding ± 6.00 diopters (spherical equivalent), axial length ≤ 22 mm or ≥ 26 mm, amblyopia, previous intraocular surgery, ocular hypertension, glaucoma, intraocular inflammation, or any media opacity that could compromise imaging. Eyes with poor-quality OCTA scans due to motion artifacts or segmentation errors were also excluded. Given the restricted age range, absence of hormonal medication use, and consistent cycle documentation, follicular and luteal phase durations were considered physiologically comparable among participants. Mean arterial pressure (MAP) was estimated using the following formula: MAP = diastolic blood pressure + 1/3 (systolic blood pressure - diastolic blood pressure). The central corneal thickness was obtained through the utilisation of ultrasound pachymetry (USP). Prior to the measurement, a topical anaesthetic was administered to the eye. The patient was then instructed to fixate on a distant target while the USP probe was positioned perpendicularly to the central corneal surface. Axial length measurements were conducted using the AL-Scan Optical Biometer (NIDEK Co., Ltd., Aichi, Japan) prior to pupillary dilation. For this study, axial length values were obtained as the average of three consecutive high-quality scans. All measurements were performed under standardized room lighting conditions by trained personnel, following the manufacturer’s recommended protocol. All examinations were conducted within a single menstrual cycle for each participant. The examinations were conducted at three specific time points: early follicular phase on cycle day 3, ovulatory phase on cycle day 14, and mid-luteal phase on cycle day 21. To minimize potential circadian variations, all examinations were performed between 11:00 and 12:00 am. Measurements were performed within the same menstrual cycle. An examination was conducted, comprising the evaluation of blood pressure, best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) assessment using a Snellen chart, slit-lamp biomicroscopic examination, intraocular pressure measurement with a Goldmann applanation tonometer, dilated fundus examination, and imaging using OCT and OCTA. OCT and OCTA imaging utilizing the Topcon DRI Triton (Topcon Inc., Japan) swept-source OCT (SS-OCT) with angiography were conducted. Imaging protocol Macular and peripapillary regions were evaluated using a wide-field OCT scan (12 mm x 9 mm) acquired on the device. The instrument’s integrated software was employed to analyze central macular thickness (CMT), ganglion cell inner plexiform layer (GCIPL) thickness, and central CT. Images were analyzed and measured independently by two graders. Retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) thickness and CT were assessed in each of the four peripapillary quadrants. CT was measured manually from the outer border of the hyperreflective retinal pigment epithelium layer to the inner surface of the sclera in the temporal, superior, nasal and inferior segments. Microvascularity in the macula and peripapillary area was assessed using Topcon DRI Triton OCTA. Two scan patterns were used: a 3 × 3 mm volume scan for the macular region and a 4.5 × 4.5 mm volume scan for the peripapillary region. Segmentation of the superficial capillary plexus (SCP), deep capillary plexus (DCP), and choriocapillaris (CC) was performed automatically using the integrated software (IMAGEnet6, v1.23.15008, Basic License 10) (Fig. 1 ). All OCTA scans underwent a meticulous review process to ensure accurate segmentation and optimal image quality. The foveal avascular zone (FAZ) areas in both the SCP and DCP were manually delineated using the built-in measurement tool of the OCTA device by two independent, masked graders. The average of the two observers’ measurements was used for subsequent FAZ area analysis. To assess the reliability of the manual measurements, the interobserver intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) was calculated based on 30 randomly selected images. Fig. 1 Macular and peripapillary vessel density measurements for the superficial capillary plexus (SCP), deep capillary plexus (DCP), and choriocapillaris (CC) Vessel density (VD) within the SCP, DCP, and CC was evaluated in both the foveal (central) and parafoveal (superior, temporal, inferior, nasal) sectors. This analysis utilized a modified ETDRS grid featuring a 1 mm inner diameter and a 3 mm outer diameter ring to define the measurement regions. Additionally, peripapillary VD was automatically analyzed within each segmented slab across the four peripapillary quadrants (Fig. 1 ). Statistical analysis The analysis of the data was carried out using IBM SPSS Statistics version 23.0 (SPSS Inc. Chicago, IL). Normality of data distribution was evaluated using the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test, in line with the default configuration of the statistical software. Although the Shapiro-Wilk test is generally preferred for smaller samples, the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test remains a valid and widely used method. Quantitative data are presented as means and standard deviations (SD) within the text and tables. A comparison of measurements between the follicular, ovulatory and mid-luteal phases was conducted using repeated measures analysis of variance. The significance level for pairwise comparisons of significant variables was determined using the Bonferroni correction, calculated by dividing 0.05 by the number of sub-hypotheses. The Greenhouse-Geisser correction was used in the lack of sphericity. A p-value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

[results] Results A total of 33 subjects were initially enrolled in the study; however, two were lost to follow-up, resulting in a final sample size of 31 participants. The mean age of the participants was 27.3 years (SD ± 3.9 years). The mean axial length of the study eyes was 23.4 ± 0.9 mm (range: 22.2–24.8 mm). Notably, all participants maintained BCVA of 20/20 or better throughout the study period, demonstrating no significant changes in BCVA. The baseline ophthalmic examination revealed a mean intraocular pressure of 12.4 ± 2.0 mmHg. All subjects were phakic. There was no history of any previous intraocular surgical procedure. The average menstrual cycle length was 28.1 ± 2.2 days (range: 26 to 32 days). Our analysis revealed no statistically significant differences ( p > 0.05) in MAP, intraocular pressure, refractive error, or central corneal thickness measurements between the three menstrual phases (Table 1 ). Statistical analysis revealed a significant difference ( p = 0.002) in macular CT measurements between the three menstrual phases. Moreover, measurements of peripapillary CT in the temporal and inferior quadrants also showed a significant difference ( p = 0.004 and p = 0.004 respectively). However, peripapillary RNFL thickness measurements in all four quadrants (temporal, nasal, superior, and inferior) did not show statistically significant variations. Tables 3 and 4 present the findings from OCTA measurements of the macular and peripapillary regions, respectively. The results revealed no statistically significant differences in FAZ parameters across the menstrual cycle. However, VD measurements exhibited a significant difference only within the inferior quadrant of the CC in the macula ( p = 0.030). In the peripapillary region, VD measurements demonstrated significant differences during the menstrual cycle, in the superior quadrant of the SCP and the inferior quadrant of the DCP ( p = 0.040 and p = 0.008 respectively). It is notable that there were significant variations observed in the density of CC VD between menstrual cycle phases in the peripapillary region in both the temporal and inferior quadrants ( p = 0.011 and p = 0.007 respectively). In the macular region, CC VD in the inferior quadrant increased significantly from the early follicular phase (54.05 ± 1.76) to ovulation (54.51 ± 2.09, p = 0.001), and further increased in the mid-luteal phase (55.25 ± 1.94, p = 0.001). In the peripapillary region, SCP VD in the superior quadrant rose from 67.53 ± 6.31 to 68.90 ± 5.32 during ovulation ( p < 0.001), then decreased to 66.48 ± 6.24 in the mid-luteal phase ( p = 0.008). DCP VD in the inferior quadrant showed a similar pattern, increasing from 45.14 ± 7.29 to 48.92 ± 7.69 ( p = 0.001), then declining to 47.05 ± 8.20 ( p = 0.007). Peripapillary CC VD also varied significantly across phases. In the temporal quadrant, it rose from 66.34 ± 6.25 to 68.15 ± 5.66 ( p = 0.010) and then declined to 67.11 ± 6.37 ( p < 0.001). In the inferior quadrant, it increased from 57.60 ± 4.85 to 61.40 ± 4.63 ( p < 0.001), followed by a decrease to 59.39 ± 4.91 ( p = 0.001).
