[discussion] Discussion The present study constitutes an investigation of menstrual cycle-associated alterations in microvascular and structural characteristics within the macular and peripapillary regions. The investigation was conducted through a comprehensive analysis of OCT parameters such as GCIPL, CMT and CT, as well as OCTA-assessed SCP, DCP, and CC layers. The study analysis demonstrated a significant decrease in macular CT from the follicular to the mid-luteal phase of the menstrual cycle. Peripapillary CT measurements also exhibited a significant reduction in the temporal and inferior quadrants. Furthermore, detailed OCTA analysis revealed significant variations in VD. In the macula, a significant increase was observed exclusively within the inferior quadrant of the CC layer. VD measurements within the peripapillary region revealed significant differences between menstrual cycle phases, particularly in the superior quadrant of the SCP and the inferior quadrant of the DCP. It is noteworthy that significant differences were observed in the CC VD between the different menstrual cycle phases within the peripapillary region, specifically in the temporal and inferior quadrants. Whilst a recent study has examined menstrual cycle-related changes in the microvasculature of the macula and the peripapillary region, the present study is the first to investigate layer-segmented peripapillary vascular changes across menstrual phases using OCTA [ 9 ]. The vasodilatory influence of estrogen on ocular blood flow is exerted via both endothelium-dependent and independent mechanisms [ 3 ]. Elevated estrogen levels have been observed to stimulate the synthesis of nitric oxide while concurrently inhibiting the production of endothelin-1. These counterbalancing effects collectively promote vasodilation and reduce vascular resistance [ 10 ]. In contrast, progesterone functions as an estrogen antagonist, inducing vasoconstriction [ 11 ]. The majority of studies examining the effects of hormones on ocular microvasculature have concentrated on the period of pregnancy. Previous research into the subject of pregnancy has indicated that the observed increase in vascular density within the retina and the optic disc during pregnancy is likely attributable to physiological adaptations necessitated by the gravid state [ 12 ]. Hormonal fluctuations constitute a potential mechanism underlying these ocular vascular changes. Given the established influence of sex hormones on various physiological and pathological processes within ocular tissues, it has been postulated that these hormones may contribute to the pathogenesis of certain ocular diseases [ 13 , 14 ]. A small number of studies have been conducted into the impact of the menstrual cycle on ocular anatomy and vascularization, as evidenced in the literature [ 4 , 5 , 8 ]. Guo et al. demonstrated a significant decrease in VD within the nasal and inferior quadrants of the DCP during the ovulatory phase compared to the early follicular and luteal phases. The authors hypothesise that hormone fluctuations across the menstrual cycle may be responsible for the significant changes in DCP microvasculature observed during the ovulatory phase. This finding contrasts with the stability of DCP VD observed during the early follicular and luteal phases [ 8 ]. In contrast to the aforementioned study, our findings revealed no statistically significant difference in SCP and DCP in the macular area. Nevertheless, a statistically significant alteration in VD was observed in the inferior quadrant of the CC layer. This discrepancy may be attributed to the fact that the choroidal circulation exhibits a comparatively limited capacity for autoregulation in comparison to the retina. The choroid is more directly influenced by systemic conditions and exhibits reduced capacity to maintain constant blood flow during fluctuations in ocular perfusion pressure [ 15 ]. Moreover, retinal arterioles function as the primary resistance vessels within the retinal vasculature, exerting a considerable influence on retinal hemodynamics through the modulation of their luminal diameter. The mechanism of vascular autoregulation in retinal vessels represents a significant area of investigation. Retinal arterioles modulate capillary perfusion by dynamically adjusting their luminal diameter through changes in vascular smooth muscle tone [ 16 ]. Furthermore, the utilisation of different OCTA devices may have contributed to the observed variability in the findings. Device-specific differences in OCTA technology, including segmentation algorithms, can influence the measurement and visualisation of vascular changes and may contribute to the observed discrepancies in VD across studies. In alignment with the established characteristics of the ovulation phase, characterized by elevated estrogen and reduced progesterone levels, the peripapillary region exhibited a notable increase in VD measurements between menstrual cycle phases. This was particularly evident in the superior quadrant of the SCP, as well as in the inferior quadrant of the DCP, and also in the temporal and inferior quadrants of the CC. Post-hoc analysis revealed a pronounced increase in VD during the ovulatory phase relative to both the follicular and mid-luteal phases. Furthermore, our study evaluated alterations in the choroid through OCT. The study demonstrated a significant reduction in macular CT from the follicular to the mid-luteal phase of the menstrual cycle. Also, peripapillary CT measurements demonstrated a significant reduction in the temporal and inferior quadrants. The structural changes observed in the choroid across the menstrual cycle were found to be parallel with the microvascular alterations in the CC. The present findings are consistent with those of recent studies exploring hormonal influences on posterior ocular structures. A recent study reported reduced DCP VD in women using combined oral contraceptives (COCs), suggesting a hormone-mediated modulation of retinal microvasculature detectable by OCTA. In a similar vein, Icoz et al. observed a significant decrease in choroidal vascular index values in individuals who used COC, in comparison to those who did not use COC. This observation is indicative of alterations in the luminal component of the choroid. These findings are complementary to our observation of increased VD during the ovulatory phase, suggesting that the physiological estrogen surge may enhance microvascular perfusion, whereas hormonally suppressed states such as COC use may attenuate it, thereby highlighting the hormonal sensitivity of retinal and choroidal microcirculation [ 17 , 18 ]. The existing literature has demonstrated alterations in CT throughout the menstrual cycle. Ulaş et al. employed spectral-domain OCT to demonstrate a significant reduction in CT during the mid-luteal phase in comparison to earlier stages of the menstrual cycle in a cohort of healthy women. Similarly, Asıkgarip et al. reported a consistent reduction in CT across all regions during the mid-luteal phase. Proposed mechanisms for these changes in CT include vasoconstriction mediated by alpha-adrenergic and/or renin activity, as well as hormonal fluctuations that are characteristic of the mid-luteal phase [ 19 , 20 ]. Although we discussed multiple mechanisms through which estrogen exerts its impact on retinal microvascular structure, there remains a degree of inconsistency in how this effect is represented within the scientific literature. Leung et al. conducted a study to explore the relationship between hormone replacement therapy and retinal arteriolar diameter in an older female population. Contrary to the prevailing hypothesis, their findings indicated no significant vasodilatory effect of hormone replacement therapy on retinal arterioles. In contrast, Wong et al. reported a cross-sectional association between estrogen replacement therapy and narrower retinal vessel diameters in a population-based study, independent of blood pressure and other vascular factors. Both studies employed a standardized methodology, measuring retinal arteriolar and venular diameters within a one-disc diameter radius of the optic disc using computer-assisted image analysis of digitized color fundus photographs [ 21 , 22 ]. Two decades ago, Karadeniz et al. investigated the changes in hemodynamics observed in the ophthalmic, central retinal, and posterior ciliary arteries across the menstrual cycle. Doppler ultrasonography was performed on 23 healthy women across six distinct menstrual cycle phases. No significant variations in pulsatility index, resistance index, or peak systolic velocity were observed among the arterial segments. The authors proposed that these findings indicate that the complex interplay of hormonal influences on systemic hemodynamics, including heart rate, blood pressure, and blood volume, may offset any potential menstrual cycle-related alterations in ocular blood flow [ 7 ]. Recent advancements in ophthalmic technology have shifted the paradigm for assessing intraocular perfusion away from traditional Doppler and pulsatility examinations towards the utilization of OCT and OCTA. In alignment with this technological shift, our study comprehensively employed OCT and OCTA imaging for data acquisition. In a previous OCTA study, Savastano et al. observed a significantly higher VD measurements of CC in patients with hypothalamic amenorrhea compared to controls, both in the whole area and in the fovea area. The authors attributed this finding to potential endothelial dysfunction secondary to estrogen deficiency, characterized by reduced nitric oxide production, elevated oxidative stress, and proinflammatory cytokine levels, which ultimately lead to abnormal vascular remodeling and microvascular constriction. Based on these findings, they hypothesized that the observed increase in VD might reflect compensatory microvascular changes in response to these underlying pathophysiological processes [ 23 ]. Consistent with the findings of Savastano et al., a recent study by Çetinkaya et al. utilized swept-source OCTA to investigate retinal and choroidal microvascular changes in postmenopausal women. The study reported a reduction in VD of CC in postmenopausal participants compared to age-matched controls with ongoing menstruation. Notably, a negative correlation between VD and menopause duration suggested that prolonged hypoestrogenism adversely impacts the microvascular structure of this layer [ 24 ]. A primary limitation of this study was the absence of serum sex hormone level measurements. Without direct quantification of estradiol and progesterone levels, we were unable to correlate retinal and choroidal microvascular changes with precise hormonal fluctuations during the menstrual cycle. Although menstrual phases were carefully timed using well-established cycle days (day 3, 14, and 21) and participants were required to prospectively document the onset of menstruation to confirm regular ovulatory cycles, individual variation in hormonal profiles may still exist. This limitation introduces the potential for misclassification of cycle phases, which could affect the accuracy of interpreting phase-specific vascular changes. The absence of biochemical validation may reduce the granularity of phase assignment. As such, the observed changes in VD and CT should be interpreted with caution, and future studies incorporating serum hormone analysis would offer a more robust framework for confirming the hormonal basis of the vascular alterations observed with OCTA. Additionally, the relatively small sample size may limit the generalizability of the findings and reduce statistical power to detect more subtle microvascular changes across menstrual phases. In conclusion, the present study indicates that the hormonal fluctuations associated with the menstrual cycle exert a substantial impact on the architecture and vasculature of the retina and choroid. A crucial finding is the identification of structural alterations in the choroid across menstrual phases, which may indicate a potential association with concurrent microvascular changes in the CC. Notable variations were observed in both peripapillary CT and CC microvasculature in the inferior and temporal regions. It is therefore recommended that clinicians take the menstrual cycle phase into account when interpreting OCTA data in women of reproductive age, to ensure optimal diagnostic accuracy and treatment planning.

[conclusion] Conclusion Hormonal fluctuations throughout the menstrual cycle appear to influence both retinal and choroidal microvasculature, particularly in the choriocapillaris and peripapillary regions. These findings underscore the importance of considering menstrual phase when interpreting OCTA measurements in women of reproductive age, to improve diagnostic accuracy and consistency in clinical and research settings.

retrieved_papers.title: The effect of postmenopausal hormonal drop on optic nerve head and peripapillary perfusion using optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA).
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2022]
[Abstract] We studied the effect of menopause with subsequent estrogen drop on optic nerve head structure and peripapillary vasculature. This cross-sectional analytic study was carried out on 100 eyes of 100 patients; patients were divided into a premenopausal group (50 eyes) and a postmenopausal group (50 eyes). Optical coherence tomography was done to evaluate retinal nerve fiber layer thickness (RNFLT) and optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) to assess the peripapillary capillary vessel density. RNFLT as well as the peripapillary vessel density (VD) were significantly lower in the postmenopausal group (P value < 0.001) with increasing age, hormonal drop, and higher intraocular pressure (IOP), specifically in the inferior quadrant. However, the negative correlation between IOP and VD (r = - 0.541) was stronger than its negative correlation with RNFLT (r = - 0.318). Postmenopausal hormonal changes lead to a significant rise in IOP-although still not glaucomatous- and a decrease in the RNFLT and perfusion of the optic nerve. This confirms the relation between hormonal drop and glaucoma in postmenopausal women. Changes in peripapillary vascular density were more evident than RNFL in correlation with IOP and age changes. So, OCTA can be used to detect early optic nerve affection.

[Introduction] Introduction Despite the global increase in life expectancy, the age of menopause remains fixed. As the proportion of older women increases, an increasing prevalence of menopause and postmenopausal diseases may be expected to be seen 1 . Postmenopausal hormonal changes, especially the decline in estrogen level, are thought to play an important role in increasing the incidence of ocular symptoms and ocular diseases 2 . Estrogen is a known vasodilator and neuroprotective hormone. Its postmenopausal withdrawal can affect the retina because of the presence of estrogen receptors in the retina and its vasculature 3 . In addition, it can increase the likelihood of glaucomatous optic nerve damage 4 , because of the effect of estrogen on lowering the intraocular pressure (IOP) 5 . Currently, there is accumulating evidence that suggests a decrease in RNFLT in postmenopausal women 6 . However, no studies investigated the peripapillary vasculature in postmenopausal women to support similar changes. So, in this study, we investigated the effect of postmenopausal hormonal change on ONH and peripapillary perfusion.

[introduction] Introduction Despite the global increase in life expectancy, the age of menopause remains fixed. As the proportion of older women increases, an increasing prevalence of menopause and postmenopausal diseases may be expected to be seen 1 . Postmenopausal hormonal changes, especially the decline in estrogen level, are thought to play an important role in increasing the incidence of ocular symptoms and ocular diseases 2 . Estrogen is a known vasodilator and neuroprotective hormone. Its postmenopausal withdrawal can affect the retina because of the presence of estrogen receptors in the retina and its vasculature 3 . In addition, it can increase the likelihood of glaucomatous optic nerve damage 4 , because of the effect of estrogen on lowering the intraocular pressure (IOP) 5 . Currently, there is accumulating evidence that suggests a decrease in RNFLT in postmenopausal women 6 . However, no studies investigated the peripapillary vasculature in postmenopausal women to support similar changes. So, in this study, we investigated the effect of postmenopausal hormonal change on ONH and peripapillary perfusion.

[methods] Materials and methods This cross-sectional analytic study was carried out on 100 patients selected from the general Outpatient Clinics of Kasr Alainy school of medicine, Cairo University Hospitals. Informed consent was obtained from all participants prior to any study procedure. Patients’ selection Premenopausal and postmenopausal healthy women aged 40–60 years with a best corrected visual acuity (BCVA) ≥ 0.8 were recruited. Pregnant and lactating women, or women who were currently or previously using oral contraceptives or hormone replacement therapy (within 6 months prior to enrollment) were excluded. We also excluded subjects with a history of systemic or local disease that could affect ONH or retinal perfusion, such as diabetes mellitus, hypertension, cardiac disease, glaucoma patients, or suspects (either open or closed angle), anemia, or any neurological disease. We also excluded candidates with a history of previous ocular surgery or refractive myopia greater than − 6 diopters or hyperopia greater than + 4 diopters. In addition, those with any media opacity not allowing good fundus visualization and OCTA imaging were excluded. Patients were divided into two groups: Group 1 (Premenopausal): 50 eyes of 50 patients while group 2 (Postmenopausal): 50 eyes of 50 patients. The eyes of all participants were evaluated for BCVA using Snellen’s chart (in decimals), slit-lamp examination, gonioscopy, IOP measurement using Goldman applanation tonometry, dilated fundus examination by binocular indirect slit-lamp biomicroscopy, detection of serum estrogen level (in the mid-follicular phase of the cycle in the premenopausal group) and finally SD-OCT and OCTA (Optovue Inc., Fremont, CA, USA), using split-spectrum amplitude-decorrelation algorithm. Each image set comprised two raster volumetric patterns (one with vertical priority and one with horizontal priority) that scanned an area of 4.5 × 4.5 mm centered on ONH to assess RNFLT and VD in the peripapillary area. Each volume comprised 216 line-scan locations at which five consecutive B-scans were obtained. Each B-scan contained 216 A-scans, which compared the consecutive B-scans at the same location to detect flow based on motion contrast. An En-face angiogram was obtained using the maximum flow (decorrelation value) projection. The OCTA images were co-registered with OCT B-scans that were obtained concurrently to enable visualization of both the vasculature and structure in tandem. Analysis of OCT A and OCTA Images The built-in Angio Analytics software was used to evaluate VD and RNFLT. The software defines the peripapillary region as a 1.0 mm wide round annulus extending from the optic disc boundary. The disc margin is automatically detected based on Bruch’s Membrane Opening (BMO), and both cup and rim are measured within the BMO plane. The ONH map protocol was used to obtain RNFLT measurements which were calculated in a 10 pixel-wide band along a circle of 3.45 mm in diameter centered on the ONH. The overall average RNFLT as well as that in the superior, inferior, nasal, and temporal quadrants were used. Peripapillary VD was defined as the percentage of the area occupied by the vessels in the peripapillary region. The software calculated the whole image vessel density (wiVD) in the entire 4.5 × 4.5 mm 2 image. Overall peripapillary VD, as well as the VD measurements of the 4 quadrants, were calculated. Color maps were also used to show the VD. All OCTA scans were evaluated for image quality and segmentation errors. Quality index lower than 6 or images with persistent motion artifacts as doubled vessel pictures and artifact lines were excluded. If both eyes were eligible, a random blinded selection was done. In case of high refractive error or media opacity hindering the OCT scanning (e.g., dense cataract, nebulous cornea), the other eye was chosen. Statistical analysis Data was coded and entered using the statistical package for the Social Sciences (SPSS) version 26 (IBM Corp., Armonk, NY, USA). Data was summarized using mean and standard deviation. Comparisons between groups were done using the unpaired t test 7 . Correlations between quantitative variables were done using the Pearson correlation coefficient. Multivariate stepwise linear regression analysis was done to detect if age, estrogen and IOP act as independent predictors of densities 8 . P -value less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant . Ethical approval This report was approved by the ethical committee of at Cairo University and followed the tenets of the Declaration of Helsinki. Consent to participate All participants received oral and written consents as mentioned in methodology.

[results] Results Demographic and clinical characteristics One hundred eyes of one hundred patients were enrolled in the study divided into fifty eyes into each of the premenopausal and postmenopausal groups. The Patients’ age ranged from 41 to 48 years with a mean age of 43.22 ± 2.18 years in the premenopausal group and from 52 to 60 years with a mean age of 54.56 ± 3.80 years in the postmenopausal group. The estrogen level was significantly lower in the postmenopausal group. Postmenopausal women also had significantly lower BCVA and higher IOP than the premenopausal ones ( P value < 0.001). Table 1 summarizes the clinical characteristics of the study population. Table 1 Demographic and clinical characteristics of the study population. Premenopausal group Postmenopausal group P value Mean Standard deviation Mean Standard deviation Age (Years) 43.22 2.18 54.56 3.80 < 0.001 BCVA (Decimals) 0.94 0.06 0.81 0.07 < 0.001 CCT (um) 577.92 27.22 506.46 14.27 < 0.001 SE (diopters) 0.21 1.05 − 0.01 1.54 0.203 IOP (mmHg) 13.54 1.18 18.18 1.66 < 0.001 VCDR 0.31 0.11 0.32 0.09 0.351 Estrogen (pg/ml) 232.98 8.30 17.24 2.99 < 0.001 BCVA Best corrected visual acuity, CCT central corneal thickness, SE spherical equivalent, IOP Intraocular pressure, VCDR vertical cup to disc ratio. Retinal nerve fiber layer thickness The RNFLT was analyzed in the four quadrants (superior, inferior, nasal, and temporal). The mean RNFLT was significantly thinner in the postmenopausal women (Fig. 1 A) in all quadrants compared to the premenopausal ones (Fig. 1 B) ( P value < 0.001) as summarized in Table 2 . Figure 1 Example of RNFLT maps in a postmenopausal and a premenopausal participant. Table 2 Comparison between C in both groups. Premenopausal group Postmenopausal group P value Mean Standard deviation Mean Standard deviation Superior NFLT (um) 128.28 12.90 113.58 5.20 < 0.001 Inferior NFLT (um) 129.12 13.21 110.78 11.85 < 0.001 Nasal NFLT (um) 85.66 7.08 73.22 9.49 < 0.001 Temporal NFLT (um) 76.58 7.42 66.44 7.80 < 0.001 RNFLT Retinal nerve fiber layer thickness. Peripapillary vessel density (VD) The average of all peripapillary VD measurements was significantly lower in the postmenopausal group (Fig. 2 A) (wiVD, inside disc VD, peripapillary VD, superior and inferior hemi) and in the 4 quadrants (superior, inferior, nasal, and temporal) compared to the premenopausal group (Fig. 2 B) ( P value < 0.001) as summarized in Table 3 . Figure 2 Example of a peripapillary OCTA scan 4.5 × 4.5 mm of ONH at Vitreous/Retinal level, RPC level, and Choroidal level, RPC density and thickness in a postmenopausal and a premenopausal participant. Table 3 Comparison between vessel denisty in both groups. Premenopausal group Postmenopausal group P value VD (%) Mean Standard deviation Mean Standard deviation Wi VD (%) 51.55 1.98 48.80 2.72 < 0.001 Inside disc VD (%) 50.88 3.44 46.10 4.77 < 0.001 Peripapillary VD (%) 53.74 2.62 50.36 2.58 < 0.001 Superior hemi VD (%) 53.52 2.85 50.48 3.62 < 0.001 Inferior hemi VD (%) 53.84 2.86 51.21 2.26 < 0.001 Superior quadrant VD (%) 54.02 3.42 50.38 4.57 < 0.001 Inferior quadrant VD (%) 56.12 3.71 52.48 3.52 < 0.001 Nasal quadrant VD (%) 52.14 3.76 48.26 4.21 < 0.001 Temporal quadrant VD (%) 55.14 2.44 51.34 3.19 < 0.001 VD Vessel density, Wi Whole image. Pearson correlation coefficients of peripapillary VD with RNFLT showed positive correlations in the vascular and structural changes. The correlation was statistically significant between RNFLT and VD in the inferior ( r = 0.516, P < 0.001), nasal ( r = 0.446, P = 0.001) and temporal quadrants ( r = 0.577, P < 0.001) as shown in Table 4 . Table 4 Correlation between RNFLT and VD in the postmenopausal group. Postmenopausal group Superior RNFLT Inferior RNFLT Nasal RNFLT Temporal RNFLT Wi VD (%) r 0.031 0.302 0.425 0.495 P value 0.832 0.033 0.002 < 0.001 Inside disc VD (%) r -0.257- -0.038- 0.227 0.240 P value 0.072 0.796 0.114 0.094 Peripapillary VD (%) r 0.016 0.094 0.516 0.510 P value 0.912 0.515 < 0.001 < 0.001 Superior quadrant VD (%) r -0.099- 0.006 0.470 0.537 P value 0.495 0.967 0.001 < 0.001 Inferior quadrant VD (%) r 0.259 0.516 0.038 0.104 P value 0.070 < 0.001 0.792 0.474 Nasal quadrant VD (%) r 0.018 0.035 0.446 0.361 P value 0.901 0.807 0.001 0.010 Temporal quadrant VD (%) r -0.097- 0.168 0.426 0.577 P value 0.502 0.242 0.002 < 0.001 Significant values are in [bold]. RNFLT Retinal nerve fiber layer thickness, Wi VD Whole image vessel density, VD Vessel density, r Linear correlation coefficient. Correlation between IOP, estrogen and RNFLT and VD We studied the relation of IOP and estrogen with all parameters in the post-menopausal group. Analysis of Pearson correlation coefficients of the RNFLT with IOP showed a statistically significant negative correlation between IOP and RNFLT in the inferior quadrant only (r = − 0.318, P value 0.024) (Fig. 3 ). Figure 3 Correlation of IOP with inferior RNFLT and inferior VD in the postmenopausal group. Similarly, Pearson correlation coefficients of the VD with IOP revealed a significant negative correlation between IOP and VD in the inferior quadrant (r = − 0.541, P value < 0.001) (Fig. 3 ). Analysis of the relation between the estrogen level and RNFLT and VD showed that there was a statistically significant positive correlation with nasal (r = 0.286, P value 0.044) and temporal RNFLT (r = 0.287, P value 0.043) and with peripapillary VD (r = 0.257, P value 0.029) and superior VD (r = 0.340, P value 0.016). Multivariate stepwise linear regression analysis was done, with age, estrogen level and IOP (as independent predictors) and vessel density (as dependent variable). Results in Table 5 showed that estrogen level is an independent predictor for whole image, peripapillary superior and temporal VD affection. IOP has been shown to be a predictor of inferior VD affection. [discussion] Discussion Postmenopausal drop in estrogen level is thought to play an important role in increasing the incidence of ocular symptoms and ocular diseases 2 . In this study, we evaluated the structural and vascular changes of the ONH in postmenopausal women in comparison to premenopausal women. We found that the difference in the mean IOP was statistically significant between both groups with higher IOP in the postmenopausal group. This agreed well with the results of a study conducted by Siuw et al. 9 who found a significant higher IOP in the postmenopausal group, and estradiol was shown to be a protective factor in reducing IOP among these women . Regarding the optic nerve changes, older studies support the hypothesis that estrogen deficiency is involved in the pathophysiology of optic nerve aging and glaucomatous neurodegeneration through several mechanisms 6 , 9 . In this study, we found that the difference in RNFLT in the 4 quadrants was statistically significant between pre and postmenopausal groups. And by correlating the IOP with RNFLT in the different quadrants in the postmenopausal group, we found a statistically significant negative correlation between IOP and RNFLT in the inferior quadrant. Deschênes et al. in 2010 found that the measures of ONH topography indicated a significantly thicker RNFL in menopausal women on Hormonal replacement therapy (HRT) compared to women who never used HRT 3 . Comparably, Açmaz et al. 6 in 2014 evaluated the RNFLT and choroidal thickness in premenopausal and postmenopausal women where no significant difference was found between the postmenopausal study and control groups regarding all the peripapillary RNFLT thickness parameters . We found that all the average measurements of VDs are significantly lower in the postmenopausal group with a negative correlation between IOP and inferior hemi, and inferior quadrant only. No previous studies reported using OCTA to evaluate the peripapillary vasculature to compare with. Yet, others used different methods to predict ocular blood flow. As an example, Centofanti et al. in 2002 studied the pulsatile ocular blood flow (POBF) and have revealed gender and hormonal status-related alterations in the choroidal circulation 10 , 11 . We found a statistically positive correlation between the loss of VDs and RNFLT in 3 out of the 4 quadrants. This agreed with previous studies stating that the radial peripapillary capillary densities correlated significantly with the RNFL thickness 12 , 13 . We then compared the changes of RNFLT and VD with IOP. We found that with hormonal drop, increasing age and higher IOP, there was a decline in both RNFLT and peripapillary VD, especially in the inferior quadrant; but VD showed more significant negative correlation than RNFLT with IOP. So, according to this study, we can consider the assessment of VD by OCTA to be more sensitive in the early detection of peripapillary changes if IOP rises with menopause. This agreed with Lee et al. 14 who reported that the inferior VD showed better diagnostic ability than most of the other OCT measurements including peripapillary RNFLT and macular ganglion cell inner plexiform layer (GCIPL) thickness in glaucomatous highly myopic eyes. To our knowledge, this is the first study to elaborate the peripapillary vascular changes in menopausal women and correlate it with RNFLT and IOP. The limitations of this study are the lack of a group diagnosed with glaucoma to compare the changes with them. Axial length measurement would have been of additional asset to exclude changes caused by over stretching; but that could be forgiven since we excluded women with high refractive errors from the start. Also, it would have been of great value if we could follow up the premenopausal women to detect the changes that occur to them after menopause; but this needs a longer study duration.

retrieved_papers.title: Changes in macular microvasculature after Nd:YAG laser capsulotomy: an OCT-A study.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] To investigate microvascular changes in the retina and choriocapillaris using optical coherence tomography angiography (OCT-A) following neodymium:yttrium-aluminium-garnet (Nd:YAG) laser capsulotomy for posterior capsule opacification (PCO), a delayed finding that can be commonly observed after cataract surgery.
Thirty-four eyes of 34 patients undergoing Nd:YAG laser capsulotomy were included. Comprehensive ophthalmologic examination and OCT-A imaging were performed before, one week, and one month after the procedure. Retinal thickness, ganglion cell layer (GCL), retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL), and vessel densities in the superficial capillary plexus (SCP), deep capillary plexus (DCP), and choriocapillaris (CC) were evaluated.
Following laser treatment, anterior chamber depth (ACD) increased, the foveal avascular zone (FAZ) enlarged, and vessel densities in the central regions of both the SCP and DCP decreased significantly (p = 0.002, p < 0.001, p < 0.001, and p < 0.001, respectively). Additional reductions were also observed in some parafoveal and CC quadrants (Table 4). No statistically significant changes were observed in intraocular pressure (IOP), spherical equivalent (SE), axial length (AL), macular thickness, or choroidal thickness (all p > 0.05).
Our findings indicate that retinal blood flow and vessel density may decrease following Nd:YAG laser capsulotomy; however, the clinical significance of these microvascular changes remains unclear. OCT-A appears to be a promising, non-invasive modality for monitoring retinal microcirculation in patients undergoing this procedure. Nonetheless, further prospective and longitudinal studies are necessary to clarify the long-term consequences and potential functional outcomes of these vascular alterations.

retrieved_papers.title: Retinal and Choroidal Alterations in Heart Failure: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of OCT and OCT-A Findings with Emphasis on HFrEF.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] Retina and choroid, given their high metabolic demands, are vulnerable to ischemic injury during the hemodynamic instability associated with heart failure (HF). Despite this, posterior segment ocular changes in HF remain understudied. This systematic review and meta-analysis consolidates optical coherence tomography (OCT) and OCT angiography (OCT-A) findings in HF.
We conducted a comprehensive search of Web of Science, PubMed/MEDLINE, and EMBASE on March 15, 2025. A random-effects meta-analysis of six studies was performed using Hedges' g to estimate effect sizes, with six additional studies included for qualitative synthesis (total n = 1139 eyes). The meta-analysis was limited to studies on heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), as this was the only heart failure subtype with sufficient data for quantitative analysis.
Key findings revealed significant reductions in parafoveal superficial capillary plexus vessel density (SCP VD) (Hedges' g = - 0.42, 95% CI - 0.78 to - 0.05, p = 0.03) and subfoveal choroidal thickness (Hedges' g = - 0.75, 95% CI - 1.31 to - 0.18, p = 0.01) in patients with HFrEF. Furthermore, severity-dependent thinning of retinal nerve fiber layer and significant correlations between whole macular vessel density and both left ventricular EF and New York Heart Association functional class emphasize the potential of these parameters in stratifying disease severity. Additionally, one study revealed thinning of ganglion cell-inner plexiform layer in patients with HF with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF). Those with acute decompensated HF showed macular VD attenuation in superficial layers and decreased subfoveal choroidal thickness. Pediatric cases demonstrated central macular thinning, selective decreases in SCP VD, and choroidal thinning.
Collectively, OCT and OCT-A metrics provide useful information regarding systemic microvascular dysfunction, suggesting potential for disease detection and improved risk stratification. However, it is important to note that these findings are based on observational studies, which limits the ability to draw definitive causal conclusions. This highlights the need for prospective, longitudinal research to establish temporal relationships and validate these observations.

[Introduction] Introduction Heart failure (HF) is a progressive clinical syndrome characterized by systemic hypoperfusion and multiorgan dysfunction due to impaired cardiac output and/or elevated filling pressures. Among the organs affected by this microcirculatory disturbance, the eye, particularly the retina and choroid, serves as a unique window into systemic vascular health [ 1 , 2 ]. The retinal circulation, supplied by both the central retinal artery and the choroidal vasculature, is exquisitely sensitive to perfusion deficits and endothelial dysfunction [ 3 ]. Given its exceptionally high metabolic demand, the retina is especially vulnerable to ischemic injury during hemodynamic instability in HF [ 4 ]. In HF, systemic fluctuations in blood flow, including to the retina and choroid, can lead to intermittent hypoxia and nutrient deprivation. Subsequent reperfusion, while necessary for oxygen delivery and tissue survival, paradoxically exacerbates damage through ischemia–reperfusion injury, ultimately triggering apoptotic cell death in retinal neurons and vasculature [ 5 , 6 ]. These mechanisms may underlie the structural and microvascular alterations of retina and choroid observed in patients with HF. Advances in optical coherence tomography (OCT) and OCT angiography (OCT-A) have revolutionized in-depth analysis of retinal and choroidal integrity, enabling detailed evaluation of layer thickness, microvascular density, and perfusion patterns [ 7 – 10 ]. Growing evidence suggests that these changes correlate with HF severity and may serve as prognostic biomarkers [ 6 , 11 ]. Notably, both HF with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) and preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) exhibit ocular microvascular remodeling, though their distinct hemodynamic profiles may differentially affect posterior segment morphology [ 12 , 13 ]. While most studies focus on chronic HF, acute decompensated HF also induces transient but detectable alterations [ 14 ]. Pediatric HF, though less studied, demonstrates similar microvascular perturbations, highlighting the systemic nature of HF-related endothelial dysfunction [ 4 , 5 , 15 ]. Although posterior segment alterations have been well documented in conditions affecting systemic vascular health like coronary artery disease [ 16 – 18 ], diabetes [ 19 ], and hypertension [ 20 ], HF-specific involvement remains understudied. Retinal and choroidal imaging may offer a noninvasive, accessible approach to aid in early HF detection, disease stratification, and progression monitoring. This systematic review synthesizes current evidence on posterior segment structural and microvascular changes in HF, primarily focusing on adults with chronic HFrEF compared to healthy controls. Additionally, we examine findings in HFpEF, acute HF, and pediatric populations, and discuss the potential clinical utility of ocular imaging in HF management.

[introduction] Introduction Heart failure (HF) is a progressive clinical syndrome characterized by systemic hypoperfusion and multiorgan dysfunction due to impaired cardiac output and/or elevated filling pressures. Among the organs affected by this microcirculatory disturbance, the eye, particularly the retina and choroid, serves as a unique window into systemic vascular health [ 1 , 2 ]. The retinal circulation, supplied by both the central retinal artery and the choroidal vasculature, is exquisitely sensitive to perfusion deficits and endothelial dysfunction [ 3 ]. Given its exceptionally high metabolic demand, the retina is especially vulnerable to ischemic injury during hemodynamic instability in HF [ 4 ]. In HF, systemic fluctuations in blood flow, including to the retina and choroid, can lead to intermittent hypoxia and nutrient deprivation. Subsequent reperfusion, while necessary for oxygen delivery and tissue survival, paradoxically exacerbates damage through ischemia–reperfusion injury, ultimately triggering apoptotic cell death in retinal neurons and vasculature [ 5 , 6 ]. These mechanisms may underlie the structural and microvascular alterations of retina and choroid observed in patients with HF. Advances in optical coherence tomography (OCT) and OCT angiography (OCT-A) have revolutionized in-depth analysis of retinal and choroidal integrity, enabling detailed evaluation of layer thickness, microvascular density, and perfusion patterns [ 7 – 10 ]. Growing evidence suggests that these changes correlate with HF severity and may serve as prognostic biomarkers [ 6 , 11 ]. Notably, both HF with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) and preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) exhibit ocular microvascular remodeling, though their distinct hemodynamic profiles may differentially affect posterior segment morphology [ 12 , 13 ]. While most studies focus on chronic HF, acute decompensated HF also induces transient but detectable alterations [ 14 ]. Pediatric HF, though less studied, demonstrates similar microvascular perturbations, highlighting the systemic nature of HF-related endothelial dysfunction [ 4 , 5 , 15 ]. Although posterior segment alterations have been well documented in conditions affecting systemic vascular health like coronary artery disease [ 16 – 18 ], diabetes [ 19 ], and hypertension [ 20 ], HF-specific involvement remains understudied. Retinal and choroidal imaging may offer a noninvasive, accessible approach to aid in early HF detection, disease stratification, and progression monitoring. This systematic review synthesizes current evidence on posterior segment structural and microvascular changes in HF, primarily focusing on adults with chronic HFrEF compared to healthy controls. Additionally, we examine findings in HFpEF, acute HF, and pediatric populations, and discuss the potential clinical utility of ocular imaging in HF management.

[methods] Methods This systematic review was designed and executed following the established Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines [ 21 ]. Prior to commencement, the research protocol was registered with International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO) (Registration ID CRD420251018334). This article is based on previously conducted studies and does not contain any new studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors. Search Strategy A comprehensive electronic search was conducted across three major databases (Web of Science, EMBASE, and PubMed/MEDLINE) on March 15, 2025. An additional search of gray literature was conducted using Google Scholar. The search strategy aimed to explore all relevant studies investigating structural and microvascular changes in the retina and choroid of patients with HF using OCT or OCT-A imaging modalities. The search terms combined “heart failure” with either “optical coherence tomography” or “optical coherence tomography angiography”. The complete search algorithms for each database are available in Supplementary Material. To ensure no potentially relevant studies were missed, we implemented backward and forward citation tracking methods. No limitations were applied regarding publication date or country of origin. Eligible study designs included case–control and cross-sectional studies, while we excluded experimental studies (in vitro and in vivo), editorial correspondence, case reports, case series, conference abstracts, and review articles. All identified records were compiled in EndNote 20, where duplicate removal was performed through both automated functions and manual verification by two independent researchers (KB and PP). Any disagreements were resolved through consensus discussion. Study Selection and Eligibility Criteria Two authors (KB and PP) independently screened the articles to determine their eligibility, with any disagreements resolved through discussion. Studies were included if they (1) were original research published in English; (2) involved human participants; (3) examined patients with HF without coexisting ocular pathology; and (4) included a healthy control group without ocular or systemic disorders. Notably, we did not restrict inclusion on the basis of HF duration or severity. While six studies were excluded from quantitative synthesis, they were retained for qualitative analysis. These comprised investigations of special populations (pediatric HF [ 4 , 5 , 15 ], HFpEF [ 12 ], acute decompensated HF [ 14 ]) and one study reporting inconsistent OCT measurements [ 11 ]. The meta-analysis was limited to studies on HFrEF, as this was the only heart failure subtype with sufficient data for quantitative analysis. The complete study selection process is depicted in the PRISMA flowchart (Fig. 1 ). Fig. 1 PRISMA flowchart Data Extraction Two investigators (KB and PP) independently extracted data using a standardized form that captured (1) study characteristics (first author, publication year, geographic origin, study design); (2) participant demographics (e.g., age, gender), sample size, and eye selection method; (3) clinical parameters [medical history, body mass index, blood pressure, HF etiology, New York Heart Association (NYHA) classification, left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF)]; (4) ophthalmic examination results (visual acuity, axial length, intraocular pressure); (5) manufacturer and model of the OCT/OCT-A device; and (6) quantitative OCT/OCT-A parameters. Any discrepancies in data extraction were settled through joint review and discussion. Risk of Bias Assessment Study quality was assessed using the Newcastle–Ottawa Scale [ 22 ], which evaluates three key domains: selection, comparability, and exposure/outcome. Two reviewers (KB, PP) independently scored each study, with any discrepancies resolved through consensus meetings. All studies received scores of at least 7 (Supplementary Material). Statistical Analysis We conducted a random-effects model meta-analysis using Hedges’ g as the effect size measure, with restricted maximum likelihood estimation for variance components. Heterogeneity was quantified using tau-squared ( τ 2 ) and I -squared ( I 2 ) statistics, with Cochran’s Q test ( p < 0.05) indicating statistical significance. A Galbraith plot was generated to visualize individual study contributions to heterogeneity. Sensitivity analysis was conducted by sequentially excluding each study and observing the impact on pooled estimates. Publication bias was assessed using multiple approaches: Egger’s regression test, Begg’s rank correlation test, and visual inspection of funnel plot symmetry. All analyses were performed using Stata software (version 17), with statistical significance set at p < 0.05.

[results] Results Study Selection A systematic Literature search identified 1320 potential studies (99 from PubMed, 1091 from EMBASE, and 130 from Web of Science). After removal of 181 duplicate records, 1139 articles underwent title and abstract screening, of which 1128 were excluded for irrelevance. Full-text assessment of the remaining 11 articles revealed that six studies could not be included in the meta-analysis because of (1) pediatric populations [ 4 , 5 , 15 ], (2) patients with HFpEF [ 12 ], (3) acutely decompensated HF [ 14 ], and (4) inconsistent measurements [ 11 ]. However, these six studies were retained for qualitative synthesis to ensure a comprehensive evaluation of disease manifestations across distinct patient populations. Additionally, backward citation tracking yielded one eligible study, which was included in the meta-analysis [ 23 ]. The study ultimately incorporated 574 eyes from patients with HF and 565 eyes from healthy controls, totaling 1139 eyes. Figure 1 presents selection process. Characteristics of Studies Among the 12 included studies in this systematic review, four used a case–control [ 11 , 14 , 15 , 24 ] approach and eight employed a cross-sectional design [ 4 – 6 , 12 , 13 , 23 , 25 , 26 ]. The research spanned multiple regions, with contributions from Turkey (five studies) [ 6 , 11 , 23 – 25 ], followed by Poland (three studies) [ 4 , 5 , 15 ], Germany (two studies) [ 14 , 26 ], and single studies from the Netherlands [ 12 ] and Iran [ 13 ]. The primary etiology of HF was dilated cardiomyopathy in five studies [ 4 , 5 , 15 , 23 , 24 ], while three studies included patients with both ischemic and dilated cardiomyopathy [ 13 , 14 , 25 ]; the remaining studies did not specify the etiology. LVEF inclusion criteria varied: < 40% (two studies) [ 13 , 23 ], < 50% (four studies) [ 6 , 11 , 24 , 26 ], ≤ 55% (four studies) [ 4 , 5 , 15 , 25 ]. One study did not specify an LVEF cutoff [ 14 ] and another specifically included patients with HFpEF (LVEF ≥ 50%) [ 12 ]. Seven studies assessed one eye per patient [ 6 , 11 , 14 , 23 – 26 ], four included both eyes [ 4 , 5 , 13 , 15 ], and one used a mixed approach [ 12 ]. Controls were age- and gender-matched in ten studies [ 4 – 6 , 13 – 15 , 23 – 26 ], age-matched only in one [ 11 ], and unmatched in one [ 12 ]. OCT devices varied across studies: six used RTVue XR Avanti Optovue [ 4 , 5 , 13 , 15 , 24 , 26 ], while others employed DRI Triton Topcon [ 11 ], Heidelberg [ 23 , 25 ], Nikon RS-3000 [ 6 ], Zeiss Cirrus [ 14 ], and Zeiss PLEX Elite 9000 [ 12 ]. OCT imaging assessed multiple structural and vascular parameters. Macular thickness assessments covered central [ 4 , 12 – 14 , 23 ], parafoveal [ 4 ], and whole macular thickness [ 4 ]. Vascular evaluations included foveal avascular zone (FAZ) area [ 4 , 12 , 24 , 26 ], perimeter, and circularity index [ 12 ], as well as macular vessel density in the fovea [ 4 , 11 – 14 ], parafovea [ 4 , 11 , 13 , 14 , 24 , 26 ], perifovea [ 13 , 14 ], and whole macula [ 4 , 13 , 14 , 26 ]. Ganglion cell complex (GCC) parameters comprised average, superior, and inferior thickness, ganglion cell inner plexiform layer (GCIPL) thickness [ 12 ], and focal/global GCC volume loss [ 5 ]. Choroidal measurements included general thickness [ 11 ], subfoveal thickness (SFCT) [ 6 , 13 – 15 , 23 , 25 ], and thickness at 1500-μm intervals from the fovea (superior, inferior, temporal, nasal) [ 15 ], along with choroidal vascularity index (CVI) and choriocapillaris flow [ 13 ]. Retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) measurements included average thickness and quadrant-specific (superior, inferior, temporal, nasal) assessments [ 6 ]. Radial peripapillary capillary (RPC) density was evaluated for mean [ 24 , 26 ] and regional distributions (superior/inferior hemi, superotemporal/nasal, nasosuperior/inferior, inferonasal/temporal, temporoinferior/superior) [ 24 ]. Additional parameters included total retinal volume [ 12 ] and superotemporal retinal vein/artery diameters [ 14 ]. A detailed summary of study characteristics is provided in Table 1 . Table 1 Study characteristics Study Country OCT device Eyes (no.) Gender (F/M) Age (years) Case blood pressure (mmHg) LVEF (%) Case Control Case Control Case Control Systolic Diastolic Case Quantitative Khalilipur, 2023 Iran Optovue 52 64 6/20 12/20 53 ± 13 51 ± 15 112 ± 13 76 ± 12 25 ± 13 Alnawaiseh, 2021 Germany Optovue 27 31 – – 54.56 ± 10.64 52.1 ± 12.23 – – 39.56 ± 6.81 Alkan, 2023 Turkey Optovue 48 50 15/33 17/33 43.43 ± 10.24 45.32 ± 10.58 116.97 ± 9.38 75.20 ± 9.72 30.00 ± 7.57 Altinkaynak, 2014 Turkey Heidelberg 56 56 30/26 27/29 53.6 ± 1.49 53.4 ± 2.41 110 ± 11.1 80.1 ± 10.3 37.5 ± 11.50 Kanar, 2020 Turkey Nikon 72 40 – 25/15 63.0 ± 3.1 64.1 ± 2.8 110.7 ± 11.3 82.3 ± 10.3 – Alur, 2019 Turkey Heidelberg 44 44 10/34 10/34 59.3 ± 12.4 59.3 ± 8.5 118.6 ± 19.9 74.3 ± 13.8 – Qualitative Weerts, 2024 Netherlands ZEISS PLEX 22 24 16/6 10/14 74 [68–80] 68 [58–77] 141 [124–151] 81 [66–89] 62 [58–66] Abdin, 2024 Germany Cirrus/Heidelberg 40 40 13/27 14/26 78.9 ± 8.76 77.8 ± 8.7 – – 43 ± 12.8 Rakusiewicz, 2021 Poland Optovue 60 60 14/16 14/16 9.9 ± 3.57 10.16 ± 3.42 – – 49.03 ± 6.63 Rakusiewicz, 2020 Poland Optovue 60 60 14/16 14/16 9.9 ± 3.57 10.08 ± 3.41 – – 49.03 ± 6.63 Rakusiewicz, 2021 Poland Optovue 60 60 14/16 14/16 9.9 ± 3.57 11.27 ± 3.33 – – 49.03 ± 6.63 Topaloglu, 2023 Turkey Triton 33 36 15/18 16/20 73.66 ± 5.37 67.88 ± 3.94 – – – OCT optical coherence tomography, F female, M male, LVEF left ventricular ejection fraction Data Synthesis Adults with Chronic HFrEF Pooled Effect Size Estimates Figure 2 illustrates the comparison of OCT and OCT-A parameters between patients with HF and healthy controls where data pooling was feasible. The analysis demonstrated significantly lower SFCT values in patients with HF compared to controls (Hedges’ g = − 0.75, 95% CI − 1.31, − 0.18, p = 0.01), with high heterogeneity ( I 2 = 86.56%, p < 0.001). Parafoveal superficial capillary plexus (SCP) vessel density (VD) was also significantly reduced (Hedges’ g = − 0.42, 95% CI − 0.78 to − 0.05, p = 0.03), though without significant heterogeneity ( I 2 = 55.97%, p = 0.11). In contrast, parafoveal deep capillary plexus (DCP) VD did not differ significantly between groups (Hedges’ g = − 0.62, 95% CI − 1.68 to 0.43, p = 0.25), despite high heterogeneity ( I 2 = 94.17%, p < 0.001). Fig. 2 Forest plots depicting retinal and choroidal structural and vascular changes in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction compared to healthy controls. SFCT subfoveal choroidal thickness, SCP superficial capillary plexus, DCP deep capillary plexus Sensitivity Analysis Sensitivity analyses assessed the robustness of these findings. Exclusion of studies by Khalilipur et al. [ 13 ] and Alkan et al. [ 24 ] rendered parafoveal SCP VD differences nonsignificant. Furthermore, after the studies by Khalilipur et al. [ 13 ] and Kanar et al. [ 6 ] were excluded, the observed differences in SFCT were no longer statistically significant. However, for parafoveal DCP VD, no individual study exclusion altered the overall effect sizes. Visual depictions of these analytical findings are presented in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3 Sensitivity analyses of retinal and choroidal structural and vascular changes in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction compared to healthy controls. SFCT subfoveal choroidal thickness, SCP superficial capillary plexus, DCP deep capillary plexus Heterogeneity We assessed between-study heterogeneity using Galbraith plots, which graphically display variation in effect sizes across included studies. For all OCT and OCT-A parameters, the plots demonstrated low heterogeneity when comparing patients with HF to healthy controls (Fig. 4 ). Fig. 4 Galbraith plots of retinal and choroidal structural and vascular changes in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction compared to healthy controls. SFCT subfoveal choroidal thickness, SCP superficial capillary plexus, DCP deep capillary plexus Publication Bias To comprehensively evaluate potential publication bias, we employed a multimodal analytical approach. First, funnel plots were systematically examined for visual asymmetry. Second, we conducted formal statistical assessments using Egger’s regression test and Begg’s rank correlation test to quantitatively measure bias. In our comparative analysis of patients with HF versus healthy controls, all three methodologies consistently demonstrated no significant evidence of publication bias (all p values > 0.05) (Fig. 5 and Supplementary Material). Fig. 5 Funnel plots of retinal and choroidal structural and vascular changes in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction compared to healthy controls. SFCT subfoveal choroidal thickness, SCP superficial capillary plexus, DCP deep capillary plexus Meta-regression To explore potential confounders, we performed a meta-regression, limited by available data to the relationship between systemic blood pressure and SFCT. Systolic blood pressure (SBP) was a significant moderator ( p = 0.022), explaining R 2 = 67.93% of the between-study variance. By contrast, diastolic blood pressure was not significant ( p = 0.197) and accounted for a smaller share of heterogeneity ( R 2 = 22.05%). These results suggest that differences in SBP across studies may contribute to observed variability in SFCT (Table 2 ). Notably, the population in the study by Alur et al. [ 23 ] had the highest SBP, which may explain why it was the only study that did not observe a significant reduction in SFCT among patients with HF. This also likely accounts for the loss of significance in the pooled estimate during sensitivity analyses, as sequential exclusion of other studies increased the relative influence of Alur et al.’s findings. Table 2 Meta-regression of subfoveal choroidal thickness in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction compared to healthy controls Subfoveal choroidal thickness Systolic blood pressure p value 0.022 β 0.125 R 2 (%) 67.93 Diastolic blood pressure p value 0.197 β − 0.106 R 2 (%) 22.05 Importantly, all studies included in our meta-analysis excluded participants with diabetes mellitus; therefore, diabetes could not have acted as a confounding factor for the observed changes. This exclusion reduces the likelihood that diabetic microvascular disease explains the observed associations. Qualitative Evidence Synthesis Central macular thickness (CMT) was comparable between cases and controls, a result that persisted after age/sex adjustment [ 13 , 23 ]. Whole en face SCP and DCP VD were comparable between groups [ 13 , 26 ], as were foveal and perifoveal SCP/DCP VD [ 13 ]. However, parafoveal DCP VD for superior/inferior hemi and all quadrants (superior, temporal, inferior, and nasal) was lower in HF, while parafoveal SCP VD in these same regions showed no significant difference [ 24 ]. Confirming these trends, Topaloglu et al. reported similar findings: comparable SCP VD but significantly reduced DCP VD in patients with HF. Additionally, they observed lower VD in both SCP and DCP when comparing NYHA class III versus class II patients, suggesting the potential utility of OCTA metrics as objective prognostic indicators for assessing heart failure progression [ 11 ]. Concordantly, macular flow density correlated with NYHA class and LVEF, in another study [ 26 ]. FAZ area findings were inconsistent: one study reported larger FAZ area in HF [ 24 ], while another found no difference in the superficial layer [ 26 ]. FAZ area did not correlate with LVEF [ 26 ]. Topaloglu et al. measured choroidal thickness but did not specify whether their measurements included SFCT; consequently, we excluded their data from our meta-analysis. Nevertheless, their findings align with our meta-analysis results, showing significantly reduced choroidal thickness in NYHA class II and III patients with HF compared to controls. Additionally, they observed no significant difference between NYHA II and III patients [ 11 ]. Two studies demonstrated significant correlations between SFCT and both age and LVEF [ 6 , 25 ], whereas two others observed no correlation between SFCT and LVEF [ 13 , 23 ]. Additionally, these studies found no significant correlations between SFCT and axial length, intraocular pressure, or systolic and diastolic blood pressure [ 13 , 25 ]. Choriocapillaris flow and CVI did not differ between groups, even after age/sex adjustment [ 13 ]. CVI was unrelated to systolic/diastolic blood pressure or LVEF [ 13 ]. RNFL thickness, including average, superior, inferior, nasal, and temporal quadrants, was significantly reduced in patients with HF with LVEF < 30% compared to both patients with HF with LVEF 30–50% and healthy controls. However, no significant difference was observed between patients with HF with LVEF 30–50% and controls. Notably, while RNFL thickness demonstrated a significant correlation with age, it showed no association with LVEF values in the HF cohort [ 6 ]. In the study by Alkan et al., RPC density was significantly lower in patients with HF across all regions except the superotemporal area [ 24 ]. Similarly, Alnawaiseh et al. reported reduced optic nerve head flow density in HF, unrelated to NYHA class [ 26 ]. Notably, Kanar et al. identified SFCT < 250 μm and RNFL < 97 μm as discriminative parameters for HF [ 6 ]. Adults with HFpEF Patients with HFpEF showed reduced GCIPL thickness and total retinal volume, though left eye measurements alone showed no significant difference. Similarly, macular VD was reduced in patients with HFpEF, with this finding remaining unchanged when data were stratified by right and left eyes. In contrast, average central retinal thickness, central retinal perfusion density, and all FAZ metrics (perimeter, area, and circularity index) showed no significant differences between groups in either combined or eye-specific analyses [ 12 ]. Several significant correlations were identified: (1) GCIPL thickness with both central retinal thickness and total retinal volume; (2) macular VD with FAZ perimeter and circulatory index; (3) total retinal volume with markers of diastolic dysfunction; and (4) specifically in right eyes, FAZ perimeter and area with left atrial volume index [ 12 ]. Adults with Acutely Decompensated HF Abdin et al. examined posterior segment structural and vascular changes during both the initial 1–2 days of acute decompensation and the subsequent recompensation phase after diuretic treatment. Compared to controls, patients with HF exhibited significantly reduced SFCT in both phases, while maintaining similar CMT. During acute decompensation, patients demonstrated larger vessel diameters in both the superior temporal retinal vein and artery relative to controls. The investigators also reported significantly reduced both length and area of perfused vasculature per unit area across all measured regions in superficial layers during acute decompensation: the full scan area, foveal center (1 mm), inner ring (3 mm), and outer ring (6 mm) compared to controls [ 14 ]. Children with Chronic HF Rakusiewicz et al. conducted three studies examining pediatric patients with chronic HF secondary to dilated cardiomyopathy [ 4 , 5 , 15 ]. Their findings demonstrated that average GCC thickness and superior/inferior quadrant measurements showed no significant differences between cases and controls. Both focal and global GCC volume loss were also comparable between groups. These measurements were independent of sex, age, eye laterality refractive error, LVEF, and NT-proBNP levels [ 5 ]. In a subsequent study, they observed significant reductions in whole macular, foveal (1 mm), parafoveal (3 mm) thicknesses, in addition to SCP VD across all regions. By contrast, DCP VD, whether measured across the whole macula or specifically in foveal and parafoveal regions, along with FAZ area in SCP showed no case–control differences. Notably, while foveal thickness and foveal SCP VD correlated with age, and FAZ area correlated with foveal thickness, none of these parameters correlated with LVEF or NT-proBNP levels. Furthermore, neither SCP nor DCP VD showed any correlation with sex or refractive error [ 4 ]. Their choroidal thickness evaluation revealed significantly reduced SFCT and choroidal thickness at all 1500-μm locations (superior, inferior, temporal, and nasal to the fovea) in patients with HF compared to controls. The nasal location showed the thinnest measurements while the subfoveal region remained thickest. Although girls exhibited thicker choroidal measurements than boys, choroidal thickness showed no correlation with gender, age, refractive error, NT-proBNP levels, or LVEF [ 15 ]. Table 3 presents a comprehensive overview of retinal and choroidal structural and microvascular alterations, while Fig. 6 depicts macular microvascular changes across HF subtypes and age groups.
