[discussion] Discussion Patients with HF show ocular microvascular and structural changes, including reduced choroidal thickness and altered retinal vessel density, which may be associated with systemic hemodynamic alterations. These manifestations might reflect interactions between systemic circulation and ocular health and could possibly serve as a tentative diagnostic indicator of cardiac dysfunction, pending further validation. Converging evidence from OCT and OCT-A studies across diverse populations substantiates these mechanistic associations, supporting the utility of these noninvasive imaging modalities as potential surrogate markers for early decompensation detection, disease monitoring, and risk stratification. However, it should be noted that these findings are derived from observational studies, precluding definitive causal or prognostic conclusions. Prospective, longitudinal studies are therefore required to establish temporal relationships. Reduced choroidal thickness emerged as a consistent finding in adults with HFrEF, acutely decompensated HF, and pediatric HF. Supporting this finding, Schuster et al. demonstrated that reduced LVEF, as a cardiovascular risk factor, is significantly associated with choroidal thickness [ 27 ]. Given the choroid’s high metabolic demand [ 28 ], its compensatory capacity to counteract perfusion pressure fluctuations [ 29 – 33 ] appears insufficient to withstand the microvascular changes seen in HF. One proposed mechanism is that low cardiac output in HF reduces systemic blood flow, triggering compensatory vasoconstriction in peripheral vessels, including ocular arteries, to prioritize oxygen delivery to vital organs [ 13 ]. Over time, this may damage choroidal arteries, restrict oxygen and nutrient delivery [ 14 , 15 , 34 ], and induce ischemic changes that promote retinal pigment epithelial atrophy and reduced SFCT [ 25 , 35 – 37 ]. The choroid’s vulnerability stems from its reliance on terminal vascular branches with limited collateral circulation [ 14 ]. Furthermore, elevated central venous pressure may impair venous drainage, causing systemic venous congestion [ 14 ]. This stagnant blood fails to meet oxygen demands and may also exert hydrostatic stress on the choriocapillaris, promoting stromal fluid extravasation and compression of the choroidal stroma. Notably, a single study reported preserved choriocapillaris flow density and CVI in patients with HFrEF, suggesting that both stromal and luminal compartments contribute to HF-related choroidal changes [ 13 ]. While requiring further validation, this observation may underscore HF-specific mechanisms which contrasts with conditions like hypertension or diabetes, where CVI reductions predominate as a result of capillary degeneration [ 38 , 39 ]. One study demonstrated significantly reduced RNFL thickness in patients with severe HFrEF (LVEF < 30%) compared to both healthy controls and moderate HFrEF groups (LVEF 30–50%) [ 6 ]. This reduction in severe cases may result from orbital vessel vasoconstriction secondary to diminished cardiac output and consequent chronic ischemia [ 6 ]. The preserved RNFL observed in moderate HFrEF may reflect compensatory mechanisms which may fail in advanced disease. These findings may indicate potential utility of RNFL measurements as a prognostic marker for HF severity stratification. Regarding VD in the RPC network, both available studies consistently reported decreased values [ 24 , 26 ]. While the underlying pathophysiology remains unclear, some evidence suggests that inadequate blood supply to the optic nerve may contribute to this finding [ 40 , 41 ]. Alternatively, Alkan et al. proposed that technical factors, particularly excessive overlapping of radial capillaries near the optic disc that could have introduced measurement errors, potentially account for this observation [ 24 ]. The observed GCIPL thinning in HFpEF aligns with the microvascular paradigm of its pathophysiology. Retinal hypoxic-ischemic injury can cause retinal ganglion cell (RGC) loss [ 42 – 46 ]. Local renin–angiotensin system (RAS) hyperactivity may impair neurovascular coupling, the process by which light-stimulated retinal neurons release vasoactive signals to dilate nearby vessels and match blood flow to metabolic demand, thereby further contributing to GCIPL thinning [ 47 – 50 ]. Choroidal hypoperfusion may also contribute to photoreceptor degeneration, as it supplies the outer retina [ 51 ]. Preserved GCC thickness in pediatric HF may reflect age-dependent ischemic resilience, as inner retinal layers, though vulnerable to oxidative stress, have stronger adaptive defenses against hypoxia [ 46 , 52 , 53 ]. This resilience may stem from nutrient reserves in the vitreous and retina, which support anaerobic glycolysis in retinal cells to sustain ATP production during hypoxia [ 5 , 54 ]. While CMT remained relatively unchanged in adults with HF across different subtypes, pediatric patients exhibited significant reductions in macular thickness across various regions. This discrepancy may arise from developmental variations in retinal structure or age-dependent differences in vulnerability to hypoxic damage, resulting in an impaired capacity to compensate for hemodynamic fluctuations associated with reduced cardiac output. Compared to healthy controls, the macular microvasculature shows distinct alterations across HF subtypes, potentially influenced by the unique pathophysiological mechanisms and hemodynamic characteristics of each subtype. In HFrEF, reduced cardiac output and tissue hypoperfusion lead to FAZ enlargement and parafoveal SCP VD reduction. These alterations reflect compromised retinal autoregulation, likely due to dysfunctional constriction of terminal retinal vessels [ 4 , 13 ], and ultimately leads to chronic ischemic stress. Peripheral vasoconstriction may further reduce VD [ 55 – 57 ]. Additionally, the sympathetic nervous system and RAS may be implicated [ 58 ]. Supporting this, Almeida-Freitas et al. demonstrated through color Doppler imaging that patients with HF exhibit elevated vascular resistance indices and compromised blood flow in the ophthalmic artery circulation [ 42 ]. A study of adults with HFpEF showed diffuse macular VD reduction without FAZ changes, reinforcing microvascular dysfunction as a central player in HF pathophysiology independent of systolic impairment. These findings substantiate the paradigm of HFpEF as not merely a disorder of myocardial stiffness but rather a systemic vasculopathy. A study of acute decompensation revealed reduced VD in superficial layers secondary to hemodynamic instability and sudden increases in central venous pressure, which disrupts retinal venous outflow and induces vascular congestion [ 14 , 59 ]. Concurrent blood pressure spikes may provoke vessel dilation or structural remodeling [ 14 ]. Notably, Abdin et al. reported a reduction in intraocular pressure following recompensation, attributing this to abrupt variations in systemic venous pressure. These alterations disrupt ocular venous hemodynamics and hinder transscleral drainage of intraocular fluid in decompensated states [ 14 ]. The temporary widening of retinal vessel diameters during acute decompensation may serve as a compensatory mechanism to maintain blood flow amid systemic hypoperfusion; however, this often proves insufficient to prevent tissue hypoxia, as evidenced by persistent VD and perfusion deficits. Furthermore, following recompensation, certain anatomical parameters (e.g., vessel densities) improve in parallel with NT-proBNP normalization. However, functional outcomes (e.g., visual acuity) often fail to recover, suggesting persistent damage following short-term treatment [ 14 , 60 ]. Pediatric HF demonstrates selective SCP involvement with whole, foveal, and parafoveal VD decreases. The DCP remains relatively spared in both HFrEF and pediatric cases. The distinct ischemic patterns observed between the SCP and DCP may stem from their anatomical differences [ 4 ]. The SCP contains medium-to-large arterioles with smooth muscle layers, while the DCP comprises delicate capillaries lined only by endothelial cells, basement membrane, and pericytes [ 61 ]. Notably, the heightened hypoxia sensitivity of pericytes renders the DCP particularly vulnerable to hemodynamic alterations and ischemic damage [ 62 – 64 ]. Collectively, these distinct patterns observed in OCT-A findings highlight the complex, layer-specific responses of retinal vasculature to systemic hemodynamic stress and underscore the importance of segmental retinal and choroidal analysis in HF characterization. The observed reductions in parafoveal SCP VD and choroidal thickness may hold significant clinical relevance. However, our sensitivity analyses attenuated the statistical significance of these parameters, indicating that their utility remains inconclusive and requires further validation through additional studies. If confirmed, these OCT/OCTA-derived metrics could potentially serve as noninvasive surrogate biomarkers of systemic microvascular impairment in HFrEF. Such ocular parameters may enable earlier detection of deteriorating cardiovascular status prior to the manifestation of overt clinical symptoms. Notably, severity-dependent RNFL thinning emerges as a robust biomarker for patient stratification, supported by recent large cohort data linking RNFL attenuation to elevated cardiovascular risk [ 65 ]. Furthermore, the correlation between whole macular VD and both LVEF and NYHA functional class in patients with HFrEF underscores its stratification potential. Together, these OCT/OCTA metrics may provide complementary, clinically accessible markers that reflect systemic HF severity. Their integration could enhance risk stratification, facilitate disease progression monitoring, and ultimately inform personalized therapeutic approaches in HFrEF management. Limitations and Future Directions While our findings offer compelling insights, several limitations should be acknowledged. The small number of available studies limits the generalizability of results for these subgroups. For several key parameters, interpretation was restricted by single-study reporting, underscoring the critical need for more studies with larger and diverse populations, particularly among patients with HFpEF and acute HF. The cross-sectional nature of most included studies limits our ability to determine whether observed ocular microvascular changes precede, coincide with, or result from systemic cardiac dysfunction. Another limitation is that the eligible studies employed different OCT instruments from various manufacturers, each with its own test–retest reliability and segmentation algorithms. While this may limit direct comparison of parameters across studies, the validity of comparisons within studies, where measurements were taken using the same instrument, remains intact. Moreover, the inclusion of multiple instruments enhances the generalizability of this systematic review. Future studies should prioritize longitudinal assessments to determine whether retinal and choroidal parameter changes predict clinically relevant endpoints including disease progression, rehospitalization, and survival outcomes. Notably, retinal and choroidal imaging represents a promising avenue for elucidating the microvascular mechanisms of heart failure-mediated end-organ damage, particularly in highly perfused tissues such as the brain and kidneys, an area that warrants prioritized investigation in future studies.

[conclusion] Conclusion Our systematic review suggests that ocular posterior segment changes, including choroidal thinning and altered retinal vascular metrics, are observed across HF subtypes and may reflect systemic hemodynamic status. The integration of OCT/OCTA into HF management could provide clinicians with actionable insights to support precision medicine in cardiovascular care. However, it is important to note that the current body of literature remains limited, preventing definitive conclusions. Additionally, we observed high heterogeneity in the SFCT meta-analysis, and sensitivity analyses revealed that the differences in SFCT and parafoveal SCP VD were no longer statistically significant after excluding individual studies. Therefore, while ocular biomarkers show potential as accessible tools for HF detection and management, further research, particularly longitudinal studies, is needed to confirm their clinical utility.

retrieved_papers.title: Evaluation of retinal nerve fibre layer, ganglion cell layer and choroidal thickness with optical coherence tomography in migraine patients: a case-control study.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2018]
[Abstract] Evaluation of retinal nerve fibre layer (RNFL), ganglion cell layer (GCL) and choroidal thickness (CT) with optical coherence tomography (OCT) in chronic migraine patients, to compare with healthy controls.
Ninety-four eyes of 47 chronic migraine patients (Group 1) and 68 eyes of 34 healthy individuals (Group 2) were included in this prospective case-control study. The right and left eyes were separately evaluated. Mean peripapillary RNFL thicknesses, mean GCL measured from superior and inferior quadrants, and mean CT were measured at three different regions (central, 500 μm nasal and temporal region of the fovea).
There was no statistically significant differences in RNFL between the two groups (p > 0.05), while CT values were significantly higher and GCL values were significantly lower in chronic migraine groups (p < 0.05). There were no statistically significant differences between migraine duration, frequency and length of attacks, presence of aura, relation to menstrual cycle, white matter lesions in cranial magnetic resonance imaging and RNFL, GCL and CT (p > 0.05).
In this study, we observed chronic migraine disease does not have any effect on peripapillary RNFL thickness; however, increases in CT and decreases in GCL thickness were observed in migraine patients.

[methods] Methods This study is a hospital-based comparative, cross-sectional study conducted in the departments of ophthalmology and neurology of a tertiary care hospital in eastern India from June 2023 to July 2024. Institutional ethical committee clearance was obtained before starting the study (IEC/IMS.SH/SOA/2023/553 dated June 27, 2023). The study adhered to the basic tenets of the Helsinki Declaration. Written informed consent was obtained from each patient before enrolling them in the study. All patients aged between 18 and 50 years diagnosed with migraine as per the International Classification of Headache Disorders, third edition (ICHD-3) classification were included in the study.[ 6 7 ] Healthy age- and sex-matched individuals without any ocular and systemic abnormalities constituted the control group. Patients with the following were excluded from the study: best corrected visual acuity <20/20, N6, spherical or cylindrical errors >±2 D, history of systemic disorders like diabetes, hypertension, anemia, neurologic diseases like multiple sclerosis, Parkinsonism, Alzheimer’s, and ocular diseases like glaucoma, uveitis, significant cataract, retinal and optic nerve disorders. Patients with a past or present history of smoking, history of any previous ocular trauma or surgery, and intake of any systemic medications like isotretinoin, hydroxychloroquine, thioridazine, etc., which could affect the study parameters, were also excluded from the study. After obtaining informed written consent, a detailed history including the patient’s name, age, sex, occupation, address, presenting symptom, progression, and associated symptoms was recorded. A detailed history of the type of headache, duration of the headache, duration of each episode, and laterality was obtained from each patient. Detailed information about the severity of migraine was obtained using the headache impact test-6 (HIT-6) questionnaire.[ 14 ] Best corrected visual acuity was measured using Snellen’s chart. Ishihara’s chart was used to assess color vision. A thorough evaluation of the anterior segment was done in each case using the slit lamp. Intraocular pressure was measured using Goldmann’s applanation tonometer. Pupils of both eyes were dilated using a combination of 0.8% tropicamide and 5% phenylephrine. Posterior segment evaluation was done using an indirect ophthalmoscope and a 20-D lens. Measurement of pRNFL thickness, ONH, and macular parameters was done using the 3D disc, 3D macula, and 3D macula (V) modes of the spectral domain OCT machine (3D OCT-1 Topcon Maestro version 8.3; Topcon, Tokyo, Japan). RNFL and ONH details were obtained using the 3D disc mode having a scan length of 6.0 × 6.0 mm and a resolution of 512 × 128. Macular parameters were studied using the 3D macula (V) and 3D macula modes having a scan length of 7.0 × 7.0 and 6.0 × 6.0 mm, respectively. The resolution of both the above macular scans was 512 × 128. The RNFL parameters studied were average, superior, inferior, nasal, and temporal thicknesses. The ONH parameters included were disc area, cup area, rim area, vertical cup disc ratio, linear cup disc ratio, and rim volume. Central macular thickness (CMT), superior macular RNFL, inferior macular RNFL, superior GCL, and inferior GCL thicknesses were the various macular parameters studied. The thickness calculated in each cell was compared with a normative database that had already been fed into the machine. Values within the normal range were represented as green, values in the borderline as yellow, and values outside the normal limits were shown in red. In our study, migraine patients were divided into episodic and chronic as per the ICHD-3 criteria.[ 6 7 ] According to the HIT-6 score,[ 14 ] the severity of migraine is classified as mild (little-to-no) impact: HIT-6 score: 36–49; moderate impact: HIT-6 score: 50–59; and severe impact: HIT-6 score: 60–78. Statistical analysis All analyses were done using Statistical Package for the Social Sciences software, version 24.0. The analysis included profiling patients using different demographic and clinical parameters. For all statistical purposes, values of the right eye of both study and control groups were used. Descriptive analysis of quantitative parameters was expressed as means and standard deviation. Categorical data was expressed as absolute numbers and percentages. Independent Student’s t -test was used for testing the mean between independent groups, whereas paired Student’s t -test and analysis of variance were used to calculate statistical significance between two or more groups. Cross tables were generated, and the Chi-squared test was used to test associations. P value <0.05 was considered statistically significant.

[results] Results The present study enrolled 200 subjects (100 migraine patients and 100 healthy subjects). The mean age in migraine and control groups was 31.01 ± 8.56 and 30.80 ± 8.65 years, respectively. Most subjects were females (84% in migraine group and 82% in the control group). Of the 100 migraine patients, only three patients had migraine with aura. In 43%, 32%, and 25% of cases, mild, moderate, and severe migraine were seen, respectively. Episodic migraine was seen in 71% of cases, with chronic migraine making up the rest. The mean duration of migraine in study subjects was 6.56 ± 5.08 years. The mean duration of individual attacks was 9.33 ± 13.41 h, and the mean number of migraine attacks each month was 9.31 ± 6.57. Salient demographic details are presented in Table 1 . Table 1 Salient demographic characteristics Variable Migraine ( n =100) Control ( n =100) Age (years) 31.01±8.56 30.80±8.65 Sex Male (16), female (84) Male (18), female (82) Intraocular pressure (mmHg) 14.10±2.61 14.14±2.49 Aura Present (3), absent (97) - Severity of migraine Mild- 43 Moderate- 32 Severe- 25 - Type of migraine Episodic- 71 Chronic- 29 - Headache duration (years) 6.56±5.08 - Individual attack duration (h) 9.33±13.41 - Attack frequency (months) 9.31±6.57 - Analysis of RNFL thickness The average pRNFL thickness in migraine patients and controls was 91.62 ± 15.70 and 106.19 ± 10.46 µm, respectively [ Table 2 ]. Migraine patients had statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinner pRNFL (average and all quadrants) compared to age- and gender-matched healthy controls. Compared to patients with episodic migraine, chronic migraine patients exhibited statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinning in pRNFL (average and all quadrants). pRNFL (average and all quadrants) was found to be statistically thinner ( P < 0.05) in severe migraine patients compared to those with mild and moderate migraine. Compared to migraine patients with a disease duration of less than 5 years, patients with a disease duration of more than 5 years had statistically significant thinning in pRNFL (average and all quadrants). Optic disc area, cup area, vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume were more in migraine patients compared to healthy controls, and this difference was statistically significant ( P < 0.05). All the ONH parameters, that is, optic disc area, cup area, rim area, linear and vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume, were more in chronic migraine patients than in episodic migraine patients. However, this difference was statistically insignificant ( P > 0.05). Optic disc area, cup area, rim area, linear and vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume were more in severe migraine patients than in mild and moderate migraine patients. However, this difference was statistically significant ( P < 0.05) only in optic disc area and rim volume. In patients with migraine for more than 5 years, there was an increase in optic disc area, cup area, rim area, linear and vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume. However, this difference was found to be statistically insignificant ( P > 0.05). Analysis of macular parameters Mean CMT was 207.67 ± 16.92 µm min migraine patients and 228.09 ± 13.58 µm in healthy controls. This difference was found to be statistically significant ( P < 0.05). Migraine patients had statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinner macular parameters, including superior and inferior macular RNFL thicknesses, CMT, and inferior GCL thickness, compared to age- and gender-matched healthy controls. Chronic migraine patients had statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinning in all macular parameters compared to episodic migraine patients. Compared to patients with mild and moderate migraine, those with severe migraine exhibited statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinner macular parameters. Similarly, all macular parameters were found to be significantly ( P < 0.05) thinner in migraine patients with a longer duration of the disease (>5 years). [discussion] Discussion The present study was a hospital-based comparative, cross-sectional study that included 100 migraine patients and 100 healthy subjects. In our study, the mean age in the migraine group was 31.0 ± 8.56 years and in the control group was 30.80 ± 8.65 years ( P > 0.05), which was comparable to the findings reported by Simsek[ 15 ] (study group: 35.7 ± 9.5, control group: 40.9 ± 12.7 years), Gunes et al .[ 13 ] (mean age: 33.05 ± 8.83 years), and Tunç et al .[ 16 ] (migraine group: 30.1 ± 7.36 years). Female preponderance was seen in both groups. Female preponderance has been reported in most of the studies.[ 15 17 ] Females suffer from migraines at about twice the rate of men. Although both biological and psychosocial factors contribute to the sex differences in migraine, it seems that sex hormones (estrogen) are the most important. Migraine attacks are associated with fluctuating hormonal levels. High estrogen levels, high estrogen fluctuations, and hormonal replacement therapy are associated with the worst migraine outcomes.[ 18 ] Compared to the studies performed by Simsek[ 15 ] on 40 migraine patients (18- migraine with aura and 22- migraine without aura) and Kanar et al .[ 19 ] on 77 migraine patients (37- migraine with aura and 40- migraine without aura), our study had a very contrasting feature: only three patients out of 100 had migraine with aura and the rest had migraine without aura. There was no explanation for the discrepancy in the results between our study and other studies, which is a limitation of our study. Due to its simplicity and ease of use, the HIT-6 questionnaire was used to determine the severity of migraine in our study, in contrast to the Migraine disability assessment test (MIDAS) score used in the studies of Simsek[ 15 ] and Kanar et al .[ 19 ] Out of 100 migraine cases, 43% had mild, 32% had moderate, and 25% had severe migraine. Moreover, 71% of the study subjects had episodic migraine. In comparison, 29% had chronic migraine, in contrast to Reggio et al .,[ 12 ] who reported episodic and chronic migraine in 43% and 57% of their cases, respectively. Certain factors like stress, menstrual cycle changes, sleep disturbances, and alcohol trigger migraine attacks. Most likely, most of our patients had an intermittent presence of these predisposing factors, leading to a higher incidence of episodic migraine compared to chronic migraine. The mean duration of migraine in study subjects was comparable to the values reported in the studies of Simsek[ 15 ] (6.2 ± 5.6 years) and Kanar et al .[ 19 ] (8.05 ± 4.7 years). Our study’s mean duration of individual migraine attacks was 9.33 ± 13.41 h. Studies by Reggio et al .[ 12 ] (28.1 ± 22.8 h) and Sorkhabi et al .[ 20 ] (25.2 ± 31.2 h) have reported considerably higher durations of individual migraine attacks. Probable reasons for the shorter duration of the individual migraine attacks in our study can be patients presenting earlier to the neurologist, a correct diagnosis, and early institution of treatment. The higher number of patients with episodic migraine in the present study could be the reason for the lesser mean number of migraine attacks per month (9.31 ± 6.57/month). This is similar to that reported in the study of Demircan et al .[ 21 ] (8.05 ± 3.16/month), but differs from that reported by Sorkhabi et al .[ 20 ] (13.6 ± 10.2/month). Migraine is a neurovascular and progressive disorder. During a migraine attack, there is vasoconstriction of the retrobulbar artery, leading to optic nerve hypoperfusion and, eventually, ganglionic cell death. Kara et al .[ 22 ] used color Doppler imaging to demonstrate that migraine patients had greater vascular resistance in the central retinal and posterior ciliary arteries during both headache and headache-free periods. Supporting this hypothesis, our study showed statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinner pRNFL (average and all quadrants) in migraine patients compared to age- and sex-matched healthy controls. Martinez et al .[ 11 ] found thinning in the temporal quadrant, Simsek[ 15 ] and Sorkhabi et al .[ 20 ] in the nasal quadrant, Reggio et al .[ 12 ] in the nasal, temporal, and inferior quadrants, while Gunes et al .[ 13 ] and Yülek et al .[ 23 ] found no differences. This discrepancy could be attributed to the use of different OCT machines, racial variations, and the absence of standardization in the inclusion and exclusion criteria of the participants. This study examined ONH parameters since recurring constriction of the retinal and ciliary arteries can cause ischemic damage and changes to the optic disc. We found that optic disc area, cup area, vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume were higher in migraine patients compared to healthy controls. This difference was statistically significant ( P < 0.05). The results of our study contradicted the findings of Michael et al .,[ 17 ] Martinez et al .,[ 11 ] and Yener and Korucu.[ 24 ] They reported no difference in ONH parameters. This disparity might have arisen from including individuals with supranormal values in the migraine group, which would have affected intergroup comparisons. It can also be attributed to differences in the OCT machines used in different studies. Changes in central retinal artery circulation can alter the inner retinal layers, which may be reflected in macular thickness. In the macular region, ganglionic cells form stratified multicellular layers. Thus, loss of axons due to ischemia will lead to GCL thinning. Macular parameters like superior and inferior RNFL thicknesses, CMT, and inferior GCL thickness were lesser in migraine patients ( P < 0.05) compared to healthy controls. In contrast to the present study, Demircan et al .[ 21 ] and Hamurcu et al .[ 25 ] reported no difference in the mean macular thickness between the migraine and healthy groups. Kanar et al .[ 19 ] found superior and inferior GCL thickness reduction, while Tunç et al .[ 16 ] reported no difference in GCL measurements. CMT in our study group was comparable to that reported in the study by Cankaya and Tecellioglu[ 26 ] (211.07 ± 7.36 µm). Hamurcu et al .[ 25 ] reported no difference in RNFL thickness, CMT, or optic disc area, although the optic disc rim area was larger in the migraine group. In chronic migraine, due to more frequent attacks and resultant episodes of vasospasm and hypoperfusion, there are ischemic changes in the retina and optic nerve. These changes correlate with the number of headache episodes.[ 27 28 ] Our research findings showed that individuals with chronic migraine had a significant ( P < 0.05) decrease in pRNFL thickness (average and all quadrants) and macular parameters, including CMT, superior and inferior RNFL thicknesses, as well as superior and inferior GCL thicknesses, when compared to individuals with episodic migraine. Our findings differ from those of Gunes et al .,[ 13 ] who found no differences in RNFL values, but thinner GCL values. Like the present study, Reggio et al .[ 12 ] have reported thinner RNFL, especially in the superior and inferior sectors, and GCL in chronic migraine patients compared to patients with episodic migraine. In contrast to our findings, a study by Kirbas et al .[ 29 ] reported thinning in superior quadrant RNFL thickness only (no difference in other quadrants RNFL and macular parameters) in patients with chronic migraine (CM). Since migraine is a chronic condition with varying severity, the duration and intensity of the disease may have a degrading effect on RNFL and GCL. The study’s findings revealed that migraine patients with a duration of disease >5 years exhibited thinning in pRNFL and macular parameters. Specifically, all quadrants and average values of pRNFL, as well as superior and inferior GCL thicknesses, superior and inferior macular RNFL thicknesses, and CMT showed statistically significant differences between patients with duration <5 years and >5 years ( P < 0.05). However, the ONH parameters did not show significant changes. The study suggests that as the duration of migraine increases, there is a greater extent of vasoconstriction, impacting blood flow to the choroid and retina. Several studies have reported similar results. Gunes et al .[ 13 ] found an inverse correlation between headache duration and superior and temporal RNFL, Yülek et al .[ 23 ] found an inverse correlation between duration and average RNFL thickness, and Abdellatif and Fouad[ 30 ] found an inverse correlation of GCL thickness with duration. However, the findings of Gipponi et al .,[ 31 ] Kirbas et al .[ 29 ] (no correlation with RNFL), and Cankaya and Tecellioglu[ 26 ] (no correlation with CMT) contradicted our study. Our study found an inverse correlation of severity with pRNFL thickness (average and all quadrants) and macular parameters (CMT, superior and inferior GCL, and superior and inferior RNFL). Similar to our study, Kanar et al .[ 19 ] and Abdellatif and Fouad[ 30 ] found that severity (MIDAS score) was inversely correlated with superior and inferior GCL; the former found a significant inverse correlation with average, superior, and nasal RNFL thicknesses and the latter found a significant inverse correlation with inferior and temporal RNFL thicknesses only. The present study has a few limitations. The study was limited to a specific geographic area, and the results cannot be generalized to migraine patients from other areas. Since the study was conducted in an urban setting at a tertiary institute, the study population might not reflect true representatives of the general population of rural areas. OCT angiography could have helped in supporting our hypothesis of chronic ischemia in migraine being responsible for changes in retinal and ONH parameters. However, choroidal thickness and assessment of retinal vascular plexuses could not be done because of the absence of the requisite technology in our center. We also did not perform visual field analysis in our subjects, the addition of which could have further increased the value of the study. The study is cross sectional in nature; the effect of disease progression on the retinal parameters could not be assessed.

[conclusion] Conclusion The present study reports a significant variation in pRNFL, ONH, and macular parameters in migraine patients compared to age- and gender-matched healthy individuals. Patients with a history of longer duration (>5 years) of migraine had significantly thinner pRNFL. Based on our findings, we recommend careful examination of retinal and ONH parameters in migraine patients at regular intervals to avoid any future complications. Conflicts of interest There are no conflicts of interest.

retrieved_papers.title: Iron-associated central macular ganglion cell complex thinning and choroidal vascularity index elevation in transfusion-dependent β-thalassemia: potential OCT/OCTA biomarkers.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] This study aimed to evaluate alterations in macular microstructure and blood flow density in patients with transfusion-dependent β-thalassemia (TDT) using optical coherence tomography (OCT) and optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA), and to explore their associations with markers of iron metabolism.
A cross-sectional study was conducted involving 31 TDT patients (61 eyes) and 31 age- and sex-matched healthy controls (61 eyes) at the Affiliated Hospital of Guangdong Medical University from October 2022 to December 2023. Data collected included basic information, medical history, best-corrected visual acuity, ocular biometry, fundus photography, and OCT/OCTA imaging. Parameters assessed comprised the thickness and blood flow density of individual macular layers, as well as choroidal stromal area, choroidal luminal area, and choroidal vascular index (CVI). Pearson correlation analysis was employed to examine relationships among macular parameters, serum ferritin (SF) levels, duration of iron chelation therapy (ICT), and other variables.
Compared to healthy controls, TDT patients exhibited significant thinning of the central macular area ganglion cell layer, retinal nerve fiber layer, and ganglion cell complex (mGCC), accompanied by increased blood flow density in the deep vascular complex, nerve fiber layer vascular plexus, and deep capillary plexus (all P < 0.05). The choroidal stromal area was reduced (P = 0.037), whereas CVI was elevated (P = 0.034). Pearson correlation analysis revealed a positive correlation between central mGCC thickness and the duration of ICT (r = 0.280, P < 0.05),and a significant positive correlation between CVI with SF levels (r = 0.426, P < 0.01). Stepwise linear regression analysis identified the duration of ICT (β = 0.306, P = 0.011) as a significant independent factor influencing central mGCC thickness. Similarly, SF levels significantly influenced CVI (β = 0.469, P < 0.001). Additionally, 62.30% of TDT patients' eyes presented with tilted optic discs, 34.43% showed tessellated fundus, and 6.56% had pseudoxanthoma elasticum.
TDT patients exhibit both macular neuroretinal thinning and increased microvascular blood flow density. CVI and central mGCC thickness may serve as potential biomarkers for monitoring iron-related ocular pathology and therapeutic efficacy. Future longitudinal studies are warranted to validate their predictive value and establish causality. Regular ocular screening is warranted in this population.

[Introduction] Background Transfusion-dependent β-thalassemia (TDT), a hereditary hemolytic disorder caused by β-globin gene mutations, necessitates lifelong blood transfusions for survival. However, chronic iron overload secondary to transfusions leads to multi-organ damage, including hepatic, cardiac, and endocrine dysfunction [ 1 – 3 ]. It is recognized globally as a significant public health challenge [ 4 ]. Studies have demonstrated that approximately 46.87% of TDT patients exhibit ocular complications, including retinal thinning, pseudoxanthoma elasticum (PXE) changes, and microstructural abnormalities in the macular region, all of which significantly impair patients’ quality of life [ 5 – 7 ]. In recent years, the cumulative effect of regular blood transfusions and iron overload on the eyes has gained increasing attention, but the specific mechanism of its influence on macular microstructure and hemodynamics remains unclear. The macula, as the vision-sensitive core of the retina, relies on the structural integrity of layers including the ganglion cell complex (GCC) and photoreceptor layers. Chronic anemia and iron overload in TDT patients induce oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction, triggering retinal ganglion cell (RGC) apoptosis and vascular endothelial damage [ 7 – 10 ]. Evidence suggests significant macular thinning and microstructural disruptions in TDT patients [ 6 , 11 ], yet comprehensive analyses integrating full-thickness retinal parameters with hemodynamic metrics are lacking. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) and angiography (OCTA) provide non-invasive, high-resolution quantification of retinal layers and microvascular density, offering novel insights into disease progression. Previous OCT/OCTA studies have mostly focused on a single structural parameter [ 12 – 14 ]. However, the changes in the structure of each layer in the macular area and blood flow density, as well as their correlations with iron metabolism indicators, have not been systematically clarified. Furthermore, the southern part of China is a high-incidence area of thalassemia. The carrying rates of thalassemia in Guangxi and Guangdong are as high as 24.5% and 17.7% [ 15 – 17 ], respectively. This region-specific genetic burden provides a unique population for investigating iron overload-induced ocular complications. However, up to now, the epidemiological data of characteristic fundus changes in the TDT patient population are still limited. This study aims to systematically characterize macular structural and blood flow density changes in a Southern Chinese TDT cohort using OCT/OCTA, while elucidating their correlations with iron overload indicators and therapeutic histories. By identifying biomarkers of retinal iron toxicity, this research seeks to optimize screening protocols and inform targeted interventions for TDT-associated retinopathy.

However, chronic iron overload secondary to transfusions leads to multi-organ damage, including hepatic, cardiac, and endocrine dysfunction [ 1 – 3 ]. [methods] Methods Study population This cross-sectional study was conducted from October 2022 to December 2023. A total of 31 patients diagnosed with TDT and managed in the Department of Hematology of Guangdong Medical University Affiliated Hospital were enrolled (TDT group), along with 31 age- and sex-matched healthy children (control group). Inclusion criteria for the TDT group were as follows: According to the “Chinese guideline for diagnosis and treatment of transfusion dependent β-thalassemia (2022)” [ 2 ], patients aged 4–18 years diagnosed with TDT by two deputy directors/chief physicians of the Department of Hematology at the Affiliated Hospital of Guangdong Medical University, who had received regular and standardized erythrocyte replacement therapy for ≥ 1 year and had taken iron chelating agents regularly for ≥ 3 months, and had stable vital signs. The control group comprised healthy children matched by age and gender during the same period. The exclusion criteria were as follows: (1) Patients with other types of anemia or other significant systemic diseases; (2) History of ocular surgery (including laser treatment) for conditions such as glaucoma, corneal disease, vitreous disease, or retinal disease; (3) History of ocular trauma, congenital ocular diseases, or contact lens wear; (4) Axial length (AL) > 26 mm, spherical equivalent refraction (SER) ≤ − 6.00 diopters (D), SER ≥ + 3.00 D, or astigmatism ≥ + 2.00 D; (5) Inability to cooperate sufficiently to complete ophthalmic examinations or poor image quality due to poor cooperation. Before the study began, the researchers provided a comprehensive explanation to all the guardians of the patients and clarified the purpose of the study and possible discomforts. Written informed consent was obtained from all guardians, and assent was obtained from participants where appropriate, ensuring their voluntary participation. This study adheres to strict privacy protection measures to safeguard the personal information of all participants and their guardians. This study was approved by the Clinical Research Ethics Committee of the Affiliated Hospital of Guangdong Medical University (approval number: KT2023-016-01). All procedures involving human participants were performed in accordance with the ethical standards of this committee and with the 1964 Helsinki Declaration and its later amendments. Measurements Basic information for all subjects was collected, including name, age, gender, height, past medical history and weight data. And calculate the body mass index (BMI). For the TDT group, the following data were collected: blood transfusion duration, pre-transfusion hemoglobin (Hb) level, SF level within the past month, and the types and duration of iron chelation therapy (ICT). All subjects underwent standardized ophthalmic examinations such as best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA), refraction examination, slit lamp examination, fundus examination, and non-contact intraocular pressure (IOP) measurement. The BCVA was examined using the national standard logarithmic visual acuity chart (GB11533-1989). Laser scanning fundus photography was performed using the Optos Daytona P200T (Optos plc, Dunfermline, UK). AL was measured using the Lenstar LS900 ocular biometer (HAAG-STREIT, Koeniz, Switzerland). The refraction was examined after ciliary muscle paralysis using a computerized automatic refractometer (CRK-8800, CHAROPS, South Korea), and the spherical equivalent refraction (SER) was calculated. Each eye was measured three times consecutively, and the average value was recorded. All the examiners were optometrists or ophthalmologists who have received training from the Affiliated Hospital of Guangdong Medical University. Spectral-domain optical coherence tomography (SD-OCT) and OCT angiography (OCTA) imaging were performed for all participants using the SPECTRALIS ® OCT device (Heidelberg Engineering GmbH, Heidelberg, Germany). OCT scanning was used to obtain the images of the retinal structures of each layer in the macular area with the fovea centralis as the center and the scanning range being 9 mm×9 mm. The macula was divided according to the Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS) grid sectors: a central circular zone (1 mm diameter), an inner subfield (3 mm diameter minus central zone), and an outer subfield (6 mm diameter minus inner and central zones) (Supplementary Figure Sa ). The inner and outer subfields were further subdivided into superior, inferior, nasal, and temporal quadrants, resulting in nine sectors in total. The thickness of each quadrant of each layer of the macular retina was measured using the built-in image analysis of the software. Enhanced depth imaging (EDI) mode was used to acquire a B-scan centered on the fovea for choroidal analysis, with a measurement window width of 1500 μm positioned beneath the fovea (Supplementary Fig. Sb ) [ 18 ]. OCTA scans were acquired over a 3 × 3 mm area centered on the fovea for the blood vessels of each layer of the retina and choroid. Vascular parameters assessed included vessel density in the following plexuses: the macular retina, superficial vascular complex (SVC), deep vascular complex (DVC), nerve fiber layer vascular plexus (NFLVP), superficial vascular plexus (SVP), intermediate capillary plexus (ICP), deep capillary plexus (DCP), avascular complex, choriocapillaris, choroid, and full of the above layers. The original OCTA images were exported and processed using ImageJ software (version 1.53; National Institutes of Health, Bethesda, MD, USA). Images were converted to 8-bit grayscale format and binarized using the Niblack auto-local thresholding method [ 19 ] to facilitate vessel segmentation. Vessel density (VD) was then calculated within the specified regions as the percentage area occupied by vessels. Choroidal vascular parameters were analyzed by ImageJ software (version 1.53). The choroid beneath the fovea within a 1500 μm width was segmented. The Niblack auto-local thresholding method [ 19 ] was applied to binarize the image, differentiating the choroidal vascular lumen (hyporeflective) from the stroma (hyperreflective) (Supplementary Fig. Sa ). The luminal area, stromal area, and total choroidal area of the 1500 μm wide region of the choroid below the fovea macula of the choroid were obtained. And calculate the choroidal vascular index (CVI). All OCT and OCTA measurements were performed by a single, trained technician. To assess intra-observer reliability, a random subset of 20 scans was re-measured by the same technician one week later. The intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) for key parameters (e.g., central mGCC thickness, CVI) exceeded 0.90, indicating excellent reproducibility. Definitions SER Calculation: SER = Sphere power (D) + 0.5 × Cylinder power (D). BMI Calculation: BMI (kg/m²) = Weight (kg) ÷ Height² (m²). mGCC Definition: The sum of the thickness of macular retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL), ganglion cell layer (GCL), and inner plexiform layer (IPL) thicknesses. Choroidal Parameters: Total choroidal area (TCA) = Choroidal luminal area (CLA) + Choroidal stromal area (CSA). CVI = (CLA / TCA) × 100%. Statistical analysis The OCT/OCTA parameters were processed and calculated through the Image J (1.53) software. The data were analyzed by the Statistical Package for Social Sciences (Version 27 for Windows; SPSS Inc, Chicago, IL, USA). Categorical variables were tested using the chi-square test. Continuous variables were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD) or median (inter quartile range, IQR) based on data distribution, and normal distribution analysis was performed using the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test. We employed Student’s t-test and Non-parametric test for normally and abnormally distributed data, respectively. The correlation was evaluated by Pearson correlation analysis. The closer the absolute value of r was to 1, the stronger the correlation. When |r| ≥ 0.8, it was a very strong correlation. When 0.6 ≤ |r| < 0.8, it is a strong correlation. When 0.4 ≤ |r| < 0.6, it is moderately correlated. When 0.2 ≤ |r| < 0.4, it is weakly correlated. When 0 ≤ |r| < 0.2, it is a very weak correlation. P < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. A post-hoc power analysis was performed using PASS 15.0 software (NCSS, LLC) to assess the statistical power of the significant findings reported in this study, based on the observed effect sizes and the present sample size. A power value greater than 0.80 was considered adequate.

[results] Results Demographic and clinical characteristics Initially, 31 TDT patients (62 eyes) and 31 healthy controls (62 eyes) were enrolled. After applying exclusion criteria (one eye from the TDT group and one from the control group were excluded, as noted in the Methods), a final dataset of 61 eyes per group (122 eyes total) was included for analysis. The TDT group comprised 31 patients (61 eyes), with 60.66% of eyes (37/61) belonging to male subjects. The mean age of TDT group is 10.31 ± 3.67 years. The control group included 31 healthy subjects (61 eyes), with males’ eye representing 50.82% (31 eyes). The mean age of control group is 11.00 ± 3.18 years. No statistically significant differences were observed between the two groups in age ( P = 0.271) or gender ( P = 0.274). The mean SER was − 0.39 ± 2.19 D in the TDT group and − 0.72 ± 1.29 D in the control group. There was no significant differences between the two groups ( P = 0.323). However, the TDT group exhibited significantly shorter axial lengths compared to the control group (23.32 ± 0.97 mm vs. 24.06 ± 0.99 mm, P < 0.001) (Table 1 ). Among TDT patients, the mean values of BMI, pre-transfusion Hb level, transfusion duration, SF level (within the past month), and ICT duration were 15.39 ± 1.46 kg/m², 80.45 ± 15.96 g/L, 8.78 ± 3.79 years, 4070.00 (3024.00, 6270.00) µg/L, and 3.17 ± 2.54 years, respectively. Among the sixty-one eyes in the TDT group, the corresponding patients were receiving deferasirox (DFX) monotherapy for thirty-one eyes (50.82%) and combined DFX and deferoxamine (DFO) therapy for thirty eyes (49.18%) (Supplementary Table S1 ). Macular structural analysis The retinal structures of each layer in the macular area were scanned by OCT and their thicknesses were analyzed. The results are detailed in Table 1 . The average thickness of inner nasal, inner superior, inner temporal, outer inferior and central GCL in the macular area of the TDT group was significantly thinner than that of the corresponding structural layer thickness in the control group ( P values were < 0.001, 0.019, < 0.001, 0.030, 0.033, respectively). The average thickness of inner nasal, inner superior, inner temporal and central GCC in the macular area of the TDT group was significantly thinner than the corresponding layer in the control group ( P values were < 0.001, 0.039, < 0.001, 0.007, respectively). The average thickness of the macular central RNFL of the TDT group was significantly thinner than the control group ( P = 0.022). There was no statistically significant difference in the thickness of other retinal structural layers in the macular area in the TDT group (all P values were > 0.05) (Table 1 ). calculated by T-test Bold values represent significance ( p < 0.05) Macular blood flow density The blood flow density of each layer of the retina in the macular area was assessed by OCTA. The results in Table 2 show that compared with the control group, the blood flow densities of DVC, DCP and NFLVP in the TDT group were significantly increased ( P values were 0.044, < 0.001, 0.008 in sequence), while there were no significant differences in the blood flow densities of the other structural layers (all P values > 0.05). The results in Table 3 show that compared with the control group, the TDT group exhibited a significant reduction in CSA ( P = 0.037) and elevated CVI ( P = 0.034), while choroidal CLA remained unchanged ( P = 0.191) (Table 3 ). The CVI exhibited a significant positive correlation with SF levels ( r = 0.426, P < 0.01). Stepwise linear regression analysis identified SER ( β = 0.358, P = 0.003) and the duration of ICT ( β = 0.306, P = 0.011) as significant independent factors influencing central GCC thickness, with SER demonstrating a stronger effect (Table 4 ). Similarly, transfusion duration ( β = − 0.257, P = 0.030) and SF levels ( β = 0.469, P < 0.001) significantly influenced CVI, with SF showing a more pronounced association (Table 5 ). However, since no significant correlation was observed between CVI and transfusion duration ( r = − 0.177, P > 0.05) (Supplementary Table S2 ), the clinical significance of transfusion duration on CVI requires further validation.
