[methods] 7.2 Application of materials science in NAION Although the application of materials science in the treatment of non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (NAION) remains in the exploratory phase, early advances show promising potential. Notably, curcumin-polydopamine nanocomposite hydrogels (Cur@PDA@GelCA) have demonstrated significant neuroprotective effects in optic nerve injury models. These materials act by inhibiting reactive oxygen species (ROS)-mediated oxidative stress, indicating potential utility for acute-phase intervention in NAION. In addition to their therapeutic efficacy, the hydrogel’s strong tissue adhesion and excellent biocompatibility highlight its suitability for localized drug delivery and targeted treatment ( Liu et al., 2024 ). These findings underscore the broader feasibility of integrating materials science into NAION therapy. Future research should aim to further elucidate the molecular mechanisms underlying NAION and leverage multi-omics technologies alongside biomaterials innovation to develop personalized treatment strategies that can improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_papers.title: Comprehensive analysis of posterior segment characteristics in high myopic Turkish patients.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] We aimed to investigate the structural characteristics of high myopia (HM) in a Turkish cohort using spectral domain optical coherence tomography (OCT) and evaluate the relationship between axial length (AL) and various retinal, choroidal, and scleral parameters.
A retrospective analysis of medical records and imaging data was conducted in HM patients examined at Dokuz Eylül University between January 2019 and January 2023. Eyes were categorized into three groups based on AL: Group 1 (26.00-27.49 mm), Group 2 (27.50-28.99 mm), and Group 3 (≥ 29.00 mm). Retinal, choroidal, and scleral parameters were quantitatively assessed, and posterior segment findings were analyzed across these groups.
A total of 146 eyes from 91 patients (mean age: 46.55 ± 17.05 years) were included. Central foveal thickness was significantly lower in Group 1 compared to Group 2 (p = 0.017). Moreover, Group 1 exhibited lower values for average macular thickness, average macular volume, and scleral sink, along with higher subfoveal choroidal thickness values compared to both Group 2 and Group 3 (p < 0.05 for all comparisons). The most common posterior segment findings were peripapillary atrophy (91.8%), tilted disc (47.3%), vitreomacular adhesion (39.0%), and posterior staphyloma (PS, 27.4%). Group 3 exhibited the most advanced stages of myopic maculopathy among the groups (p = 0.001). 'Plus' lesions, defined as the presence of macular neovascularization (MNV), Fuchs spots, or lacquer cracks, were detected in 14.4% of eyes. Pathologic myopia was diagnosed in 49.3% of eyes, including 47.2% based on myopic maculopathy stage and 0.7% each on the presence of PS, active MNV, or scarred MNV. Active MNV was more frequent in Group 3, while scarred MNV was predominant in Group 2.
Present findings highlight the progressive nature of structural changes associated with increasing AL in HM patients, emphasizing the importance of early detection and monitoring of posterior segment complications. This study provides valuable insights into the prevalence and distribution of myopia-related complications in a Turkish population, aiding in the clinical management of HM.

[Introduction] Background The rising prevalence of high myopia (HM) has emerged as a major public health concern over the past few decades [ 1 ]. Even though HM is commonly defined by a spherical equivalent (SE) refractive error of ≥ -6.00 diopters (D) and/or an axial length (AL) of ≥ 26.00 mm [ 2 ], it is still challenging to determine its prevalence due to variations in its definition. The global prevalence of HM is reported to be approximately 4% [ 1 ]. In Turkey, a study by Onal et al. [ 3 ] on medical students aged 18–26 reported a HM prevalence of 1.4% among myopic individuals and 0.4% in the overall population. Additionally, Kaya [ 4 ] reported a HM prevalence of 1.7% in Turkish myopic children. Pathologic myopia (PM), a more severe form of the condition, is characterized by structural alterations in the posterior segment, such as posterior staphyloma (PS), myopic maculopathy, and HM-associated optic neuropathy, which predominantly affect the posterior pole [ 5 ]. Although these pathological findings are frequently observed in eyes with HM, they can also be present in individuals with less severe refractive errors or normal AL values [ 6 ]. Axial elongation in HM is primarily driven by biomechanical stretching of the ocular tissues, resulting from progressive ocular growth. At the cellular level, axial elongation is associated with extracellular matrix remodeling within the sclera, characterized by decreased collagen synthesis and increased matrix metalloproteinase activity, which collectively reduce scleral rigidity. These alterations facilitate posterior scleral expansion, especially at the posterior pole, leading to thinning of the retina, choroid, and sclera [ 7 ]. The resulting structural changes contribute to a range of sight-threatening complications in HM, with the macular region being particularly susceptible. These complications can induce irreversible photoreceptor damage, ultimately leading to central vision loss [ 8 ]. The utilization of optical coherence tomography (OCT) has revolutionized the visualization and classification of HM-related posterior segment changes, offering near-histological resolution superior to conventional fundoscopy [ 2 , 9 ]. As both environmental and genetic factors are thought to contribute to the onset and progression of HM, region-specific investigations are crucial for identifying population-based variations. Despite the rising global prevalence of HM, there remains a notable lack of large-scale, imaging-based studies characterizing the posterior segment features of Turkish adults with HM. To address this gap, the present study aims to provide comprehensive multimodal imaging data from a Turkish cohort, thereby contributing to a deeper understanding of the structural alterations and their clinical implications in this population.

[introduction] Background The rising prevalence of high myopia (HM) has emerged as a major public health concern over the past few decades [ 1 ]. Even though HM is commonly defined by a spherical equivalent (SE) refractive error of ≥ -6.00 diopters (D) and/or an axial length (AL) of ≥ 26.00 mm [ 2 ], it is still challenging to determine its prevalence due to variations in its definition. The global prevalence of HM is reported to be approximately 4% [ 1 ]. In Turkey, a study by Onal et al. [ 3 ] on medical students aged 18–26 reported a HM prevalence of 1.4% among myopic individuals and 0.4% in the overall population. Additionally, Kaya [ 4 ] reported a HM prevalence of 1.7% in Turkish myopic children. Pathologic myopia (PM), a more severe form of the condition, is characterized by structural alterations in the posterior segment, such as posterior staphyloma (PS), myopic maculopathy, and HM-associated optic neuropathy, which predominantly affect the posterior pole [ 5 ]. Although these pathological findings are frequently observed in eyes with HM, they can also be present in individuals with less severe refractive errors or normal AL values [ 6 ]. Axial elongation in HM is primarily driven by biomechanical stretching of the ocular tissues, resulting from progressive ocular growth. At the cellular level, axial elongation is associated with extracellular matrix remodeling within the sclera, characterized by decreased collagen synthesis and increased matrix metalloproteinase activity, which collectively reduce scleral rigidity. These alterations facilitate posterior scleral expansion, especially at the posterior pole, leading to thinning of the retina, choroid, and sclera [ 7 ]. The resulting structural changes contribute to a range of sight-threatening complications in HM, with the macular region being particularly susceptible. These complications can induce irreversible photoreceptor damage, ultimately leading to central vision loss [ 8 ]. The utilization of optical coherence tomography (OCT) has revolutionized the visualization and classification of HM-related posterior segment changes, offering near-histological resolution superior to conventional fundoscopy [ 2 , 9 ]. As both environmental and genetic factors are thought to contribute to the onset and progression of HM, region-specific investigations are crucial for identifying population-based variations. Despite the rising global prevalence of HM, there remains a notable lack of large-scale, imaging-based studies characterizing the posterior segment features of Turkish adults with HM. To address this gap, the present study aims to provide comprehensive multimodal imaging data from a Turkish cohort, thereby contributing to a deeper understanding of the structural alterations and their clinical implications in this population.

[methods] Methods This retrospective cross-sectional study was conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki and local ethical regulations, with approval from the Dokuz Eylül University Non-Interventional Research Ethics Committee (Approval No: 2023/06–23). The medical records, ophthalmologic examination findings, and multimodal imaging data, including spectral-domain OCT (SD-OCT) and color fundus images (CFI), were reviewed for patients with HM who underwent examination at the Dokuz Eylül University Department of Ophthalmology between January 2019 and January 2023. Inclusion and exclusion criteria Patients were included in the study if they met the following criteria: AL ≥ 26.00 mm, age ≥ 18 years, and SD-OCT image quality ≥ 18. Exclusion criteria included age < 18 years, previous ocular surgery other than cataract surgery (e.g., refractive surgery, glaucoma surgery, or vitreoretinal surgery), SD-OCT image quality < 18, significant lens opacity (cataract grade ≥ 3 according to the Emery-Little classification) [ 10 ], ophthalmic diseases apart from HM, and a history of trauma or systemic medication use potentially affecting retinal or macular morphology. Demographic and clinical data Demographic and clinical data were extracted from medical records, including SE refractive error for phakic eyes, which was calculated as spherical refraction plus half of the cylindrical error. The Nidek TonoRef II (NIDEK Co., Ltd, Japan) was used for measurements. Best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) was assessed using the Snellen chart and converted to logMAR for statistical analysis. The IOL Master 500 (Carl Zeiss Meditec, Jena, Germany) was used to measure AL, and lens status (phakic or pseudophakic) was determined via slit-lamp biomicroscopy. Optical coherence tomography imaging protocol Spectral-domain OCT imaging was performed using the Heidelberg Spectralis (Heidelberg Engineering, Heidelberg, Germany). A 7 × 7 mm macular scan centered on the fovea was acquired, consisting of 49 horizontal OCT sections spaced 160 μm apart. A similar 7 × 7 mm optic disc scan was obtained with 49 horizontal OCT sections at 160 μm intervals. In addition, a single 9-mm transfoveal horizontal enhanced-depth imaging (EDI) OCT scan was performed. Analysis of optical coherence tomography sections In the 7-mm single horizontal OCT section through the fovea (Fig. 1 A), several parameters were assessed. The central foveal thickness (CFT) was measured as the shortest distance between the internal limiting membrane (ILM) and Bruch’s membrane (BM) (Fig. 1 B) [ 11 ]. Scleral sink (SS) was measured as the vertical distance between the lowest and highest points of the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) (Fig. 1 C) [ 12 ]. Mirror artifact (MA) was identified as image inversion caused by increased retinal curvature (Fig. 1 D) [ 12 ]. Dome-shaped maculopathy (DSM) was characterized by a protrusion of ≥ 50 μm from the lowest RPE level toward the vitreous (Fig. 1 E) [ 12 ]. Lamellar macular hole (LMH) was defined as partial separation of the inner retinal layers at the fovea (Fig. 1 F) [ 13 ], while full-thickness macular hole (FTMH) represented complete separation of retinal layers from ILM to RPE (Fig. 1 G) [ 14 ]. Myopic macular retinoschisis (MMR) referred to a separation of the foveal and/or parafoveal neuroretinal layers by a hyporeflective space (Fig. 1 H) [ 14 ]. Vitreomacular adhesion (VMA) was identified as partial vitreoretinal separation without distortion [ 15 ], and vitreomacular traction (VMT) was defined as partial vitreoretinal separation with associated retinal distortion [ 15 ]. Fig. 1 Evaluation of a single transfoveal horizontal 7-mm optical coherence tomography (OCT) section. A ; an example of a transfoveal 7-mm horizontal OCT section. B ; the measurement of central foveal thickness as the shortest distance between the internal limiting membrane (ILM) and Bruch’s membrane (a). C ; the measurement of scleral sink as the vertical distance between the lowest and highest points of the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE, b). D ; white arrow indicating the mirror artifact. E ; a protrusion of ≥ 50 μm from the lowest RPE level toward the vitreous (c), indicating dome-shaped macula. F ; partial separation of the inner retinal layers at the fovea indicating a lamellar macular hole (white arrow). G ; complete separation of retinal layers from ILM to RPE, indicating a full-thickness macular hole. H ; separation of the foveal neuroretinal layers by a hyporeflective space, indicating myopic macular retinoschisis Horizontal OCT sections obtained in a 7 × 7 mm area centered on the fovea were further analyzed for additional parameters (Fig. 2 A). The average thickness and volume values within the central 1-mm retinal area, as defined in the Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study, were recorded as average macular thickness (AMT) and average macular volume (AMV), respectively, using the retinal thickness map feature of the device (Fig. 2 B) [ 11 ]. Epiretinal membrane (ERM) was defined by the presence of an irregular hyperreflective band on the ILM [ 13 ]. Macular neovascularization (MNV) was characterized by a hyperreflective membrane on the RPE in the outer retinal layers [ 14 ]. Hemorrhage or intraretinal/subretinal fluid-associated MNVs were considered ‘active’ (Fig. 2 C), while MNVs without these findings, and Fuchs spots, were classified as ‘scarred’ (Fig. 2 D). Fig. 2 Evaluation of horizontal optical coherence tomography (OCT) sections obtained from a 7 × 7 mm area centered on the fovea and/or optic disc. A ; nine subfields according to the Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS). B ; average macular thickness (272 μm) and average macular volume (0.21 mm 3 ) within the central 1-mm retinal area based on ETDRS. C ; hyperreflective material above the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) in association with intraretinal and subretinal fluid, indicating an active macular neovascularization (MNV). D ; hyperreflective material above the retinal pigment epithelium without hemorrhage or intraretinal/subretinal fluid, indicating a scarred MNV. E ; horizontal OCT sections from a 7 × 7 mm area centered on the optic disc. F ; a hyporeflective space within the inner choroidal layers beneath an intact RPE, indicating a peripapillary intrachoroidal cavitation. G ; the separation of inner retinal layers, characterized by a distinct hyporeflective space, indicating peripapillary retinoschisis. H ; cystic spaces or defects within the inner retinal layers in the perivascular areas, indicating perivascular inner retinal defect Horizontal OCT sections from a 7 × 7 mm area centered on the optic disc were analyzed for specific retinal abnormalities (Fig. 2 E). Peripapillary intrachoroidal cavitation (PICC) was diagnosed upon identifying a hyporeflective space within the inner choroidal layers beneath an intact RPE (Fig. 2 F) [ 16 ]. Peripapillary retinoschisis (PR) was defined by the separation of retinal layers, characterized by a distinct hyporeflective gap (Fig. 2 G) [ 14 ]. Horizontal OCT sections obtained in a 7 × 7 mm area centered on the fovea and/or optic disc, passing through areas adjacent to retinal vascular structures were evaluated for cystic spaces or defects within the inner retinal layers. These findings were classified as perivascular inner retinal defects (PIRDs) (Fig. 2 H) [ 17 ]. Analysis of enhanced-depth imaging optical coherence tomography sections In the 9-mm transfoveal horizontal EDI-OCT section, several key parameters were assessed (Fig. 3 A). Subfoveal choroidal thickness (SFCT) was defined as the shortest distance between the hyperreflective outer boundary of the RPE and the choroid-sclera junction (Fig. 3 B) [ 12 ]. Posterior staphyloma was identified by the presence of an outward scleral protrusion, with a curvature radius smaller than that of the adjacent sclera (Fig. 3 C) [ 18 ]. Subfoveal scleral visibility (SFSV) was measured as the distance between the choroid-sclera junction and the outermost discernible level of scleral reflectivity (Fig. 3 D) [ 12 ]. Fig. 3 Evaluation of a single transfoveal horizontal 9-mm enhanced depth imaging optical coherence tomography (EDI-OCT) section. A ; an example of a transfoveal 9-mm horizontal EDI-OCT section. B ; the measurement of subfoveal choroidal thickness as the shortest distance between the hyperreflective outer boundary of the retinal pigment epithelium and the choroid-sclera junction (a). C ; an outward scleral protrusion, with a curvature radius smaller than that of the adjacent sclera, indicating posterior staphyloma. D ; the measurement of subfoveal scleral visibility as the distance between the choroid-sclera junction and the outermost discernible level of scleral reflectivity (b) Color fundus imaging protocol and analysis Color fundus images were acquired using the Zeiss VISUCAM 500 (Carl Zeiss Meditec, Jena, Germany), covering a central 45-degree field that included the optic disc and macula. The Meta-Analysis for Pathologic Myopia (META-PM) classification was applied to categorize myopic maculopathy into five categories: category 0 (normal fundus), category 1 (tigroid fundus), category 2 (diffuse chorioretinal atrophy), category 3 (patchy chorioretinal atrophy), and category 4 (macular atrophy) [ 19 ]. The presence of additional posterior segment findings, including peripapillary atrophy (PPA), lacquer cracks (LC), tilted disc, and Fuchs spots were also evaluated. Group classification Eyes with active or scarred MNV / Fuchs spot or LC were classified as having ‘plus’ lesions. Eyes included in the study were classified as having PM if they exhibited myopic maculopathy equal or greater than category 2 or higher, and/or PS, and/or at least one ‘plus’ lesion. As all eyes included in the study had an axial length AL ≥ 26.00 mm, consistent with the definition of HM, they were further classified into three subgroups to assess the impact of varying degrees of axial elongation on structural findings. Specifically, Group 1 comprised eyes with AL ranging from 26.00 to 27.49 mm, Group 2 included eyes with AL between 27.50 and 28.99 mm, and Group 3 consisted of eyes with AL ≥ 29.00 mm. This stratification allowed for a more detailed evaluation of progressive anatomical alterations within the HM cohort. Statistical analysis The data collected in this study were analyzed using SPSS 28.0 (Statistical Package for the Social Sciences). Descriptive statistical methods, including frequency, percentage, mean, standard deviation, minimum, median, and maximum values, were employed to summarize the data. The normality of the data distribution was assessed using the Kolmogorov-Smirnov and Shapiro-Wilk tests. Parametric tests were applied to measurements that followed a normal distribution, while non-parametric tests were utilized for data that did not conform to normality. For quantitative data, analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed to compare the means of at least three independent groups with normally distributed data, while the Kruskal-Wallis test was used to compare the medians of non-normally distributed data across independent groups. For the post-hoc analyses, independent samples t-tests and Mann-Whitney U tests with Bonferroni correction were used for pairwise comparisons. The relationship between categorical variables was assessed using the Chi-square test, and Fisher’s Exact test was employed when the expected frequencies for any category were less than five. A p -value below 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

[results] Results A total of 146 eyes from 91 patients were included in the study. The cohort comprised of 37 males (40.7%) and 54 females (59.3%), with a mean age of 46.55 ± 17.05 years (range: 18–84 years). Both eyes of 55 patients (60.4%) met the inclusion criteria. Of the 146 eyes included in the study, 123 (84.2%) were phakic, and 23 (15.8%) were pseudophakic. Group 1 comprised 79 eyes from 54 patients, while Group 2 included 32 eyes from 25 patients, and Group 3 included 35 eyes from 23 patients. No significant differences were observed between the groups in terms of sex distribution ( p = 0.864) or mean age ( p = 0.067). However, a significantly higher percentage of phakic eyes was observed in Group 1 compared to the other groups ( p = 0.001). Statistically significant differences were found between the three groups in terms of the mean SE value ( p = 0.001), AL ( p < 0.001), and BCVA ( p < 0.001), as expected (Table 1 ). Table 1 Clinical characteristics of the study population Entire Cohort Group 1 Group 2 Group 3 p value Number of patients / eyes , n 91 / 146 54 / 79 25 / 32 23 / 35 − Age , years (mean ± SD) 46.55 ± 17.05 42.43 ± 17.29 48.04 ± 16.20 52.39 ± 15.51 0.067 a Gender , male / female 37 / 54 22 / 32 9 / 16 10 / 13 0.864 b Lens status , phakic / pseudophakic 123 / 23 75 / 4 23 / 9 25 / 10 0.001 b SE , diopters (mean ± SD) -10.95 ± 4.28 -9.13 ± 3.46 -12.18 ± 3.37 15.28 ± 3.71 0.001 c AL , mm (mean ± SD) 28.07 ± 2.26 26.56 ± 0.46 28.17 ± 0.38 31.39 ± 2.13 < 0.001 a BCVA , logMAR (mean ± SD) 0.30 ± 0.34 0.17 ± 0.20 0.32 ± 0.34 0.58 ± 0.40 < 0.001 a AL, axial length; BCVA, best-corrected visual acuity; SD, standard deviation, SE, spherical equivalent a Kruskal-Wallis test b Chi-square test c Analysis of variance (ANOVA) Significant differences were also observed among the three groups in terms of the mean CFT, AMT, AMV, SS, and SFCT ( p = 0.015, p = 0.007, p = 0.006, p < 0.001, and p < 0.001, respectively). Notably, AMT and AMV could not be calculated by the device for one eye in Group 2 and six eyes in Group 3. Post-hoc analyses revealed that the significant difference in CFT was primarily driven by a higher value in Group 2 compared to Group 1 ( p = 0.017). The differences in AMT, AMV, and SS were attributed to Group 1 exhibiting lower values compared to Group 2 and Group 3. Specifically, for AMT, Group 1 showed significantly lower values than both Group 2 and Group 3 ( p = 0.026 and p = 0.049, respectively). Similar trends were observed for AMV, with Group 1 having lower values than Group 2 and Group 3 ( p = 0.019 and p = 0.049, respectively). For SS, Group 1 also had significantly lower values compared to both Group 2 and Group 3 ( p < 0.001 for both). A significant difference in mean SCFT was observed across all pairwise group comparisons, with Group 1 displaying the highest thickness and Group 3 the lowest ( p = 0.016 for Group 1 vs. Group 2, p < 0.001 for Group 1 vs. Group 3, and p < 0.001 for Group 2 vs. Group 3). No significant difference was observed among the three groups in terms of the mean SFSV ( p = 0.354) (Table 2 ). Table 2 Spectral-domain optical coherence tomography findings of the study population Parameter Entire Cohort ( n = 146) Group 1 ( n = 79) Group 2 ( n = 32) Group 3 ( n = 35) p value CFT , µm 240.10 ± 52.15 233.90 ± 35.00 259.34 ± 63.69 236.49 ± 68.10 0.015 φ,a AMT* , µm 281.96 ± 47.62 273.30 ± 40.27 297.13 ± 59.80 289.31 ± 47.98 0.007 φ,a, b AMV* , mm 3 0.22 ± 0.04 0.21 ± 0.03 0.23 ± 0.05 0.23 ± 0.04 0.006 φ,a, b SS , µm 744.45 ± 477.53 556.29 ± 423.55 934.63 ± 472.46 995.29 ± 419.95 < 0.001 φ,a, b SFCT , µm 163.15 ± 95.80 209.18 ± 77.21 157.31 ± 95.78 64.60 ± 46.36 < 0.001 φ,a, b SFSV , µm 352.30 ± 108.16 347.77 ± 105.87 378.19 ± 125.79 338.86 ± 94.24 0.354 λ AMT, average macular thickness; AMV, average macular volume; CFT, central foveal thickness; SFCT, subfoveal choroidal thickness; SS, scleral sink; SFSV, subfoveal scleral visibility * AMT and AMV were not calculated by the device for one eye in Group 2, and six eyes in Group 3 φ Kruskal-Wallis test λ Analysis of variance (ANOVA) a Post-hoc significant difference between Group 1 and Group 2 b Post-hoc significant difference between Group 1 and Group 3 c Post-hoc significant difference between Group 2 and Group 3 Based on the META-PM classification, 11 eyes (7.5%) were classified as category 0, 66 eyes (45.5%) as category 1, 43 eyes (29.5%) as category 2, 14 eyes (9.6%) as category 3, and 12 eyes (8.2%) as category 4 myopic maculopathy. Group 2 exhibited more advanced META-PM stages compared to Group 1, Group 3 demonstrated the most advanced stages, surpassing both Groups 1 and 2 ( p = 0.001) (Table 3 ). Table 3 The distribution of META-PM categories in the study population META-PM Category Entire Cohort ( n = 146) Group 1 ( n = 79) Group 2 ( n = 32) Group 3 ( n = 35) p value* Category 0 , n (%) 11 (7.5) 9 (11.4) 2 (6.3) 0 (0) 0.001 Category 1 , n (%) 66 (45.2) 46 (56.8) 16 (50) 4 (11.4) Category 2 , n (%) 43 (29.5) 24 (30.4) 10 (31.3) 9 (25.7) Category 3 , n (%) 14 (9.6) 0 (0) 1 (3.1) 13 (37.1) Category 4 , n (%) 12 (8.2) 0 (0) 3 (9.4) 9 (25.7) META-PM: Meta-Analysis for Pathologic Myopia *Fisher’s exact test Most commonly observed posterior segment findings were PPA (134 eyes, 91.8%), tilted disc (69 eyes, 47.3%), VMA (57 eyes, 39.0%), and PS (40 eyes, 27.4%) respectively throughout the whole cohort. Significant differences were found among the groups regarding the presence of PPA, tilted disc, VMA, PS, MA, ERM, PR, PIRD, DSM, LC, scarred MNV, active MNV, and MMR ( p < 0.05 for all). All these findings were more frequently observed in Group 3, except for VMA, which was more common in Group 1, and scarred MNV, which was more prevalent in Group 2. No significant differences were observed among the groups in terms of the presence of PICC, LMH, FTMH, and VMT ( p > 0.05 for all). Among the eyes with LMH, one was included in Group 1, while two were in Group 3. Additionally, one eye with FTMH and another with VMT were classified in Group 3. The frequency of all posterior segment findings investigated in the present study, along with the comparison among the groups, is provided in detail in Table 4 . Table 4 The frequency of posterior segment findings in the study population Posterior segment finding, n (%) Entire Cohort ( n = 146) Group 1 ( n = 79) Group 2 ( n = 32) Group 3 ( n = 35) p value Peripapillary atrophy 134 (91.8) 68 (86.1) 31 (96.9) 35 (100.0) 0.022 b Tilted disc 69 (47.3) 25 (31.6) 16 (50) 28 (80) < 0.001 a Vitreomacular adhesion 57 (39) 40 (50.6) 10 (31.3) 7 (20) 0.005 a Posterior staphyloma 40 (27.4) 4 (5.1) 9 (28.1) 27 (77.1) < 0.001 a Mirror artifact 26 (17.8) 9 (11.4) 4 (12.5) 13 (37.1) 0.003 a Epiretinal membrane 24 (16.4) 4 (5.1) 3 (9.4) 17 (48.6) < 0.001 a Peripapillary retinoschisis 23 (15.8) 6 (7.6) 6 (18.8) 11 (31.4) 0.005 a Paravascular inner retinal defect 22 (15.1) 5 (6.3) 6 (18.8) 11 (31.4) 0.002 a Peripapillary intrachoroidal cavitation 17 (11.6) 9 (11.4) 3 (9.4) 5 (14.3) 0.834 b Dome-shaped maculopathy 14 (9.6) 2 (2.5) 3 (9.4) 9 (25.7) 0.001 b Lacquer cracks 8 (5.5) 0 (0) 2 (6.3) 6 (17.1) 0.001 b Scarred macular neovascularization 8 (5.5) 1 (1.3) 4 (12.5) 3 (8.6) 0.002 b Active macular neovascularization 7 (4.8) 1 (1.3) 1 (3.1) 5 (14.3) 0.002 b Myopic macular retinoschisis 7 (4.8) 0 (0) 3 (9.4) 4 (11.4) 0.004 b Lamellar macular hole 3 (2.1) 1 (1.3) 0 (0) 2 (5.7) 0.264 b Vitreomacular traction 1 (0.7) 0 (0) 0 (0) 1 (2.9) 0.834 b Full-thickness macular hole 1 (0.7) 0 (0) 0 (0) 1 (2.9) 0.459 b a Chi-square test b Fisher’s exact test A total of 21 eyes (14.4%) displayed ‘plus’ lesions, including seven (4.8%) with active MNV, six (4.1%) with LC, six (4.1%) with scarred MNV, and two (1.3%) with both LC and scarred MNV. In total, 72 eyes (49.3%) were diagnosed with PM, including 69 eyes (47.2%) with category 2 or higher myopic maculopathy, and one eye (0.7%) each with PS, active MNV, and scarred MNV, despite having category 1 myopic maculopathy.

[discussion] Discussion Currently, there is no consensus on the definition or cutoff values for refractive error or AL in diagnosing HM, complicating the generalization of findings. The prevalence of myopia and its complications varies by region, likely influenced by environmental and genetic factors. Consequently, determining the global prevalence of HM, associated posterior segment findings, and their relationship with AL remains challenging. Axial elongation-induced mechanical tension is redistributed toward the peripheral retina to preserve foveal function in high myopic eyes. This mechanism may cause peripheral macular thinning while increasing central retinal thickness [ 20 , 21 ]. Lam et al. [ 20 ] reported a decrease in retinal thickness in the relatively peripheral regions of the macula, while central retinal thickness increases in Chinese patients with HM. Similar results were reported in Saudi [ 22 ] and Indian [ 23 ] populations. In contrast, Deng et al. [ 24 ] observed a decrease in CFT in eyes with more advanced stages of maculopathy; while Wu et al. [ 25 ] did not report a significant difference in retinal thickness between eyes with or without HM in Chinese children. Çıtırık et al. [ 26 ] and Uçak et al. [ 27 ] reported slightly higher CFT in eyes with HM compared to controls in Turkish populations, though these differences were not statistically significant. Our study showed significantly higher CFT values in eyes with longer ALs. Additionally, significantly lower AMT and AMV values were observed in Group 1. Variability in findings may be due to differences in inclusion and exclusion criteria for defining HM or PM. The choroid plays a key role in the development and progression of HM [ 28 ]. Lower SFCT in eyes with HM, along with a negative correlation between SFCT and AL have been consistently reported in various populations, including those from China [ 25 , 28 , 29 ], Malaysia [ 30 ], Japan [ 31 ], Brazil [ 32 ], and Singapore [ 33 ]. In the present study, we similarly observed a progressive decrease in SFCT from Group 1 to Group 3, aligning with findings from the previous studies in Turkish eyes with HM [ 34 – 36 ], as well as reports from other regions [ 25 , 28 – 33 ]. Scleral measurements such as SS and SFSV have not been commonly assessed in eyes with HM. Shin et al. [ 12 ] defined SS in the Korean population as the vertical distance (Y-axis) between the lowest and highest points of the RPE. They proposed that a SS greater than 500 μm, as well as a minimum SFSV of 100 μm, could serve as diagnostic criteria for HM. Ohno-Matsui et al. [ 37 ] reported that the posterior scleral interface was visible in 31% of Japanese high myopic eyes using EDI-OCT and in 53% of eyes using swept-source OCT. In the present study, the mean SS and SFSV were measured as 744.45 ± 477.53 μm and 352.3 ± 108.16 μm, respectively, in Turkish individuals with HM. While the mean SS in Group 1 was significantly lower than in the other groups, no significant differences were observed regarding the mean SFSV among the groups. Since EDI-OCT was used to assess SFSV, actual values may have been underestimated. Regarding the myopic maculopathy; Wong et al. [ 38 ] reported that the tigroid fundus is the most common fundus finding in Chinese children with HM, typically occurring in cases with shorter ALs. Diffuse chorioretinal atrophy was observed in 12.2% of Chinese children and adolescents with HM by Jiang et al. [ 39 ], while Liu et al. [ 40 ] reported a prevalence of 20.6%. Patchy chorioretinal atrophy was reported in 10.5% of eyes with HM in Japan population by Xie et al. [ 41 ], whereas Ohno-Matsui et al. [ 42 ] reported a slightly higher prevalence of 12.3%. The most common myopic maculopathy category in Turkish population observed in the present study was tigroid fundus (45.5%), followed by diffuse chorioretinal atrophy (29.5%). Nearly half of the eyes were diagnosed to have PM. Group 3 demonstrated the most advanced stages of myopic maculopathy, surpassing both Group 1 and Group 2. Although PPA and tilted disc are not classified within the spectrum of myopic maculopathy or PM, they are commonly observed in eyes with HM. In Chinese population, the prevalence of PPA and tilted disc in pediatric cases was reported as 90.6% and 78.4%, respectively [ 39 ]. In contrast, lower prevalences of 81.2% and 57.4% were reported in the Singaporean population [ 43 ]. İlhan et al. [ 44 ] reported the prevalences of PPA and tilted disc in Turkish high myopic adults as 92.9% and 46.8%, respectively. In our cohort, we observed similar prevalences of 91.8% and 47.3%, respectively. Both findings were significantly more frequent in Group 3 compared to other groups, with 100% and 80% for PPA and tilted disc, respectively. The variability in reported prevalences across studies is likely attributable to differences in the inclusion or exclusion of eyes with PM. Posterior staphyloma can be assessed using various imaging modalities, including CFI, magnetic resonance imaging, B-mode ultrasonography, and OCT [ 18 , 45 ]. In the Spanish population, Flores-Moreno et al. [ 46 ] found a PS prevalence of 69.4% in highly myopic eyes using indirect ophthalmoscopy, CFI and 12-mm OCT scans. They emphasized that eyes with PS tend to exhibit more advanced stages of myopic maculopathy, and that the risk of developing PS increases by 70% with each mm increase in AL. Lower prevalences were reported in Singapore, ranging from 20.9 to 23.0%, possibly due to the use of a lower cutoff value for the inclusion of eyes [ 43 , 47 ]. The prevalence in Turkish population was previously reported as 11.4% using CFIs [ 44 ]. In our study, PS was observed in 27.4% of eyes, with significantly higher rates observed in eyes with longer ALs. However, due to the utilization of a single 9-mm horizontal EDI-OCT section, PS types not involving the macula may have been missed, potentially underestimating the true prevalence. Mirror artifacts are observed in OCT systems using Fourier-domain scanning, arising from the inability to distinguish between positive and negative time delays [ 48 ]. The prevalence of MAs in HM has been rarely studied. Shin et al. [ 12 ] reported MAs in 41% of Korean eyes with HM, while none were found in control eyes. In our study, MA was present in 17.8% of Turkish high myopic eyes, with a significantly higher prevalence in Group 3. The relatively lower prevalence observed may be attributed to the use of a narrower OCT scan protocol. Additionally, variations in acquisition settings (e.g., scan depth, centration, use of horizontal or vertical scans), software algorithms for image reconstruction, and signal processing may also contribute to discrepancies in reported MA prevalence across different studies. These technical factors should be considered when interpreting MA frequency in OCT-based research. Myopic traction maculopathy was observed in 43.5% of high myopic eyes in China, with 37.9% exhibiting paravascular retinoschisis, 27.5% having PR, and 23.0% showing MMR [ 49 ]. A study conducted in Singapore reported that 28.1% of eyes exhibited myopic traction maculopathy, with 24.7% and 4.2% of eyes exhibiting ERM and VMA, respectively [ 50 ]. The most frequent vitreomacular interface abnormalities in Turkish eyes in the present study were ERM (16.4%), PR (15.8%), and PIRD (15.1%); with increasing prevalence in eyes with longer ALs. Conversely, the most common OCT finding was VMA (39.0%), which was significantly more frequent in Group 1. The higher prevalence of VMA in Group 1 may be attributed to earlier stages of vitreous changes typically observed in less advanced HM. In eyes with relatively shorter ALs, the posterior vitreous may remain partially attached to the macula, resulting in a higher rate of detectable VMA on OCT. As AL increases and myopic degeneration progresses, the likelihood of complete posterior vitreous detachment increases, thereby reducing the frequency of VMA in more advanced groups. Lacquer cracks have been reported in up to 15.7% of high myopic eyes in Chinese population [ 51 ]. Two observational studies conducted in Singapore reported a lower prevalence of 1.0% and 1.8% for LC in high myopic eyes [ 43 , 52 ]. In Turkish population, we identified LCs in 5.5% of eyes, 75% of which were in Group 3. However, the prevalence we observed may be underestimated, as the assessment was based on CFIs rather than dye-based angiographic modalities, which are considered as the gold standard for detecting these lesions [ 51 ]. The varying prevalences among the studies may be attributed to differences in the assessment methods used, as well as the inclusion and exclusion criteria applied. Sudden central vision loss in eyes with HM is primarily caused by MNV [ 53 ]. Epidemiological data indicate a prevalence of 5% in the United States [ 54 ] and 10% in Japan [ 55 ]; while a higher prevalence was observed in an Egyptian cohort with rates of 18.0% for active MNV and 7.2% for inactive MNV [ 56 ]. However, this prevalence is likely an overestimation, as the study exclusively included visually symptomatic individuals. In the present study, active MNV was observed in 4.8% of Turkish eyes with HM, while scarred MNV was found in 5.5%. Active MNV was significantly more prevalent in Group 3. The prevalence of DSM in high myopic eyes has been reported to range from 9.3 to 20% in Japan [ 57 – 60 ], 13.8% in China [ 61 ], and 22.8% in Spain [ 62 ], whereas a lower prevalence of 4.4% was found in Mexican eyes with HM [ 63 ]. Hocaoglu et al. [ 64 ] reported the presence of DSM in 16.0% of high myopic eyes in Turkish adults. We observed the DSM in 9.6% of the eyes, with none exhibiting subretinal fluid. It was observed more frequently in eyes with longer ALs. The varying prevalences among the studies may stem from the lack of a precise definition of DSM, as well as differences in the assessment methods used. With the advent of OCT, PICC has been more precisely characterized as hyporeflective spaces at the choroidal level [ 65 ]. It can resemble malignant conditions such as amelanotic choroidal nevi or melanoma, complicating the differential diagnosis in eyes with HM. The prevalence of PICC in high myopic eyes has been reported as 4.9% in Japan [ 16 ], and 16.9% in China [ 66 ]. In our study, PICC was observed in 11.6% of all Turkish eyes with HM, which aligns with the literature. No statistically significant differences in the prevalence were found among the groups. The structural differences identified across the groups in the present study may carry important clinical implications. Eyes with longer ALs exhibited more pronounced degenerative changes, including advanced stages of myopic maculopathy, higher SS, thinner SCFT, and a higher prevalence of sight-threatening complications such as MNV and PS. These findings underscore the need for stratified follow-up protocols based on AL, as patients with longer ALs may benefit from more frequent monitoring using multimodal imaging to facilitate early detection of progressive macular pathology. Incorporating AL measurement into routine clinical assessment could help stratify patients by risk and guide the timing and frequency of imaging follow-up. Furthermore, recognizing early structural changes in patients with elongated ALs may support earlier therapeutic interventions, potentially mitigating irreversible vision loss. Early identification of these risk indicators may also enable preemptive management strategies and patient education to preserve visual function and improve long-term outcomes. The main limitations of this study include its retrospective, single-center design and monoracial setting, which may contribute to differences between our findings and those in the literature. The inclusion of a wide range of ALs and degenerative findings, such as MNV, could have influenced the results, though this approach aimed to maximize the generalizability of the results to high myopic eyes. Another limitation is the lack of magnification correction for ALs during retinal, choroidal, and scleral measurements, as the device did not allow AL input. Moreover, treatment options and outcomes for certain complications, such as MNV and vitreomacular interface disorders, could not be evaluated due to the retrospective cross-sectional design of the study. Lastly, although the lens status of all eyes was recorded, the study groups were not stratified based on this factor. Given that pseudophakia may influence retinal image quality or affect certain measured parameters, this could have introduced a potential confounding effect on the results. Despite these limitations, this study is, to the best of our knowledge, the most comprehensive analysis of posterior segment manifestations of HM, utilizing SD-OCT to quantitatively assess specific findings like SS, SFSV, PIRD, and PICC in a relatively large Turkish cohort.

[conclusion] Conclusions Posterior segment alterations in high myopic eyes are primarily driven by axial elongation and these degenerative changes generally correlate with the increased AL. In this study, Turkish patients with longer ALs exhibited more advanced structural abnormalities, including higher rates of PM, PS, and MNV. While our findings align with previously reported trends in East Asian and European populations, the prevalence and distribution of specific features such as SS, SFCT, and vitreomacular interface abnormalities appear to vary, likely reflecting underlying genetic, anatomical, or environmental differences. By providing detailed imaging-based data from a Turkish cohort, this study offers region-specific insights that may inform individualized risk stratification and guide clinical decision-making in the management of HM.

retrieved_papers.title: SS-OCT/OCTA Assessment of Choroidal Structure in Vitreoretinal Lymphoma Patients and Their Changes During Intravitreal Methotrexate Chemotherapy.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] To evaluate choroidal structure changes in vitreoretinal lymphoma (VRL) patients during intravitreal methotrexate (MTX) therapy and quantitative characteristics of active VRL at choroidal level using swept-source optical coherence tomography angiography (SS-OCT/OCTA).
In this retrospective study, patients diagnosed with VRL and received intravitreal MTX chemotherapy from January 2022 to December 2023 were included, along with healthy volunteers of the same age range and sex ratio. Three-phase intravitreal MTX chemotherapy consisting of induction, consolidation, and maintenance was scheduled. SS-OCT/OCTA images obtained before intraocular chemotherapy and by the end of each phase of treatment were reviewed. Choroidal thickness (CT), choriocapillaris (CC) perfusion area, choroidal vessel volume (CVV), choroidal vascularity index (CVI), and choroidal stromal volume (CSV) were analyzed.
Forty-four eyes of 29 VRL patients (15 males, 14 females) were enrolled in this study. CT, CVV, and CSV decreased significantly by the end of induction phase and maintained a continuous decrease during intravitreal MTX chemotherapy (all P < 0.05), whereas CC perfusion area and CVI remained statistically comparable. Comparative analysis demonstrated significantly lower CVI in VRL eyes versus normal controls across all measured regions (all P < 0.05), whereas CT, CVV, and CSV showed no intergroup differences.
VRL eyes demonstrated a decreased CVI, which reflected lymphoma-induced structural changes at the choroidal level. After intravitreal MTX treatment, CT, CVV, and CSV all showed a significant reduction.
SS-OCT/OCTA-derived choroidal parameters (CT, CC perfusion area, CVI, CVV, and CSV) enable noninvasive monitoring of choroidal changes in VRL patients.

[Introduction] Introduction Vitreoretinal lymphoma (VRL) is a rare ocular malignancy, with an annual incidence of approximately 0.046 cases per 100,000 individuals. 1 It most often affects patients over the age of 50, with a mean age of 63. 1 It is currently classified as one of the lymphomas of immune-privileged sites, with diffuse large B-cell lymphoma as the predominant histopathological type. 2 Clinically, 65%–90% of primary VRL (PVRL) patients will develop central nervous system (CNS) involvement during the course of the disease, whereas 15% to 25% of patients with primary central nervous system lymphoma will eventually develop ocular involvement. 1 Intravitreal methotrexate (MTX) chemotherapy is one of the main treatment approaches for PVRL, and combined systemic chemotherapy is suggested for patients with bilateral or CNS involvement. 3 VRL is histologically characterized by lymphoma cells infiltration into vitreous, retina, and optic nerve. 4 , 5 Recently optical coherence tomography (OCT) findings have been reported to be helpful in revealing the retinal abnormalities in VRL eyes as well as monitoring treatment response. 6 – 8 However, limited research exists regarding whether VRL induces changes in the choroid, or their clinical implications, despite the choroid being the primary source of blood supply to the outer retinal layer. As swept-source optical coherence tomography angiography (SS-OCTA) became available, it is now possible to accurately detect and quantitative blood flow and structural changes at the choroidal level in VRL. The study aims to monitor choroidal structure changes in VRL patients during intravitreal MTX therapy and also evaluate characteristics of active VRL at choroidal level using SS-OCT/OCTA.

[introduction] Introduction Vitreoretinal lymphoma (VRL) is a rare ocular malignancy, with an annual incidence of approximately 0.046 cases per 100,000 individuals. 1 It most often affects patients over the age of 50, with a mean age of 63. 1 It is currently classified as one of the lymphomas of immune-privileged sites, with diffuse large B-cell lymphoma as the predominant histopathological type. 2 Clinically, 65%–90% of primary VRL (PVRL) patients will develop central nervous system (CNS) involvement during the course of the disease, whereas 15% to 25% of patients with primary central nervous system lymphoma will eventually develop ocular involvement. 1 Intravitreal methotrexate (MTX) chemotherapy is one of the main treatment approaches for PVRL, and combined systemic chemotherapy is suggested for patients with bilateral or CNS involvement. 3 VRL is histologically characterized by lymphoma cells infiltration into vitreous, retina, and optic nerve. 4 , 5 Recently optical coherence tomography (OCT) findings have been reported to be helpful in revealing the retinal abnormalities in VRL eyes as well as monitoring treatment response. 6 – 8 However, limited research exists regarding whether VRL induces changes in the choroid, or their clinical implications, despite the choroid being the primary source of blood supply to the outer retinal layer. As swept-source optical coherence tomography angiography (SS-OCTA) became available, it is now possible to accurately detect and quantitative blood flow and structural changes at the choroidal level in VRL. The study aims to monitor choroidal structure changes in VRL patients during intravitreal MTX therapy and also evaluate characteristics of active VRL at choroidal level using SS-OCT/OCTA.

[methods] Methods This is a single-center, observational retrospective study. The study was approved by the institutional review board and ethic committee of the Eye and ENT Hospital of Fudan University (approval ID 2020119). Informed consent for surgical procedures was obtained from all participants, and all examinations were performed in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki. Subjects Patients diagnosed with VRL who underwent intravitreal MTX chemotherapy at our institution (Fudan University Eye and ENT Hospital, Shanghai, China) from January 2022 to December 2023 were reviewed and included if (1) the diagnosis of VRL was confirmed by vitreous cytopathology or combined clinical-imaging-molecular criteria 9 (including CNSL history, typical ocular manifestation, intraocular interleukin level, and myeloid differentiation factor 88 [MYD88 L265P ] mutation), (2) no less than one phase of intravitreal MTX chemotherapy was completed, (3) ocular imaging was acquired during no less than two treatment phases, and (4) complete medical records could be obtained. Exclusion criteria were: (1) patients with chorioretinopathy secondary to systemic diseases such as diabetes or hypertension, (2) eyes with retinal or choroidal neovascularization, (3) eyes with axial length ≥26 mm, (4) eyes with intraocular pressure >21 mm Hg, or (5) patients unable to cooperate with examinations because of poor fixation, media opacity, or eyes with poor-quality imaging results. Healthy volunteers matched to the VRL group in age range and sex ratio were included in the normal control group, which could serve as the normal references for further analysis of choroidal changes. The volunteers underwent an ophthalmic workup including SS-OCT/OCTA. Inclusion criteria were (1) age ranged from 30 to 70 years, (2) best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) no worse than 20/25, (3) axial length shorter than 26 mm, and (4) all clinical data were available. Eyes were excluded if the participant (1) could not cooperate with SS-OCT/OCTA examination or (2) had any history of ocular diseases or surgeries. Therapeutic Regimen Unilaterally involved VRL patients without CNS involvement received intravitreal MTX chemotherapy, whereas those with bilateral or CNS involvement were first evaluated by oncologists and then underwent systemic treatment followed by intravitreal MTX chemotherapy. Intravitreal MTX injections (400 µg/0.1 mL) were performed under a treatment regimen 6 consisting of induction, consolidation, and maintenance phases: the induction phase involved biweekly treatments for two weeks (or weekly for four weeks), the consolidation phase included weekly treatments for four weeks (or two weeks if clinical remission was reached earlier), and the maintenance phase consisted of monthly treatments for three to 10 months. In case of relapse, treatment was restarted from the induction phase. Clinical Data Basic clinical data of enrolled patients were collected, including sex, age of onset, symptoms, systemic condition, treatment regimen, follow-up duration, and other medical history. All the patients underwent ocular examinations, which included slit-lamp biomicroscopy, intraocular pressure measurement, axial length assessment, BCVA, as well as multi-model imaging including ultrasound examination, fundus photography, and SS-OCT/OCTA (VG200; SVision Imaging, Ltd., Luoyang, China). Results from the following time points were collected: before intravitreal MTX chemotherapy, by the end of each phase, and at the latest follow-up visit after completion of intraocular treatment. Treatment response was ranked as complete remission (CR), partial remission (PR), or progressive disease (PD) on the basis of the clinical manifestation. CR was defined as complete disappearance of anterior chamber keratic precipitates (KP), absence of vitreous cells, and resolution of retinal lesions or no active lesions. PR was defined as a reduction in anterior chamber KP, vitreous opacity, and retinal lesions compared to baseline, but not reaching the level of CR. PD was defined as an increase or recurrence of ocular lesions compared to the previous follow-up. SS-OCT/OCTA Image Acquisition and Analysis Images were obtained using a commercial SS-OCT/OCTA device (VG200; SVision Imaging, Ltd.), which used a swept-source light with a central wavelength of 1050 nm to capture high-resolution fundus images. The scanning range covers a 12 × 12 mm area of the fundus centered on the macular fovea using a protocol of “Angio” model and “Average” for blood flow signal. The system achieved an axial resolution of 3.8 µm and a lateral resolution of 10 µm at a scanning rate of 200,000 A-scans per second. Each B-scan consists of 1024 A-scans and is stored in “volume” data, from which C-scan images are extracted in both x and y axes, generating three-dimensional reconstruction of the choroid. The device incorporates enhanced tissue-resolving depth and artificial-intelligence-driven choroidal layer segmentation and quantification of choroidal vessels. Images with clear segmentation, no obvious defects or artifacts, and a signal strength≥7 (7/10) were included for further analysis. Choroidal parameters including choroidal thickness (CT), choriocapillaris (CC) perfusion area, choroidal vessel volume (CVV), and three-dimensional –choroidal vascularity index (3D-CVI) were obtained using the built-in algorithm of VG200 device. CT is defined as the straight-line distance from the outer boundary of the RPE layer to the choroid-scleral junction. CC perfusion area refers to the area of blood flow signals within the CC layer. CVV represents the volume of blood flow signals within the large vessel layer of the choroid (Sattler's and Haller's layers). The 3D-CVI (hereafter referred to as CVI) is the ratio of choroidal vascular volume to total choroidal volume, reflecting the density of large vessels in the choroid within the measured region. Choroidal stroma volume (CSV) refers to the difference between total choroidal volume and choroidal vascular volume and is calculated using the following equation: C S V = CVV - CVV · CVI / CVI A circular area with a 9 mm diameter, centered on the macular fovea, was selected within the 12 × 12 mm fundus scanning area for calibration and statistical analysis. The Early Treatment of Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS) grid was used to divide the area into four concentric rings with diameters of 0-1, 1-3, 3-6, and 6-9 mm, corresponding to the fovea, parafovea, perifoveal, and extrafoveal regions ( Fig. 1 ). Figure 1. Schematic diagram of SS-OCT/OCTA examination. ( A ) An example of a SS-OCT/OCTA-based quantitative assessment of the choroid, with the OCTA imaging of choroidal blood flow of a VRL patient shown on the left and the corresponding OCTA B-Scan image on the right. ( B ) Schematic diagram of the ETDRS grid used for calculation. The region is divided into four concentric rings centered on the fovea, referring to foveal (diameter 0-1 mm), parafoveal (diameter 1–3 mm), perifoveal (diameter 3–6 mm), and extrafoveal (diameter 6–9 mm). Statistical Analysis Statistical analysis of the study data was performed using IBM SPSS Statistics (version 29.0.1; SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA) and R software (version 4.2.2; R Core Team, 2022). The Shapiro-Wilk test was used to assess the normality of continuous variables. Continuous variables normally distributed were presented as mean ± standard deviation, whereas non-normally distributed variables are expressed as median (interquartile range). BCVA was converted to the logarithm of the minimum angle of resolution (LogMAR). Intergroup comparisons of categorical variables were conducted using the χ 2 test or Student's t -test. For statistical analysis at the eye level, generalized estimating equations (GEE) were applied. When we analyzed longitudinal repeated measurements (e.g., visual acuity and choroidal parameters) across different treatment stages within each eye, an autoregressive working correlation matrix structure was used, and P values were adjusted using Benjamini-Hochberg correction. For the horizontal comparisons of baseline and post-treatment choroidal parameters between VRL eyes and normal control eyes, an exchangeable correlation matrix structure was applied to adjust correlation between the two eyes of each individual, and P values were adjusted using Bonferroni correction. The effects of axial length and age on choroidal parameter measurements were adjusted by including them as covariates in GEE. Statistical results were subsequently imported into GraphPad Prism (version 10.1.1; GraphPad Software, Boston, MA, USA) for graphical representation.

[results] Results Demographic Data of VRL Patients and the Normal Control Group Records of 82 eyes from 43 VRL patients were reviewed and 44 eyes of 29 VRL patients (15 males, 14 females) were enrolled in this study, with a median follow-up of 10.0 (5.5, 15.5) months. Table 1 summarizes the patients’ demographic and clinical characteristics. The mean age of presentation was 59.55 ± 9.7 years. Table 1. A total of 29 individuals (38 eyes) were included in the control group, comprising 15 males (19 eyes) and 14 females (19 eyes). No significant differences were observed in age, sex, axial length or baseline intraocular pressure between VRL patients and the control group ( P = 0.366, 1.000, 0.166, 0.854, respectively) ( Table 2 ). Clinical Characteristics of VRL Patients Among the VRL patients, two (7%) were diagnosed with primary central nervous system lymphoma before intraocular involvement, one (3%) had both CNS and ocular involvement at diagnosis, and 26 (90%) had PVRL, of whom seven later developed CNS involvement. Bilateral involvement was observed in 26 patients (90%), whereas three (10%) had unilateral disease. All 29 patients underwent diagnostic vitrectomy, of whom 21 (72%) showed positive cytopathological findings as diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. MYD88 L265P mutations were detected in vitreous fluid cell-free DNA analysis in 28 patients (97%), including eight with negative cytopathology. Cytokine profiling of intraocular fluid revealed an interleukin (IL)-10/IL-6 ratio >1 in 39 eyes of 25 patients. Regarding treatment, 10 patients (35%) received intravitreal MTX chemotherapy, while the remaining 19 patients (65%) underwent combined whole-brain radiation or systemic MTX-based systemic chemotherapy followed by intravitreal MTX. At the final follow-up, PR was observed in six eyes (five patients), with a mean of 5.67 ± 2.88 MTX injections per eye; CR was achieved in 38 eyes (24 patients), with a mean of 9.32 ± 3.29 MTX injections per eye. The mean number of injections at the end of induction, consolidation, and maintenance phase were 3.69 ± 0.70, 6.32 ± 1.70, and 11.15 ± 3.93, respectively. Medians of BCVA (LogMAR) at baseline and each of the timepoint were 0.301 (0.10, 0.70), 0.398 (0.19, 0.52), 0.222 (0.10,0.76), 0.189 (0.04,0.52), and 0.097 (0.00,0.24), respectively. VRL patients showed significant BCVA improvement at the maintenance phase and the latest follow-up post-treatment compared to the baseline ( P = 0.011, P = 0.003, respectively; See Supplementary Table S1 ). P value was calculated using GEE and adjusted using Benjamini-Hochberg correction. * Statistically significant after correction (α = 0.05). CT A gradual decline in CT was observed across all regions following the initiation of intravitreal chemotherapy. By the end of induction phase, CT in all regions had significantly decreased compared to the pre-treatment values ( P = 0.002, 0.002, 0.001, <0.001, respectively). CT progressively declined with ongoing treatment, and at each phase of intraocular chemotherapy, CT remained lower than pretreatment levels ( P < 0.05 for all). CC Perfusion Area Although the CC perfusion area showed a decreasing trend after treatment, only the perifoveal region demonstrated a statistically significant reduction at the end of the maintenance phase versus pre-treatment ( P = 0.028). No other regions exhibited significant changes across treatment stages ( P > 0.05 for all). CVV CVV at completion of the induction phase was notably lower than pretreatment levels ( P = 0.005, 0.028, 0.010, 0.002, respectively). CVV continued to decline across all regions throughout treatment, with these reductions persisting until the completion of intraocular chemotherapy ( P < 0.05 for all). CSV CSV followed a similar trend as CT and CVV. In all ETDRS sectors, CSV at completion of the induction phase was significantly lower than pretreatment levels ( P = 0.014, 0.002, < 0.001, < 0.001, respectively). CSV continued to decline, with values at each treatment stage remaining lower than pretreatment levels ( P < 0.05 for all). CVI Despite an increasing trend showed in CVI, no marked changes were observed in all regions before and after intravitreal chemotherapy (P > 0.05 for all).
